stem: A 26-year-old nulliparous woman presents to the ED with 2 days of right iliac fossa pain and scant dark vaginal spotting at LMP+7 weeks. Previous PID 3 years ago, treated. No contraception. On arrival: HR 132, BP 88/52, RR 22, SpO2 98%, peripherally cool with delayed capillary refill 4 seconds, pale, conversing but anxious. Abdominal guarding, rebound tenderness across the right iliac fossa, distended abdomen with shifting dullness. Cervical excitation, right adnexal tenderness with palpable fullness. Urine beta-hCG positive. Bedside FAST shows free fluid in the pelvis and Morrison's pouch. IV access x2 has been obtained; group and hold and crossmatch sent.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 110 bpm, BP 100/60 mmHg, RR 18/min, Temp 37.1 C
data_table.Examination: Lower right abdominal guarding. Cervical excitation positive, right adnexal tenderness and mass.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Transfer immediately to operating theatre for diagnostic laparoscopy with potential laparotomy and activate the massive transfusion protocol
options.rationale: Haemodynamic instability (shock), positive FAST and clinical features of ruptured ectopic mandate immediate operative management without delay for further imaging. Theatre transfer with MTP activation is the correct multi-step decision (RANZCOG ectopic).

options.text: Perform an urgent transvaginal ultrasound to confirm location of pregnancy before further action
options.rationale: Urgent TVUS first is wrong because while imaging is correct for haemodynamically stable women, a shocked patient with positive FAST requires immediate operative management; TVUS would delay laparoscopy and cost the patient blood and time. Would be correct only in stable women with positive beta-hCG and no peritonism (RANZCOG ectopic).

options.text: Administer single-dose intramuscular methotrexate 50 mg/m2 and admit for monitoring
options.rationale: IM methotrexate is wrong because methotrexate requires haemodynamic stability, an unruptured ectopic and low/declining beta-hCG; it is absolutely contraindicated in suspected rupture and shock. Would be correct only for stable ectopic with beta-hCG <5000 and no foetal cardiac activity (RANZCOG ectopic).

options.text: Commence broad-spectrum IV antibiotics for presumed pelvic inflammatory disease and reassess in 6 hours
options.rationale: IV antibiotics for PID is wrong because the diagnostic picture is shock plus positive pregnancy test plus free fluid - ruptured ectopic until proven otherwise; treating empirically as PID would miss the surgical emergency and likely be fatal. Would be correct only with negative beta-hCG and PID picture (RANZCOG).

options.text: Repeat beta-hCG in 48 hours and discharge home with safety-net advice
options.rationale: Repeat beta-hCG in 48h and discharge is wrong because discharging a shocked patient with positive pregnancy and free fluid is reckless and likely fatal; the anti-pattern is treating beta-hCG trending as a substitute for resuscitation. Would never be correct in shock (RANZCOG ectopic).

explanation.summary: This patient presents with classic features of an ectopic pregnancy: 7 weeks amenorrhoea, right iliac fossa pain, vaginal spotting, and risk factors (previous PID). Since her vital signs are stable, the next immediate step is a transvaginal ultrasound to confirm the diagnosis. (Source: RANZCOG: Early Pregnancy Bleeding)
key_points: Any woman of childbearing age with abdominal pain must have a pregnancy test.
Haemodynamically stable patients with suspected ectopic require urgent transvaginal ultrasound.
Haemodynamically unstable patients require immediate resuscitation and surgical intervention.
explanation.pearls: Cervical motion tenderness (cervical excitation) is a sign of peritoneal irritation from blood in the pelvis, common in both PID and leaking/ruptured ectopic pregnancy.

sources.label: Therapeutic Guidelines: Sexual and Reproductive Health

stem: A 29-year-old G1P0 at 41 weeks gestation is undergoing an induction of labour for post-dates. A vaginal dinoprostone (Prostin) pessary was inserted 6 hours ago. The midwife calls you urgently for a review. The patient is distressed, complaining of continuous, severe abdominal pain without relief between contractions. Palpation of the abdomen reveals that she is having 6 contractions every 10 minutes, with increased resting uterine tone. A continuous cardiotocograph (CTG) is running. The baseline foetal heart rate was 140 bpm an hour ago. For the past 4 minutes, the CTG has demonstrated a single, sudden, and prolonged deceleration down to a nadir of 80 bpm with loss of baseline variability.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 105 bpm, BP 120/75 mmHg, RR 22/min
data_table.Examination: Uterus is hypertonic. Cervix is 3 cm dilated, 1 cm long, membranes intact.
data_table.Investigations: CTG: Prolonged deceleration to 80 bpm for >3 minutes. Contractions: 6 in 10 mins.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate medical intervention?

options.text: Administer an intravenous bolus of magnesium sulfate.
options.rationale: IV magnesium sulfate is wrong because magnesium is for foetal neuroprotection (<30 weeks preterm labour) or eclampsia prophylaxis; this is post-dates hyperstimulation needing tocolysis. Would be correct only at preterm gestations or for severe pre-eclampsia/eclampsia (KEMH: Intrapartum Care).

options.text: Commence an intravenous oxytocin augmentation infusion.
options.rationale: Oxytocin augmentation is wrong because the uterus is already hyperstimulated; adding oxytocin would worsen tachysystole and deepen foetal hypoxia. Would never be correct in hyperstimulation (RANZCOG: Intrapartum Foetal Surveillance).

options.text: Perform an artificial rupture of the amniotic membranes.
options.rationale: Artificial rupture of membranes is wrong because the immediate life-threatening issue is hyperstimulation with prolonged deceleration; ARM does not relieve the contractile state and may worsen cord compression. Would be correct only in non-hyperstimulated labour augmentation (RANZCOG: Intrapartum Foetal Surveillance).

options.text: Perform an immediate emergency category one caesarean section.
options.rationale: Immediate category 1 caesarean is wrong because in-utero resuscitation with acute tocolysis is the first step; tocolysis often restores foetal heart rate within minutes and avoids caesarean. Would be correct only if tocolysis fails or further deterioration occurs (RANZCOG: Intrapartum Foetal Surveillance).

options.text: Administer a stat dose of subcutaneous terbutaline.
options.rationale: The patient has uterine hyperstimulation (tachysystole >5:10) secondary to prostaglandins, causing foetal hypoxia (prolonged deceleration). Acute tocolysis with terbutaline or salbutamol is the most appropriate immediate step. (Source: RANZCOG: Intrapartum Foetal Surveillance)

explanation.summary: This patient is experiencing uterine hyperstimulation (tachysystole) as a complication of prostaglandin induction. The excessive contractions are occluding the spiral arteries, preventing placental reperfusion and leading to a profound, prolonged foetal deceleration. The immediate priority is acute tocolysis (typically subcutaneous terbutaline or intravenous salbutamol) to relax the uterus and restore oxygen delivery to the fetus. (Source: RANZCOG: Intrapartum Foetal Surveillance)
key_points: Tachysystole is defined as >5 contractions in 10 minutes; hypertonus is an abnormally high resting tone.
Uterine hyperstimulation is the combination of excessive uterine activity WITH associated foetal heart rate abnormalities.
Immediate management includes stopping any oxytocin, removing vaginal prostaglandins, left lateral positioning, IV fluids, and acute tocolysis.
explanation.pearls: A prolonged deceleration (>3 minutes) is a hypoxic emergency. Always check for cord prolapse, abruption, and uterine hyperstimulation.

stem: A 28-year-old G2P1 presents to the early pregnancy assessment clinic with mild, vague left-sided pelvic discomfort. She reports 6 weeks of amenorrhoea and a positive home pregnancy test. She denies any vaginal bleeding, shoulder tip pain, or dizziness. On examination, her abdomen is soft with mild left iliac fossa tenderness but no rebound or guarding. Speculum examination is unremarkable with a closed cervical os. Vital signs are within normal limits. A quantitative serum beta-hCG returns at 1500 IU/L. An urgent transvaginal ultrasound is performed, which demonstrates an empty uterus with a homogenous endometrial thickness of 12 mm. Both ovaries appear morphologically normal, there are no visible adnexal masses, and there is no free fluid in the Pouch of Douglas.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 80 bpm, BP 115/75 mmHg, RR 16/min, Temp 36.8 C
data_table.Examination: Abdomen soft, mild LIF tenderness. Os closed, no bleeding.
data_table.Investigations: Serum beta-hCG: 1500 IU/L. TVUS: Empty uterus, normal adnexa.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Administer a systemic dose of intramuscular methotrexate.
options.rationale: IM methotrexate is wrong because methotrexate requires definitive diagnosis of ectopic or confirmed failing PUL where intrauterine viability is excluded; giving it for a PUL risks terminating a viable IUP. Would be correct only after diagnosis is confirmed (RANZCOG: Early Pregnancy Bleeding).

options.text: Arrange an immediate diagnostic and therapeutic laparoscopy.
options.rationale: Diagnostic/therapeutic laparoscopy is wrong because surgery is for identified ectopic or haemodynamic instability; this stable patient with no adnexal mass does not warrant invasive surgery. Would be correct only with imaged ectopic or instability (RANZCOG: Early Pregnancy Bleeding).

options.text: Perform a surgical dilatation and curettage of the uterus.
options.rationale: Surgical D&C is wrong because it would destroy a potentially viable early IUP not yet excluded; chorionic villi sampling can differentiate but is not first-line. Would be correct only after failing trend confirmed and viable IUP excluded (RANZCOG: Early Pregnancy Bleeding).

options.text: Commence oral progesterone supplementation to support pregnancy.
options.rationale: Oral progesterone is wrong because progesterone has no role in PUL management; it is sometimes used in recurrent miscarriage or in confirmed viable pregnancies. Would be correct only in confirmed viable IUP with bleeding (RANZCOG: Early Pregnancy Bleeding).

options.text: Arrange a repeat serum beta-hCG level in 48 hours.
options.rationale: This is a Pregnancy of Unknown Location (PUL). Because the patient is haemodynamically stable and no ectopic mass is seen, serial beta-hCG testing is required to determine the trajectory (viable IUP, failing PUL, or ectopic). (Source: RANZCOG: Early Pregnancy Bleeding)

explanation.summary: This patient presents with a Pregnancy of Unknown Location (PUL)  -  a positive pregnancy test with an empty uterus and no visible ectopic mass on transvaginal ultrasound. Given her clinical stability, the standard of care is expectant management with a repeat quantitative serum beta-hCG in 48 hours to assess the doubling rate, which helps differentiate between an early viable intrauterine pregnancy, a failing pregnancy, or an ectopic pregnancy. (Source: RANZCOG: Early Pregnancy Bleeding)
key_points: A visible gestational sac may not be seen on transvaginal ultrasound until the beta-hCG reaches the discriminatory zone (typically 1500 - 2000 IU/L, depending on local institutional guidelines).
A beta-hCG of 1500 IU/L without a visible sac does NOT confirm an ectopic; it requires careful follow-up.
In a normal early intrauterine pregnancy, the beta-hCG should rise by at least 66% over 48 hours.
Never administer methotrexate or perform a D&C for a PUL unless a viable intrauterine pregnancy has been definitively excluded.
explanation.pearls: The 'discriminatory zone' is not an absolute cut-off. If a patient is stable, it is always safer to wait and repeat the bloods in 48 hours than to inadvertently terminate a normal early pregnancy.

stem: A 32-year-old primigravida at 33 weeks and 5 days gestation presents with worsening frontal headache and epigastric discomfort for 18 hours. She has chronic hypertension managed with labetalol 200 mg twice daily and gestational diabetes controlled with metformin. Foetal movements are present but reduced compared with baseline. She denies visual loss. Her pregnancy has otherwise been uncomplicated. On examination she appears uncomfortable but alert. Reflexes are brisk with 2 beats of ankle clonus. Fundal height is appropriate for dates. CTG shows baseline 145 bpm with normal variability and two accelerations in 20 minutes. Ultrasound 3 days earlier showed estimated foetal weight on the 18th centile with normal umbilical artery Dopplers. Urine protein:creatinine ratio today is 62 mg/mmol. Blood tests show platelets 112 x10^9/L, ALT 86 U/L, AST 79 U/L, creatinine 102 micromol/L, urate 0.42 mmol/L.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 171/112 mmHg repeated after 15 minutes 168/108 mmHg, HR 88 bpm, RR 18/min, Temp 36.8 C, SpO2 98% room air
data_table.Examination: Brisk reflexes with clonus, mild RUQ tenderness, no pulmonary oedema
data_table.Investigations: PCR 62 mg/mmol, platelets 112 x10^9/L, ALT 86 U/L, creatinine 102 micromol/L, reassuring CTG
data_table.Medications: Labetalol 200 mg BD, metformin 500 mg TDS, no drug allergies

lead_in: What is the single best next intervention?

options.text: Increase oral labetalol, repeat blood tests in 12 hours, and continue expectant inpatient monitoring
options.rationale: Escalating oral labetalol and observing is wrong because severe-range hypertension with end-organ features mandates urgent IV stabilisation and delivery planning; oral escalation is too slow. Would be correct only in mild gestational hypertension without severe features (RANZCOG Hypertension in Pregnancy).

options.text: Commence magnesium sulfate, treat severe hypertension acutely, and proceed to delivery after maternal stabilisation
options.rationale: Severe pre-eclampsia with neurologic symptoms and biochemical end-organ involvement after 34 weeks approaches the threshold for maternal-first management. Stabilisation with seizure prophylaxis and blood pressure control should precede delivery. (Source: RANZCOG Hypertension in Pregnancy)

options.text: Administer corticosteroids and defer delivery for 48 hours provided CTG remains reassuring
options.rationale: Corticosteroids and defer 48h is wrong because severe maternal features override the steroid window after 34 weeks; delivery should not be delayed solely for steroid completion in deteriorating maternal status. Would be correct only at preterm gestations with stable maternal status (KEMH Hypertensive Disorders Guideline).

options.text: Arrange urgent caesarean section without seizure prophylaxis because foetal growth restriction is present
options.rationale: Urgent caesarean without MgSO4 is wrong because magnesium sulfate is mandatory in severe pre-eclampsia regardless of mode/route of delivery; growth restriction alone does not dictate caesarean either. Would never be correct to skip MgSO4 in severe pre-eclampsia (RANZCOG Hypertension in Pregnancy).

options.text: Repeat ultrasound Dopplers and continue outpatient review if laboratory values remain stable
options.rationale: Outpatient review is wrong because sustained severe hypertension and symptoms of impending eclampsia preclude outpatient management; the anti-pattern is treating severe features like mild disease. Would be correct only in mild gestational hypertension with low-risk profile (KEMH Hypertensive Disorders Guideline).

explanation.summary: This patient has severe pre-eclampsia with sustained severe hypertension, headache, clonus, elevated transaminases, thrombocytopenia, and renal involvement. Australian guidance prioritises maternal stabilisation with magnesium sulfate and antihypertensive therapy followed by delivery rather than prolonged expectant management. (Source: RANZCOG Hypertension in Pregnancy)
key_points: Severe-range BP is at least 160/110 mmHg requiring urgent treatment. (Source: RANZCOG Hypertension in Pregnancy)
Neurologic symptoms and laboratory deterioration indicate severe disease requiring delivery planning. (Source: KEMH Hypertensive Disorders Guideline)
explanation.pearls: Completion of corticosteroids should not delay delivery when maternal severe features are evolving. (Source: KEMH Hypertensive Disorders Guideline)

stem: A 29-year-old woman presents with 2 days of light vaginal bleeding and intermittent left iliac fossa pain. She is 7 weeks from her last menstrual period and conceived spontaneously. She has a history of pelvic inflammatory disease 4 years ago and laparoscopic appendicectomy as a teenager. She feels mildly light-headed when standing but has no syncope. Pregnancy test is positive. Transvaginal ultrasound shows no intrauterine gestational sac, a 19 mm left adnexal heterogeneous mass, and a small volume of free fluid in the pouch of Douglas. Serum beta-hCG is 2350 IU/L and was 2280 IU/L 48 hours earlier.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 108/72 mmHg, HR 94 bpm, RR 16/min, Temp 36.7 C
data_table.Examination: Mild left adnexal tenderness, no guarding or rebound
data_table.Investigations: beta-hCG plateau over 48 hours, TVUS suspicious left adnexal mass

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Repeat beta-hCG and ultrasound in 7 days because this is likely an early viable intrauterine pregnancy
options.rationale: Repeat in 7 days assuming viable IUP is wrong because plateauing beta-hCG above the discriminatory zone with a suspicious adnexal mass is highly suggestive of ectopic; expectant reassessment risks rupture. Would be correct only with rising appropriately doubling beta-hCG and definite IUP on scan (RANZCOG Early Pregnancy Loss).

options.text: Offer single-dose intramuscular methotrexate after confirming suitability for medical management
options.rationale: She is haemodynamically stable with likely unruptured ectopic pregnancy, plateauing beta-hCG, and imaging suspicion, making medical management appropriate if no contraindications exist. (Source: KEMH Ectopic Pregnancy Guideline)

options.text: Proceed directly to emergency laparotomy because free fluid confirms rupture
options.rationale: Emergency laparotomy is wrong because small free fluid can be physiological or limited bleeding; she is haemodynamically stable without peritonism. Would be correct only with shock, large haemoperitoneum or peritonism (KEMH Ectopic Pregnancy Guideline).

options.text: Arrange dilation and curettage to exclude missed miscarriage before treatment
options.rationale: D&C to exclude missed miscarriage is wrong because the adnexal mass and plateauing beta-hCG already strongly support ectopic; D&C risks delaying treatment and unnecessary surgery. Would be correct only when discriminating PUL between failing IUP and ectopic with no adnexal mass (RANZCOG Early Pregnancy Loss).

options.text: Commence oral progesterone and repeat beta-hCG after 48 hours
options.rationale: Oral progesterone is wrong because progesterone has no role in suspected ectopic and risks delaying definitive treatment; the anti-pattern is treating any pregnancy bleeding with progesterone reflexively. Would never be correct in suspected ectopic (KEMH Ectopic Pregnancy Guideline).

explanation.summary: This is a probable stable tubal ectopic pregnancy: plateauing beta-hCG above the discriminatory threshold plus an adnexal mass. Stable patients meeting criteria can be considered for methotrexate after confirming suitability and follow-up reliability. (Source: KEMH Ectopic Pregnancy Guideline)
key_points: Plateauing beta-hCG is concerning for non-viable or ectopic pregnancy. (Source: RANZCOG Early Pregnancy Loss)
Haemodynamic instability or rupture signs would mandate surgery. (Source: KEMH Ectopic Pregnancy Guideline)
explanation.pearls: Trend interpretation matters more than any single beta-hCG value. (Source: RANZCOG Early Pregnancy Loss)

stem: A 37-year-old primigravid woman at 34 weeks' gestation presents with a 2-day history of worsening frontal headache, flashing lights, and right upper quadrant pain. She has had increasing ankle swelling for a week. Her antenatal record shows normal blood pressure until today, and she is taking aspirin 100 mg daily and iron. She denies vaginal bleeding or contractions. On examination she looks uncomfortable and mildly oedematous. Deep tendon reflexes are brisk. The foetal heart rate is normal.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 168/108 mmHg, pulse 92/min, temp 36.7 deg C
data_table.Examination: Brisk reflexes, 2+ oedema, epigastric/right upper quadrant tenderness, no pulmonary oedema
data_table.Investigations: Urine protein:creatinine ratio 0.48; platelets 92 x10^9/L; creatinine 112 micromol/L; ALT 84 U/L
data_table.Medications: Aspirin 100 mg daily, iron tablets; no known drug allergies

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Admit for observation only and repeat blood tests tomorrow
options.rationale: Admit and observe is wrong because severe-range BP plus symptoms plus thrombocytopenia/renal/liver derangement defines severe pre-eclampsia; observation alone misses the seizure-prophylaxis and stabilisation steps. Would be correct only in mild gestational hypertension without severe features (RANZCOG hypertensive disorders).

options.text: Start oral labetalol and plan routine outpatient review
options.rationale: Oral labetalol and outpatient review is wrong because severe pre-eclampsia requires inpatient maternal stabilisation; outpatient management is unsafe with severe features. Would be correct only in mild hypertension without severe features (RANZCOG hypertensive disorders).

options.text: Begin magnesium sulfate and urgent delivery planning after maternal stabilisation
options.rationale: She has pre-eclampsia with severe features, including severe hypertension, symptoms, thrombocytopenia, and renal/liver involvement. The next step is maternal stabilisation, including magnesium sulfate, then delivery planning based on maternal and foetal status. Source: RANZCOG hypertensive disorders guidance.

options.text: Administer terbutaline and tocolyse until 37 weeks
options.rationale: Terbutaline and tocolyse to 37 weeks is wrong because tocolysis is not the priority in severe pre-eclampsia and prolonging pregnancy at the expense of maternal stability is unsafe; the anti-pattern is treating it like preterm labour. Would never be correct here (RANZCOG hypertensive disorders).

options.text: Perform caesarean section immediately without stabilisation
options.rationale: Immediate caesarean without stabilisation is wrong because magnesium sulfate and BP control should precede surgery unless maternal/foetal compromise is imminent; operating on uncontrolled severe hypertension risks intra-operative stroke and eclampsia. Would be correct only in crashing maternal or foetal emergency (RANZCOG hypertensive disorders).

explanation.summary: This woman has severe pre-eclampsia because she has severe hypertension, symptoms, thrombocytopenia, and renal and liver involvement. The next step is maternal stabilisation, including magnesium sulfate, followed by urgent delivery planning. Source: RANZCOG hypertensive disorders guidance.
key_points: Severe-range blood pressure with end-organ features defines severe disease. Source: RANZCOG hypertensive disorders guidance.
Magnesium sulfate is used for seizure prophylaxis in severe pre-eclampsia. Source: RANZCOG hypertensive disorders guidance.
Delivery planning follows stabilisation and depends on gestation and maternal-foetal status. Source: RANZCOG hypertensive disorders guidance.
explanation.pearls: Symptoms such as headache, visual disturbance, and right upper quadrant pain are red flags even before lab results return. Source: RANZCOG hypertensive disorders guidance.

stem: A 36-year-old multiparous woman develops heavy vaginal bleeding 20 minutes after an uncomplicated vaginal delivery at 39 weeks gestation. Labour was induced for gestational hypertension and lasted 18 hours with oxytocin augmentation. She delivered a 4.3 kg infant and required repair of a second-degree perineal tear. The placenta appeared complete on inspection. She has a history of asthma requiring intermittent salbutamol and experienced postpartum haemorrhage after her previous delivery. On review she feels dizzy and nauseated. The uterus is enlarged and boggy despite ongoing uterine massage. Estimated blood loss is now 1400 mL despite oxytocin infusion and tranexamic acid administration.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 88/54 mmHg, HR 132 bpm, RR 24/min, Temp 36.5 C, SpO2 98% room air
data_table.Examination: Boggy enlarged uterus, ongoing vaginal bleeding, no retained placental tissue visible
data_table.Investigations: Hb 89 g/L from antenatal baseline 118 g/L, fibrinogen 1.8 g/L
data_table.Medications: Oxytocin infusion running, tranexamic acid already administered, salbutamol PRN

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next uterotonic intervention?

options.text: Administer intramuscular carboprost because uterine atony persists despite oxytocin infusion
options.rationale: IM carboprost is wrong because carboprost (15-methyl PGF2alpha) is relatively contraindicated in asthma due to bronchospasm risk; in atonic PPH the next uterotonic must avoid her asthma. Would be correct only in non-asthmatic women (RANZCOG Postpartum Haemorrhage Guideline).

options.text: Administer intravenous ergometrine while continuing uterine massage and haemorrhage protocol activation
options.rationale: IV ergometrine is wrong because ergometrine causes vasoconstriction and worsens hypertension; it is relatively contraindicated in hypertensive disorders. Would be correct only in normotensive women (KEMH Postpartum Haemorrhage Guideline).

options.text: Administer misoprostol while preparing escalation measures for ongoing uterine atony management
options.rationale: This patient has contraindications to both carboprost and ergometrine due to asthma and gestational hypertension. Misoprostol is an appropriate additional uterotonic while escalation continues. (Source: RANZCOG Postpartum Haemorrhage Guideline)

options.text: Proceed directly to hysterectomy because recurrent postpartum haemorrhage predicts failed medical therapy
options.rationale: Direct hysterectomy is wrong because medical and tamponade measures should be exhausted in a haemodynamically manageable patient before proceeding to hysterectomy; the anti-pattern is jumping to definitive surgery prematurely. Would be correct only with refractory haemorrhage after all conservative measures (RANZCOG Postpartum Haemorrhage Guideline).

options.text: Withhold further uterotonics and prioritise fibrinogen replacement because coagulopathy is the primary issue
options.rationale: Withhold uterotonics for fibrinogen is wrong because uterine atony remains the major bleeding source and must be treated alongside fibrinogen correction; the anti-pattern is sequential rather than parallel resuscitation. Would never be correct as standalone strategy (KEMH Postpartum Haemorrhage Guideline).

explanation.summary: This is uterine atony causing major postpartum haemorrhage. The challenge is recognising that commonly used uterotonics are relatively contraindicated because of asthma and hypertensive disease, making misoprostol the safest next pharmacologic option. (Source: RANZCOG Postpartum Haemorrhage Guideline)
key_points: Carboprost may precipitate bronchospasm in asthma. (Source: KEMH Postpartum Haemorrhage Guideline)
Ergometrine should be avoided in hypertensive disorders of pregnancy. (Source: RANZCOG Postpartum Haemorrhage Guideline)
explanation.pearls: Obstetric emergencies often require choosing the least harmful effective therapy rather than the most potent routine option. (Source: RANZCOG Postpartum Haemorrhage Guideline)

stem: A 30-year-old primigravida at 41 weeks gestation is in labour following induction for post-dates pregnancy. She received dinoprostone overnight followed by oxytocin augmentation for slow progress. Epidural analgesia was inserted 3 hours ago. She is now fully dilated and has been actively pushing for 70 minutes. The fetus is in occipito-posterior position at +1 station. CTG over the preceding 40 minutes shows a baseline rising from 145 to 170 bpm with minimal variability and recurrent late decelerations after most contractions. There have been no accelerations for 35 minutes. Maternal observations show increasing pyrexia despite intravenous fluids. Vaginal examination confirms caput and moulding.

data_table.Vital signs: Maternal Temp 38.5 C, HR 122 bpm, BP 108/66 mmHg, RR 20/min
data_table.Examination: Fully dilated, OP position, +1 station, significant caput and moulding
data_table.Investigations: CTG with foetal tachycardia, minimal variability, recurrent late decelerations
data_table.Medications: Oxytocin infusion, epidural infusion, IV fluids

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Continue pushing for another hour because nulliparous women commonly require prolonged second stage labour
options.rationale: Continue pushing is wrong because the CTG is pathological with multiple concerning features; ongoing labour risks worsening hypoxia, and the nulliparous-second-stage argument does not justify continued exposure. Would be correct only with reassuring CTG (RANZCOG Intrapartum Foetal Surveillance).

options.text: Perform rotational instrumental delivery in the birth suite because the cervix is fully dilated
options.rationale: Rotational instrumental delivery is wrong because high station and significant moulding indicate uncertain likelihood of rapid safe vaginal birth; failed instrumental delivery worsens neonatal outcomes. Would be correct only with low station, minimal moulding and confidence of success (RANZCOG Operative Vaginal Birth).

options.text: Stop oxytocin, commence intrauterine resuscitation, and proceed to category 1 caesarean section
options.rationale: This CTG is pathological with foetal tachycardia, minimal variability, and recurrent late decelerations suggesting significant foetal compromise, likely worsened by suspected chorioamnionitis. Immediate intrauterine resuscitation and urgent caesarean delivery are indicated. (Source: RANZCOG Intrapartum Foetal Surveillance)

options.text: Perform foetal scalp stimulation and continue labour if a transient acceleration is observed
options.rationale: Scalp stimulation is wrong because scalp stimulation helps clarify indeterminate tracings; this is already pathological with foetal tachycardia, minimal variability and recurrent late decelerations - urgent delivery is indicated. Would be correct only for suspicious (not pathological) CTGs (RANZCOG Intrapartum Foetal Surveillance).

options.text: Administer broad-spectrum antibiotics and reassess the CTG after maternal fever resolves
options.rationale: Antibiotics and reassess is wrong because antibiotics for chorioamnionitis are necessary but must not delay urgent delivery in pathological CTG; the anti-pattern is treating infection while accepting foetal compromise. Would be correct only as adjunct alongside delivery, not as substitute (RANZCOG Intrapartum Care).

explanation.summary: This vignette requires integrating CTG interpretation with obstetric feasibility. Although fully dilated, the foetal position, station, caput, and pathological CTG make safe instrumental birth unlikely, so urgent caesarean section after intrauterine resuscitation is most appropriate. (Source: RANZCOG Intrapartum Foetal Surveillance)
key_points: Minimal variability with recurrent late decelerations and foetal tachycardia indicates pathological CTG. (Source: RANZCOG Intrapartum Foetal Surveillance)
Operative vaginal birth requires a high likelihood of safe successful delivery. (Source: RANZCOG Operative Vaginal Birth)
explanation.pearls: A fully dilated cervix does not automatically make instrumental delivery the safest option. (Source: RANZCOG Operative Vaginal Birth)

stem: A 34-year-old G3P2 woman at 35 weeks gestation presents by ambulance with sudden-onset severe abdominal pain and vaginal bleeding that began 45 minutes earlier while at home. She has chronic hypertension treated with nifedipine and continues to smoke 10 cigarettes daily despite counselling. Earlier today she noticed reduced foetal movements. Her previous pregnancies were uncomplicated vaginal deliveries. On arrival she appears distressed and reports constant abdominal pain between contractions. The uterus feels firm and diffusely tender. Vaginal bleeding is moderate in volume and dark red. CTG demonstrates recurrent prolonged decelerations with minimal variability. A bedside ultrasound shows a live singleton fetus in cephalic presentation with an anterior placenta clear of the cervical os.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 94/60 mmHg, HR 126 bpm, RR 24/min, Temp 36.6 C, SpO2 97% room air
data_table.Examination: Tender rigid uterus, ongoing dark vaginal bleeding, no signs of labour
data_table.Investigations: Hb 96 g/L, fibrinogen 1.5 g/L, platelets 142 x10^9/L, pathological CTG

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Arrange formal ultrasound with Doppler assessment because the diagnosis must be confirmed radiologically before delivery
options.rationale: Formal ultrasound first is wrong because placental abruption is primarily a clinical diagnosis; ultrasound has poor sensitivity (only ~25%) and may falsely reassure. With maternal instability and foetal compromise, imaging delays critical intervention. Would be correct only in stable women without compromise (RANZCOG Antepartum Haemorrhage).

options.text: Proceed to urgent caesarean section while initiating maternal resuscitation and blood product support
options.rationale: This patient has suspected placental abruption with maternal instability and pathological CTG indicating foetal compromise. Immediate delivery with simultaneous resuscitation is indicated. (Source: RANZCOG Antepartum Haemorrhage)

options.text: Commence tocolysis and corticosteroids because the fetus remains preterm at 35 weeks gestation
options.rationale: Tocolysis and steroids is wrong because tocolysis is contraindicated in significant abruption with bleeding and foetal compromise; delivery cannot wait for steroid completion. Would be correct only in mild abruption with stable mother and reassuring CTG at preterm gestation (KEMH Antepartum Haemorrhage Guideline).

options.text: Perform artificial rupture of membranes and attempt induction because she previously delivered vaginally
options.rationale: ARM and induction is wrong because the foetus is compromised and the mother haemodynamically unstable; induction would be too slow and unsafe. Would be correct only in mild abruption with stable mother, term gestation, favourable cervix and reassuring CTG (RANZCOG Antepartum Haemorrhage).

options.text: Observe with continuous CTG monitoring because vaginal bleeding volume is only moderate
options.rationale: Observation only is wrong because vaginal bleeding can underestimate concealed haemorrhage in abruption; maternal shock and pathological CTG mandate delivery. Would be correct only in mild, stable abruption with reassuring CTG (KEMH Antepartum Haemorrhage Guideline).

explanation.summary: This patient has classic placental abruption with abdominal pain, uterine rigidity, vaginal bleeding, maternal shock, and foetal compromise. Ultrasound cannot reliably exclude abruption, and urgent delivery with simultaneous resuscitation is required. (Source: RANZCOG Antepartum Haemorrhage)
key_points: Placental abruption is a clinical diagnosis. (Source: RANZCOG Antepartum Haemorrhage)
Concealed haemorrhage may be substantial despite modest visible bleeding. (Source: KEMH Antepartum Haemorrhage Guideline)
explanation.pearls: Low fibrinogen in obstetric haemorrhage strongly suggests severe placental abruption. (Source: KEMH Antepartum Haemorrhage Guideline)

stem: A 29-year-old woman, para 2, is 20 minutes after a normal vaginal birth at 39 weeks. The placenta delivered spontaneously, but she now has ongoing heavy vaginal bleeding and has soaked through two pads. She had antenatal iron deficiency and a previous postpartum haemorrhage after her first delivery. The current baby weighed 4.2 kg. She received intrapartum oxytocin and had a prolonged second stage. She has asthma, for which she uses inhaled budesonide-formoterol, and no drug allergies. On examination the uterus feels boggy and enlarged. There are no retained placental fragments visible, and the perineum is intact. Her blood pressure is falling, and she is becoming pale and clammy.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 88/54 mmHg, HR 126 bpm, RR 22/min, SpO2 99% on room air, temp 36.5 deg C
data_table.Examination: Boggy uterus, ongoing heavy vaginal loss, no perineal tear seen, no retained tissue visible
data_table.Investigations: Hb 86 g/L antenatally; bedside coagulation pending
data_table.Medications: Intrapartum oxytocin given; budesonide-formoterol inhaler; no drug allergies

lead_in: What is the single best next intervention?

options.text: Intramuscular ergometrine 250 mcg now
options.rationale: IM ergometrine is wrong because ergometrine is contraindicated in hypertension and is suboptimal in asthma; the patient's comorbidities exclude it. Would be correct only in normotensive non-asthmatic women (RANZCOG postpartum haemorrhage).

options.text: Intramuscular carboprost 250 mcg now
options.rationale: IM carboprost is wrong because carboprost can precipitate bronchospasm in asthma; her asthma makes it relatively contraindicated. Would be correct only in non-asthmatic women (RANZCOG postpartum haemorrhage).

options.text: Intravenous tranexamic acid 1 g now
options.rationale: Tranexamic acid should be given early in postpartum haemorrhage alongside ongoing uterine massage and escalation measures. It is appropriate regardless of whether the final cause is atony, and it is not contraindicated by asthma. Source: RANZCOG postpartum haemorrhage guidance.

options.text: Intramuscular oxytocin 10 IU now
options.rationale: IM oxytocin 10 IU is wrong because she has already received intrapartum oxytocin and is actively haemorrhaging; the priority is escalation (tranexamic acid plus alternative uterotonic plus mechanical measures) rather than repeating oxytocin alone. Would be correct only as initial prophylaxis, not in established refractory PPH (RANZCOG postpartum haemorrhage).

options.text: Immediate hysterectomy without further medical treatment
options.rationale: Immediate hysterectomy is wrong because surgical escalation is reserved for refractory bleeding after medical therapy fails; jumping to hysterectomy first skips reversible interventions including tranexamic acid. Would be correct only as last resort after all conservative measures fail (RANZCOG postpartum haemorrhage).

explanation.summary: This is primary postpartum haemorrhage due to uterine atony, with shock features and ongoing bleeding. Early intravenous tranexamic acid is the best next intervention while uterine massage and broader haemorrhage management continue. Source: RANZCOG postpartum haemorrhage guidance.
key_points: A boggy enlarged uterus after birth points to uterine atony. Source: RANZCOG postpartum haemorrhage guidance.
Tranexamic acid is used early in PPH alongside uterotonic and resuscitative measures. Source: RANZCOG postpartum haemorrhage guidance.
Carboprost is a poor choice in asthma, and ergometrine is not ideal when alternative uterotonics are safer. Source: RANZCOG postpartum haemorrhage guidance.
explanation.pearls: The correct answer in PPH often depends on the cause and the contraindications to the next uterotonic, not just the amount of bleeding. Source: RANZCOG postpartum haemorrhage guidance.

stem: A 28-year-old primigravida at 31 weeks and 4 days gestation presents with ongoing clear vaginal fluid loss for 18 hours. She has felt intermittent lower abdominal tightening but no regular contractions. Pregnancy has been uncomplicated apart from iron deficiency anaemia. Speculum examination confirms pooling of liquor in the posterior fornix. Initial CTG is reassuring and ultrasound demonstrates cephalic presentation with reduced amniotic fluid volume. She is admitted for expectant management with corticosteroids and intravenous antibiotics. Thirty-six hours later she develops increasing uterine tenderness and reports feeling feverish. The foetal heart rate baseline rises progressively over the next hour.

data_table.Vital signs: Temp 38.2 C, HR 118 bpm, BP 108/64 mmHg, RR 20/min
data_table.Examination: Uterine tenderness, ongoing liquor leakage, no heavy vaginal bleeding
data_table.Investigations: WCC 18.6 x10^9/L, CRP 112 mg/L, CTG foetal baseline 175 bpm with preserved variability
data_table.Medications: Betamethasone course commenced, IV antibiotics running

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Continue expectant management until corticosteroids are completed because CTG variability remains reassuring
options.rationale: Continue expectancy for steroid completion is wrong because suspected chorioamnionitis mandates delivery regardless of steroid status; ongoing infection drives maternal sepsis and foetal injury. Would be correct only with no infection signs and stable preterm PPROM (RANZCOG Preterm Birth).

options.text: Proceed toward delivery while continuing broad-spectrum antibiotics for presumed intrauterine infection
options.rationale: Maternal fever, uterine tenderness, inflammatory marker elevation, and foetal tachycardia strongly suggest chorioamnionitis requiring delivery rather than ongoing expectant management. (Source: KEMH PPROM Guideline)

options.text: Commence tocolysis because uterine activity may reduce after antibiotic therapy is escalated
options.rationale: Tocolysis is wrong because tocolysis is contraindicated in suspected intra-amniotic infection (delays delivery, masks deterioration); the anti-pattern is treating preterm uterine activity as the threat when infection is. Would never be correct in chorioamnionitis (KEMH PPROM Guideline).

options.text: Repeat ultrasound and continue observation because foetal tachycardia may reflect maternal dehydration alone
options.rationale: Repeat ultrasound and observe is wrong because maternal fever, uterine tenderness and raised inflammatory markers make chorioamnionitis far more likely than isolated dehydration; the anti-pattern is treating infection diagnosis as a uncertainty problem rather than clinical-syndrome recognition. Would never be correct in this clinical picture (RANZCOG Preterm Birth).

options.text: Stop antibiotics because inflammatory markers commonly rise after corticosteroid administration in pregnancy
options.rationale: Stop antibiotics, blame steroids is wrong because while steroids transiently affect WCC, the overall clinical picture is consistent with infection; stopping antibiotics in suspected chorioamnionitis worsens maternal and foetal outcomes. Would never be correct in this picture (KEMH PPROM Guideline).

explanation.summary: This patient with PPROM has developed likely chorioamnionitis, evidenced by maternal fever, uterine tenderness, inflammatory marker elevation, and foetal tachycardia. Infection supersedes attempts to prolong pregnancy for corticosteroid completion, so delivery is indicated. (Source: KEMH PPROM Guideline)
key_points: Suspected chorioamnionitis is an indication for delivery in PPROM. (Source: RANZCOG Preterm Birth)
Tocolysis is generally contraindicated in intrauterine infection. (Source: KEMH PPROM Guideline)
explanation.pearls: A reassuring CTG variability pattern does not exclude clinically significant intrauterine infection. (Source: RANZCOG Preterm Birth)

stem: A 27-year-old woman attends her GP for review of cervical screening results. She had her first cervical screening test at age 25, which was negative for oncogenic HPV. She has no abnormal bleeding, pelvic pain, or postcoital symptoms. She received partial HPV vaccination during adolescence. Her current test result shows oncogenic HPV 16 detected with negative liquid-based cytology. She asks whether she can simply repeat the test in 12 months because the cytology is normal and she feels well. She has no history of immunosuppression or previous cervical procedures.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 112/70 mmHg, HR 74 bpm, Temp 36.8 C
data_table.Examination: No examination abnormalities reported
data_table.Investigations: Cervical screening test positive for HPV 16, negative liquid-based cytology

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Repeat cervical screening in 12 months because cytology is negative and she is asymptomatic
options.rationale: 12-month repeat is wrong because HPV 16 positivity triggers direct colposcopy in the Australian NCSP regardless of cytology; the anti-pattern is treating HPV 16 like an intermediate-risk result. Would be correct only for non-16/18 HPV with negative reflex cytology (Cancer Council Australia Cervical Screening).

options.text: Refer for colposcopy because oncogenic HPV 16 carries higher cervical neoplasia risk
options.rationale: Australian cervical screening guidelines recommend direct colposcopy referral for HPV 16 or 18 positivity regardless of reflex cytology results. (Source: Cancer Council Australia Cervical Screening)

options.text: Repeat HPV testing in 5 years because prior screening at age 25 was normal
options.rationale: 5-year repeat is wrong because a previous normal result does not override current HPV 16 detection; the screening interval applies only when current result is low risk. Would be correct only with current negative HPV result (NCSP Guidelines).

options.text: Arrange immediate excisional treatment because HPV 16 confirms high-grade dysplasia
options.rationale: Immediate excisional treatment is wrong because HPV 16 increases risk but does not confirm histologic high-grade disease; treatment decisions require colposcopy and biopsy. Would be correct only after histological HSIL confirmation (Cancer Council Australia Cervical Screening).

options.text: Commence HPV vaccination booster doses before deciding whether colposcopy is required
options.rationale: HPV vaccination booster first is wrong because vaccination is preventive and does not alter management of current HPV 16 positivity; colposcopy is required regardless of vaccination status. Would never substitute for colposcopy (NCSP Guidelines).

explanation.summary: Under the Australian National Cervical Screening Program, HPV 16 or 18 positivity mandates direct colposcopy referral regardless of cytology findings because of increased risk of significant cervical pathology. (Source: Cancer Council Australia Cervical Screening)
key_points: HPV 16 and 18 positivity bypasses routine repeat testing pathways. (Source: NCSP Guidelines)
Negative cytology does not exclude clinically important cervical disease in HPV 16 positive patients. (Source: Cancer Council Australia Cervical Screening)
explanation.pearls: Many students incorrectly over-prioritise cytology and under-recognise the risk implications of HPV genotyping in the Australian screening pathway. (Source: NCSP Guidelines)

stem: A 33-year-old primigravida at 35 weeks gestation presents with worsening frontal headache and visual blurring over the past 12 hours. Her pregnancy has been complicated by gestational hypertension diagnosed at 31 weeks. She reports increasing upper abdominal discomfort and nausea today. Foetal movements remain present. On review of antenatal records, foetal growth was on the 12th percentile at the most recent ultrasound. In the assessment unit she appears uncomfortable and photophobic. Reflexes are brisk. CTG demonstrates a reactive trace without decelerations.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 174/112 mmHg, HR 96 bpm, RR 20/min, Temp 36.8 C
data_table.Examination: Brisk reflexes with 2-beat clonus, right upper quadrant tenderness, mild pedal oedema
data_table.Investigations: Platelets 108 x10^9/L, ALT 96 U/L, creatinine 102 micromol/L, urine protein:creatinine ratio elevated

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Increase oral antihypertensive therapy and continue expectant management because CTG remains reassuring
options.rationale: Oral antihypertensives plus expectancy is wrong because severe pre-eclampsia features (neurological symptoms, severe BP, thrombocytopenia, transaminitis) need IV control plus magnesium and delivery planning; oral escalation is inadequate. Would be correct only for mild gestational hypertension without severe features (SOMANZ Hypertension Guideline).

options.text: Commence magnesium sulfate and plan expedited delivery after maternal stabilisation
options.rationale: Severe pre-eclampsia with neurological symptoms and end-organ involvement requires seizure prophylaxis and delivery planning after stabilisation. (Source: SOMANZ Hypertension Guideline)

options.text: Administer corticosteroids and delay delivery for at least 7 days because prematurity risk outweighs maternal concerns
options.rationale: Steroids and delay 7 days is wrong because at 35 weeks maternal severe disease outweighs prematurity considerations; the anti-pattern is treating maternal severe disease as a trade-off for foetal lung maturity at near-term gestations. Would be correct only at earlier preterm gestations with stable maternal status (RANZCOG Hypertensive Disorders).

options.text: Arrange outpatient blood pressure review tomorrow because foetal wellbeing remains reassuring
options.rationale: Outpatient review tomorrow is wrong because severe pre-eclampsia features mandate inpatient management with magnesium sulfate; the anti-pattern is using foetal wellbeing alone to justify outpatient care. Would never be correct with severe features (SOMANZ Hypertension Guideline).

options.text: Commence intravenous antibiotics because thrombocytopenia and transaminitis suggest occult sepsis
options.rationale: IV antibiotics for sepsis is wrong because the pattern (HELLP-like with neurological symptoms and severe BP) supports severe pre-eclampsia, not sepsis; thrombocytopenia and transaminitis fit HELLP. Would be correct only with clinical or microbiological evidence of infection (RANZCOG Hypertensive Disorders).

explanation.summary: This patient has severe pre-eclampsia with neurological symptoms, severe hypertension, thrombocytopenia, liver involvement, and renal impairment. Maternal stabilisation with magnesium sulfate and planning for delivery are required despite reassuring foetal monitoring. (Source: SOMANZ Hypertension Guideline)
key_points: Reassuring CTG does not negate severe maternal disease. (Source: RANZCOG Hypertensive Disorders)
Magnesium sulfate reduces eclampsia risk in severe pre-eclampsia. (Source: SOMANZ Hypertension Guideline)
explanation.pearls: Right upper quadrant pain in pre-eclampsia may reflect hepatic involvement and impending severe complications. (Source: SOMANZ Hypertension Guideline)

stem: A 32-year-old G2P1 at 38+2 in spontaneous labour has SROM during VE (cervix 6 cm) and the midwife palpates a pulsatile cord at the introitus. CTG shows sustained foetal bradycardia at 70 bpm despite left lateral position. The on-call obstetric registrar and anaesthetist are 5 minutes away, theatre is being prepared and an emergency category-1 caesarean section has been called. The woman is in a single delivery room (no nearby toilets, no easy access to Foley-bag elevation kit). Maternal vital signs are stable.

data_table.Vital signs: Maternal HR 102 bpm, BP 125/80 mmHg, RR 22, SpO2 98% on room air
data_table.Examination: Visible umbilical cord at introitus, pulsatile, no presenting part visible, cervix fully assessed at 6 cm, station -2
data_table.Investigations: CTG: sustained foetal bradycardia at 70 bpm for 90 seconds, no recovery with position change
data_table.Medications: Terbutaline 250 mcg SC administered 2 minutes ago

lead_in: Which combination is the most appropriate immediate action while awaiting theatre?

options.text: Place the woman in knee-chest position, elevate the presenting part manually with a vaginal hand, and instil 500-700 mL normal saline into the bladder via Foley catheter to maintain elevation; administer tocolysis (terbutaline 250 mcg SC)
options.rationale: Cord prolapse management is: lift presenting part off cord (manual elevation +/- bladder filling, knee-chest or Trendelenburg), tocolysis if contractions, minimise cord handling, immediate category-1 caesarean. Bladder filling allows safe transfer to theatre (RANZCOG cord prolapse).

options.text: Instruct active maternal pushing to expedite vaginal delivery before theatre arrives
options.rationale: Active pushing is wrong because at 6 cm dilation with foetal bradycardia and prolapsed cord, vaginal delivery is impossible; pushing accelerates cord compression and worsens hypoxia. Would never be correct in cord prolapse with incomplete dilation (RANZCOG).

options.text: Manually replace the cord into the uterine cavity to restore foetal circulation
options.rationale: Manual cord replacement is wrong because manipulation triggers umbilical vessel spasm and arterial vasoconstriction; the cord is left in situ while the presenting part is elevated. Would never be correct (RANZCOG).

options.text: Repeat digital VE every 5 minutes to assess for cervical progress before theatre
options.rationale: Repeat digital VE is wrong because repeated examinations stimulate the cord, worsen vasospasm and delay theatre transfer; the focus is presenting-part elevation, not progress assessment. Would never be correct in cord prolapse (RANZCOG).

options.text: Wrap the cord in warm saline-soaked gauze outside the vagina and await spontaneous resolution
options.rationale: Wrap cord and await resolution is wrong because warm saline gauze can be applied if cord is exposed externally, but spontaneous resolution is not a viable strategy; immediate operative delivery is mandatory. Would be correct only as adjunct alongside immediate caesarean transfer (RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: This woman has a cord prolapse following spontaneous rupture of membranes at 6 cm dilation with a high presenting part (station -2). The sustained foetal bradycardia indicates cord compression. While awaiting emergency caesarean section, the priority is continuous manual elevation of the presenting part to decompress the cord, combined with bladder filling to maintain that elevation. Tocolysis has already been given to reduce uterine activity.
key_points: Cord prolapse is an obstetric emergency requiring continuous elevation of the presenting part off the cord until delivery. (Source: RANZCOG: Cord Prolapse)
Bladder filling with 500–700 mL normal saline via catheter is an effective adjunct to manual elevation while preparing for theatre. (Source: KEMH Clinical Guidelines: Cord Prolapse)
Digital examination or manipulation of the cord should be avoided as it can provoke vasospasm and worsen foetal hypoxia. (Source: RANZCOG: Cord Prolapse)
Decision-to-delivery interval target is <30 minutes when foetal compromise is present, but cord decompression measures must continue throughout. (Source: RANZCOG: Cord Prolapse)
explanation.pearls: The hand on the presenting part stays in the vagina until the surgeon is ready to make the uterine incision; do not remove it for transfer, catheterisation, or any other intervention.

stem: A 36-year-old G4P2A1 at 32+1 weeks gestation presents to the birth suite with sudden onset of bright red vaginal bleeding approximately 1 hour ago. She reports no pain and no contractions. The bleeding was initially moderate but has now reduced to spotting. Her obstetric history includes two previous lower-segment caesarean sections (the most recent 2 years ago) and a first-trimester miscarriage managed with suction curettage. At her 20-week morphology scan, the placenta was noted to be posterior and covering the internal os completely. A follow-up scan at 28 weeks confirmed a major placenta praevia. Her antenatal course has been otherwise uncomplicated. On examination, her abdomen is soft and non-tender with a singleton foetus in cephalic presentation. The fundal height is consistent with dates. Speculum examination reveals a closed cervix with a small amount of dark blood in the posterior fornix. Foetal cardiotocography shows a baseline of 145 bpm with normal variability, no decelerations, and no uterine activity. Her haemoglobin is 102 g/L (down from 118 g/L at 28 weeks) and haematocrit is 0.31. The estimated blood loss is approximately 400 mL. She is haemodynamically stable with HR 88 bpm and BP 110/70 mmHg.

data_table.Vital signs: Maternal HR 88 bpm, BP 110/70 mmHg, RR 18, SpO2 98%, Temp 36.8°C
data_table.Examination: Abdomen soft, non-tender, fundal height = dates, foetus cephalic, speculum: closed cervix, dark blood posterior fornix
data_table.Investigations: Hb 102 g/L (was 118 at 28 wks), Hct 0.31, CTG baseline 145 bpm normal variability no decelerations no contractions

lead_in: Which risk factor in this presentation most strongly raises concern for placenta accreta spectrum disorder over an uncomplicated major placenta praevia?

options.text: Maternal age over 35 years at the time of delivery
options.rationale: Maternal age over 35 is wrong because advanced maternal age modestly increases praevia and accreta risk, but the magnitude is far less than that conferred by praevia plus prior uterine surgery; the strongest predictor is the combination. Would be correct only when comparing age effects alone, not against the praevia-plus-CS interaction (RANZCOG: Placenta Accreta Spectrum).

options.text: The confirmed persistence of a major placenta praevia at 28 weeks gestation
options.rationale: Persistent major praevia at 28 weeks is wrong because while persistent praevia raises PAS risk, isolated praevia without prior caesarean carries only about 3% PAS risk; the dominant driver is the combination with prior caesareans. Would be correct only as one component, not the strongest single predictor (RANZCOG: Placenta Accreta Spectrum).

options.text: First-trimester miscarriage managed with suction curettage in addition to the two prior caesarean sections
options.rationale: Suction curettage plus prior caesareans is wrong because while curettage adds minor endometrial disruption, the magnitude of risk is dwarfed by the praevia-plus-multiple-caesarean combination. Would be correct only if asked about additive minor risks, not the strongest predictor (RANZCOG: Placenta Accreta Spectrum).

options.text: The combination of major placenta praevia with two previous lower-segment caesarean sections
options.rationale: The risk of placenta accreta spectrum rises dramatically with the number of prior caesarean sections in the setting of placenta praevia. With no prior CS, the risk is approximately 3%; with one prior CS, 11%; with two prior CS, 40%; with three prior CS, 67%. This exponential rise makes the combination the strongest risk factor. (Source: RANZCOG: Placenta Accreta Spectrum; FIGO Consensus Guidelines on PAS)

options.text: The drop in haemoglobin from 118 g/L at 28 weeks to 102 g/L at presentation with bright red bleeding
options.rationale: Drop in haemoglobin is wrong because the haemoglobin fall reflects current bleeding severity, not PAS risk; PAS risk is determined by pre-existing uterine factors. Would never be correct as a PAS risk factor (RANZCOG: Placenta Accreta Spectrum).

explanation.summary: This question asks the student to identify the strongest risk factor for placenta accreta spectrum (PAS) among the features presented. The exponential relationship between the number of prior caesarean sections and PAS risk in the setting of placenta praevia is the most important clinical predictor. The two prior CS with a persistent major praevia places this woman at approximately 40% risk of PAS, which mandates specific delivery planning including senior surgical presence, blood product availability, and consideration of a multidisciplinary team approach.
key_points: PAS risk with placenta praevia increases exponentially with number of prior caesarean sections: ~3% with none, ~11% with one, ~40% with two, ~67% with three. (Source: RANZCOG: Placenta Accreta Spectrum)

Antenatal diagnosis of PAS relies on grey-scale and colour Doppler ultrasound with findings of loss of the retroplacental clear zone, placental lacunae, and increased vascularity. (Source: FIGO Consensus Guidelines on PAS)
All women with placenta praevia and prior caesarean delivery should have a targeted ultrasound for PAS at 28–32 weeks. (Source: RANZCOG: Placenta Accreta Spectrum)

Suspected PAS should prompt referral to a tertiary centre with multidisciplinary capability including MFM, anaesthetics, interventional radiology, and massive transfusion protocol. (Source: RANZCOG: Placenta Accreta Spectrum)
explanation.pearls: When a woman with prior CS and placenta praevia bleeds, do not perform a digital vaginal examination - it can provoke catastrophic haemorrhage if there is an undiagnosed accreta or praevia.

stem: A 29-year-old primigravida at 40+0 weeks gestation is in the second stage of labour. She has been pushing for 90 minutes with an epidural in situ. Her pregnancy was complicated by diet-controlled gestational diabetes diagnosed at 28 weeks on the 75 g OGTT (fasting 5.3 mmol/L, 1-hour 10.2 mmol/L, 2-hour 8.6 mmol/L). Foetal surveillance has been normal. The head has delivered spontaneously over an intact perineum. Following delivery of the head, the chin retracts tightly against the perineum (turtle-neck sign) and restitution does not occur. Gentle downward traction fails to deliver the anterior shoulder. The accoucheur calls for help. The woman has been moved to the end of the bed and her legs are placed in hyperflexion (McRoberts' position). After one further attempt at downward traction, the anterior shoulder remains undelivered. Suprapubic pressure has been applied by an assistant using a closed fist in a downward and lateral direction with the next contraction. The shoulder has still not delivered. The paediatric team is present and the time since delivery of the head is now 2 minutes and 30 seconds.

data_table.Vital signs: Maternal HR 110 bpm, BP 135/85 mmHg, foetal HR at last assessment 130 bpm pre-push
data_table.Examination: Turtle-neck sign present, restitution absent, head tightly applied to perineum, McRoberts' position achieved, suprapubic pressure unsuccessful
data_table.Investigations: GDM: 75 g OGTT fasting 5.3, 1h 10.2, 2h 8.6 mmol/L (diagnostic per ADIPS criteria)
data_table.Medications: Epidural in situ (bupivacaine 0.1% + fentanyl 2 mcg/mL at 8 mL/hr)

lead_in: What is the SINGLE BEST next manoeuvre to attempt?

options.text: Apply firmer downward traction on the foetal head combined with continuous suprapubic pressure for a full 60 seconds
options.rationale: Firmer downward traction is wrong because excessive traction causes brachial plexus injury without disimpacting the anterior shoulder behind the symphysis; the anti-pattern is using force rather than disimpaction manoeuvres. Would never be correct (RANZCOG: Shoulder Dystocia).

options.text: Deliver the posterior arm by entering the sacral hollow, locating the humerus, sweeping it across the chest, and delivering the arm
options.rationale: When McRoberts' position and suprapubic pressure fail to resolve shoulder dystocia, delivery of the posterior arm is the next recommended step in the HELPERR mnemonic. This reduces the bisacromial diameter by up to 2 cm and has a high success rate. The procedure involves entering posteriorly along the sacral curve, splinting the humerus, and sweeping the arm across the foetal chest. (Source: RANZCOG: Shoulder Dystocia; KEMH: Shoulder Dystocia Guideline)

options.text: Rotate the foetus by applying pressure to the anterior shoulder through the abdominal wall (Rubin I manoeuvre)
options.rationale: Rubin I (external abdominal pressure on anterior shoulder) is wrong because Rubin I is essentially a form of suprapubic pressure and has already been attempted; after external manoeuvres fail, internal rotational manoeuvres or posterior arm delivery are indicated. Would be correct only before suprapubic pressure has been tried (RANZCOG: Shoulder Dystocia).

options.text: Perform an episiotomy to increase the available space for delivery before attempting further traction
options.rationale: Episiotomy is wrong because shoulder dystocia is a bony impaction, not soft-tissue obstruction; episiotomy facilitates access for internal manoeuvres but does not itself relieve impaction. Would be correct only as adjunct to internal manoeuvres, not as a standalone step (RANZCOG: Shoulder Dystocia).

options.text: Perform the Gaskin all-fours manoeuvre by rolling the woman onto her hands and knees
options.rationale: Gaskin all-fours is wrong because with epidural in situ, all-fours positioning is difficult and potentially unsafe; in an Australian tertiary setting with epidural, internal manoeuvres are the next step. Would be correct only without effective regional anaesthesia or as an earlier alternative in unmedicated labour (RANZCOG: Shoulder Dystocia).

explanation.summary: This primigravida with GDM (a known risk factor for shoulder dystocia) has a recognised shoulder dystocia with the classic turtle-neck sign and failure of restitution. McRoberts' position and suprapubic pressure (the first two HELPERR steps) have been correctly applied without success. The next step is delivery of the posterior arm, which is highly effective and reduces the bisacromial diameter. Time from head delivery is critical and internal manoeuvres should not be delayed beyond 3-4 minutes.
key_points: The HELPERR mnemonic guides sequential management: Help, Evaluate for episiotomy, Legs (McRoberts'), Pressure (suprapubic), Enter (internal rotation), Remove posterior arm, Roll (Gaskin). (Source: RANZCOG: Shoulder Dystocia)

Delivery of the posterior arm reduces the bisacromial diameter by approximately 2 cm and resolves the majority of shoulder dystocias that fail external manoeuvres. (Source: KEMH: Shoulder Dystocia Guideline)
Fundal pressure is contraindicated in shoulder dystocia - it worsens impaction and increases the risk of brachial plexus injury and uterine rupture. (Source: RANZCOG: Shoulder Dystocia)
GDM with macrosomia is a major risk factor; this woman met ADIPS diagnostic criteria on all three OGTT values. (Source: ADIPS: Diagnostic Criteria for GDM)
explanation.pearls: When entering the sacral hollow for posterior arm delivery, follow the curve of the sacrum posteriorly to avoid fracturing the humerus - splint the bone with your fingers along its length, never apply pressure at a single point.

stem: A 24-year-old primigravida at 36+4 weeks gestation is brought to the birth suite by ambulance following a tonic-clonic seizure at home that lasted approximately 2 minutes. She has a known diagnosis of pre-eclampsia with severe features diagnosed 3 days ago, for which she has been receiving oral labetalol 200 mg BD. She had been complaining of a severe frontal headache and epigastric pain since this morning. On arrival, she is postictal and drowsy with a GCS of 13 (E3 V4 M6). Her blood pressure is 175/115 mmHg on the left arm in the semi-recumbent position, confirmed with a repeat reading 15 minutes later. She has 3+ protein on urine dipstick and hyperreflexia with 4 beats of clonus at the ankle. IV access has been established. She weighs 72 kg. Her most recent bloods from 2 hours ago show: Hb 108 g/L, platelets 95 x 10^9/L, creatinine 88 micromol/L, ALT 125 U/L, urate 0.48 mmol/L.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 175/115 mmHg (confirmed), HR 98 bpm, RR 16, SpO2 96% on room air, Temp 37.1°C, GCS 13 postictal
data_table.Examination: Postictal, drowsy, hyperreflexia, 4 beats ankle clonus, 3+ proteinuria, epigastric tenderness
data_table.Investigations: Hb 108 g/L, Platelets 95 x 10^9/L, Creatinine 88 micromol/L, ALT 125 U/L, Urate 0.48 mmol/L (2 hrs ago)
data_table.Medications: Labetalol 200 mg PO BD for 3 days (last dose 4 hours ago)

lead_in: What is the most appropriate magnesium sulphate regimen for this woman?

options.text: Magnesium sulphate 4 g IV loading over 20 minutes, then 1 g/hour IV for 24 hours after delivery or last seizure
options.rationale: The standard Australian eclampsia regimen is a 4 g IV loading dose over 20 minutes followed by a 1 g/hour maintenance infusion. Therapy is continued for 24 hours after delivery OR 24 hours after the last seizure, whichever occurs later. This is distinct from the pre-eclampsia prophylaxis regimen (which omits the loading dose). (Source: SOMANZ Guideline: Management of Hypertensive Disorders of Pregnancy; KEMH: Eclampsia Guideline)

options.text: Magnesium sulphate 4 g IV loading dose over 5 minutes, then 2 g/hour IV infusion for 24 hours postpartum
options.rationale: 5-minute load plus 2 g/hr is wrong because a 5-minute load risks maternal hypotension and arrest; SOMANZ specifies 20-minute infusion. The 2 g/hr maintenance also exceeds the Australian 1 g/hr standard, increasing magnesium toxicity risk without added benefit. Would never be correct in Australian practice (SOMANZ: Hypertensive Disorders of Pregnancy).

options.text: Magnesium sulphate 4 g IM loading dose into each buttock (8 g total), then 5 g IM 4-hourly for 24 hours
options.rationale: IM Pritchard regimen is wrong because IM-only therapy is painful with unreliable absorption and is rarely used in contemporary Australian tertiary settings with IV access; IV is the standard route. Would be correct only in resource-limited settings without IV infusion capacity (SOMANZ).

options.text: Magnesium sulphate 2 g IV loading dose over 20 minutes, then 1 g/hour IV infusion for 24 hours after the last seizure
options.rationale: 2 g IV load is wrong because 2 g is sub-therapeutic for established eclampsia; the eclampsia regimen requires 4 g loading. Would be correct only for severe pre-eclampsia seizure prophylaxis, not for treating an established seizure (SOMANZ).

options.text: Magnesium sulphate 6 g IV loading dose over 30 minutes, then 1.5 g/hour IV infusion for 48 hours postpartum
options.rationale: 6 g load plus 48 hours is wrong because 6 g exceeds the 4 g standard and 48 hours exceeds the 24-hour standard (24h post-delivery or post-last-seizure, whichever later); both increase toxicity without benefit. Would never be correct in Australian practice (SOMANZ).

explanation.summary: This woman has eclampsia superimposed on severe pre-eclampsia (BP 175/115, thrombocytopenia, elevated ALT, hyperreflexia with clonus). The priority is seizure control with magnesium sulphate using the correct eclampsia treatment regimen: 4 g IV loading dose over 20 minutes followed by 1 g/hour maintenance infusion. This differs from the prophylactic regimen used for severe pre-eclampsia (which omits the loading dose in some protocols). Delivery will follow maternal stabilisation.
key_points: Eclampsia is treated with magnesium sulphate: 4 g IV loading dose over 20 minutes, then 1 g/hour maintenance infusion. (Source: SOMANZ: Hypertensive Disorders of Pregnancy)
Magnesium therapy is continued for 24 hours after delivery OR 24 hours after the last seizure, whichever is LATER. (Source: KEMH: Eclampsia Guideline)

Magnesium toxicity monitoring includes assessment of patellar reflexes (lost at 3.5-5 mmol/L), respiratory rate, urine output, and serum magnesium levels if concerned. (Source: SOMANZ: Hypertensive Disorders of Pregnancy)

The platelet count of 95 x 10^9/L and elevated ALT are features of severe pre-eclampsia with end-organ involvement, which mandates urgent delivery after stabilisation. (Source: SOMANZ: Hypertensive Disorders of Pregnancy)
explanation.pearls: Recurrent seizures despite adequate magnesium therapy occur in approximately 10% of eclampsia cases - treat with a further 2-4 g IV bolus of magnesium sulphate and consider alternative diagnoses (e.g. intracranial haemorrhage) if seizures persist.

stem: A 31-year-old G3P2 at 39+1 weeks gestation has just delivered a 4.2 kg infant vaginally following a labour augmented with oxytocin for 14 hours. The placenta delivered spontaneously 10 minutes later. Her estimated blood loss is now 800 mL and ongoing. The uterus is boggy on palpation and the fundus is above the umbilicus. The accoucheur has commenced uterine fundal massage and the midwife is running an IV infusion of oxytocin 40 units in 1 L of normal saline at 250 mL/hour. The bladder has been emptied with a straight catheter. Despite these measures for 5 minutes, the bleeding continues and the uterus remains atonic. Her current vital signs are HR 112 bpm, BP 100/65 mmHg, RR 20. She has no known drug allergies. Her antenatal haemoglobin 2 weeks ago was 115 g/L. She has a history of asthma well-controlled with salbutamol as needed, with no exacerbations in the past year.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 112 bpm, BP 100/65 mmHg, RR 20, SpO2 98%
data_table.Examination: Uterus boggy, fundus above umbilicus, ongoing moderate-heavy vaginal bleeding, placenta appears complete on inspection
data_table.Investigations: Antenatal Hb 115 g/L (2 weeks prior), current estimated blood loss 800 mL and ongoing
data_table.Medications: Oxytocin 40 U in 1 L normal saline at 250 mL/hr IV; salbutamol PRN for asthma

lead_in: Which SINGLE uterotonic agent should be administered next?

options.text: IM syntometrine 1 mL (oxytocin 5 IU + ergometrine 500 mcg) as a single dose
options.rationale: IM syntometrine is wrong because syntometrine is typically used for third-stage prophylaxis rather than treating established PPH, contains ergometrine (risk of induced hypertension in a woman with normal BP receiving oxytocin), and is suboptimal in asthma. Would be correct only as third-stage prophylaxis in a non-asthmatic, non-hypertensive woman (KEMH PPH; Therapeutic Guidelines: Obstetric Emergencies).

options.text: IM carboprost 250 mcg (Haemabate) every 15 minutes up to a maximum of 8 doses
options.rationale: IM carboprost is wrong because carboprost (PGF2-alpha) is relatively contraindicated in asthma due to bronchospasm risk; even well-controlled asthma makes it a poor choice when ergometrine is available. Would be correct only in non-asthmatic women, particularly after ergometrine fails (Therapeutic Guidelines: Obstetric Emergencies; KEMH PPH).

options.text: IV tranexamic acid 1 g slowly over 10 minutes as a single dose and IV ergometrine 250 mcg slowly
options.rationale: TXA plus ergometrine is wrong because the question asks for a single uterotonic next; TXA is an antifibrinolytic adjunct, not a uterotonic, and is given alongside uterotonics rather than as substitute. Would be correct as combination therapy, not as the single uterotonic choice (WOMAN Trial; KEMH PPH).

options.text: IV ergometrine 250 mcg slowly over 1-2 minutes, repeated once after 5 minutes if needed
options.rationale: Ergometrine is the standard second-line uterotonic in PPH after oxytocin, provided there are no contraindications. The main contraindications are hypertension (current BP normal at 100/65) and cardiac disease. Asthma is a relative contraindication but is not absolute - ergometrine can cause bronchospasm, but in a well-controlled asthmatic with no recent exacerbations, the benefit of controlling life-threatening haemorrhage outweighs this risk. Carboprost would be more problematic in asthma. (Source: KEMH: Postpartum Haemorrhage Guideline; Therapeutic Guidelines: Obstetric Emergencies)

options.text: Misoprostol 800 mcg per rectum as a single dose
options.rationale: Rectal misoprostol is wrong because rectal absorption is slow and inferior to parenteral routes in acute PPH with IV access; misoprostol is reserved for when other uterotonics are contraindicated or unavailable. Would be correct only when IV/IM routes are not accessible or when prior agents are contraindicated (KEMH PPH).

explanation.summary: This woman has a primary PPH due to uterine atony (boggy uterus, fundus above umbilicus, EBL 800 mL and rising). First-line measures - fundal massage, bladder emptying, and IV oxytocin infusion - are in progress but bleeding continues. The next step is a second-line uterotonic. Ergometrine 250 mcg IV is the appropriate choice; her BP is normal (no hypertensive contraindication) and her asthma is well-controlled (relative, not absolute, contraindication). Carboprost would be more dangerous in this asthmatic patient. TXA should be given alongside, not instead of, a uterotonic.
key_points: Second-line uterotonic for PPH when oxytocin has failed is IV ergometrine 250 mcg, repeated once after 5 minutes if needed (maximum 500 mcg). (Source: KEMH: Postpartum Haemorrhage Guideline)

Contraindications to ergometrine: hypertension, pre-eclampsia, cardiac disease, and severe asthma with recent exacerbation. Well-controlled asthma is a relative, not absolute, contraindication. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Obstetric Emergencies)
Tranexamic acid 1 g IV should be given within 3 hours of PPH onset but is an ADJUNCT to, not a replacement for, uterotonic therapy. (Source: WOMAN Trial; KEMH: Postpartum Haemorrhage Guideline)
Carboprost is relatively contraindicated in asthma because prostaglandin F2-alpha causes bronchoconstriction. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Obstetric Emergencies)
explanation.pearls: When PPH is recognised, call for help, initiate a PPH emergency, and remember the '4 Ts' - Tone (atony - most common), Trauma, Tissue (retained products), Thrombin (coagulopathy). Atony alone accounts for approximately 70% of PPH cases.

stem: A 27-year-old G2P1 at 28+3 weeks gestation presents to the emergency department reporting a sudden gush of clear fluid from her vagina approximately 2 hours ago while sitting at her desk. She has since noted ongoing trickling of fluid. She reports no pain, no contractions, and normal foetal movements. Her pregnancy has been uncomplicated to date with a normal morphology scan at 20 weeks. On examination, her temperature is 37.9°C, HR 92 bpm, BP 110/68 mmHg. The abdomen is soft and non-tender, fundal height is 28 cm. On speculum examination, a pool of clear fluid is seen in the posterior fornix with a positive nitrazine test. The cervix is closed and long on visual inspection. Cardiotocography shows a baseline of 150 bpm with moderate variability, no decelerations, and irregular tightenings every 10-12 minutes. Her most recent cervical length at 20 weeks was 42 mm. She received a full course of betamethasone for threatened preterm labour at 26 weeks (which resolved spontaneously).

data_table.Vital signs: Temp 37.9°C, HR 92 bpm, BP 110/68 mmHg, RR 18, SpO2 99%
data_table.Examination: Speculum: pooling of clear fluid, positive nitrazine, cervix closed and long; fundal height 28 cm
data_table.Investigations: CTG baseline 150 bpm, moderate variability, no decelerations, tightenings q10-12 min; cervical length 42 mm at 20 wks
data_table.Medications: Betamethasone course completed at 26 weeks (2 doses 24 hrs apart)

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management plan?

options.text: Admit for inpatient monitoring, administer a rescue course of betamethasone (11.4 mg IM, two doses 24 hours apart), commence oral azithromycin 1 g stat and ampicillin 2 g IV 6-hourly for 48 hours, then amoxicillin 250 mg PO 8-hourly for 5 days
