options.rationale: Azithromycin plus ampicillin-amoxicillin is wrong because Australian first-line PPROM latency cover is erythromycin alone (per ORACLE I); the azithromycin/ampicillin combination is not standard, and the temperature of 37.9 degC needs serial observation rather than tipping straight to extensive antibiotics. Would be correct only when erythromycin is contraindicated or unavailable (RANZCOG: PPROM Guideline; KEMH: PPROM Guideline).

options.text: Induction of labour with intravenous oxytocin, as PPROM at 28+3 weeks with a temperature of 37.9°C mandates delivery within 24 hours
options.rationale: Immediate oxytocin induction is wrong because a borderline temperature of 37.9 degC alone, with no uterine tenderness, reassuring CTG and no purulent discharge, does not diagnose chorioamnionitis or mandate delivery; serial observations and inflammatory markers are required first. Would be correct only with confirmed chorioamnionitis or significant deterioration (RANZCOG: PPROM Guideline; KEMH: PPROM Guideline).

options.text: Admit for conservative management with 4-hourly observations (temperature, HR, BP), twice-weekly CTG, weekly bloods (WCC, CRP), administer a rescue course of betamethasone, commence oral erythromycin 250 mg QID for 10 days, and plan delivery at 34+0 weeks if undelivered
options.rationale: This woman is at 28+3 weeks, PPROM has been confirmed, she is clinically stable with no clear signs of chorioamnionitis. The standard approach is conservative management with close surveillance for chorioamnionitis (4-hourly temp, HR, twice-weekly CTG, weekly inflammatory markers). A rescue course of betamethasone is indicated (prior course >7 days ago). Erythromycin 250 mg QID for 10 days is the recommended latency antibiotic in Australian practice. Delivery is planned at 34+0 weeks if undelivered. The borderline temperature of 37.9°C warrants close observation but is not yet an indication for delivery. (Source: RANZCOG: PPROM Guideline; KEMH: PPROM Guideline)

options.text: Administer a single rescue dose of betamethasone 11.4 mg IM, observe for 4 hours, and discharge home with strict return precautions if the temperature normalises and there are no signs of chorioamnionitis
options.rationale: Single betamethasone and discharge is wrong because outpatient PPROM management is not recommended due to risk of ascending infection, cord prolapse and placental abruption; inpatient monitoring is the standard. Would never be correct as discharge plan (RANZCOG: PPROM Guideline).

options.text: Admit for inpatient monitoring, administer a rescue course of betamethasone, commence IV ampicillin 2 g 6-hourly and oral clarithromycin 500 mg BD for 10 days, and plan delivery at 37+0 weeks if undelivered
options.rationale: Ampicillin/clarithromycin and deliver at 37+0 is wrong because the latency antibiotic regimen should be erythromycin (co-amoxiclav is avoided due to NEC risk per ORACLE I), and delivery is typically planned by 34+0 in Australian practice rather than 37+0. Would be correct only if erythromycin were unavailable and the patient remained stable, with revised antibiotic and delivery timing (KEMH: PPROM Guideline; RANZCOG: PPROM Guideline).

explanation.summary: This woman at 28+3 weeks with confirmed PPROM should be managed conservatively with inpatient monitoring for chorioamnionitis, rescue corticosteroids, and latency antibiotics (erythromycin). The borderline temperature of 37.9°C warrants close observation but is not a delivery indication in isolation. Delivery is planned at 34+0 weeks. The ORACLE trial demonstrated that erythromycin prolongs latency and reduces neonatal morbidity; co-amoxiclav is avoided due to increased NEC risk.
key_points: Conservative management of PPROM <34 weeks: inpatient monitoring, 4-hourly obs (temp, HR), twice-weekly CTG, weekly WCC/CRP. (Source: KEMH: PPROM Guideline)
Rescue course of betamethasone is indicated if >7 days since last course and delivery anticipated <7 days. (Source: RANZCOG: PPROM Guideline)
Latency antibiotics: erythromycin 250 mg QID for 10 days (ORACLE trial). Co-amoxiclav is avoided due to increased NEC risk. (Source: ORACLE Trial; KEMH: PPROM Guideline)
Delivery is planned at 34+0 weeks for PPROM; chorioamnionitis, non-reassuring foetal status, or labour mandate earlier delivery. (Source: RANZCOG: PPROM Guideline)
explanation.pearls: A single borderline temperature does not diagnose chorioamnionitis - look for the triad of maternal fever (>38.0°C) PLUS two or more of: maternal tachycardia, foetal tachycardia, uterine tenderness, purulent or offensive vaginal discharge, or rising WCC/CRP.

stem: A 33-year-old G2P1 at 39+0 weeks presents in spontaneous labour at 4 cm dilated. Her pregnancy has been uncomplicated. Her GBS status is unknown - the screening swab at 36 weeks was not performed because she was travelling interstate. Her previous pregnancy 4 years ago was uncomplicated, and she delivered vaginally at term. Her first infant did not develop GBS disease. On direct questioning, she reports no symptoms of a urinary tract infection during this pregnancy and no history of a positive GBS swab. Her temperature is 36.7°C, and observations are normal. The membranes are intact at present. The CTG is reassuring with a baseline of 140 bpm and normal variability. The woman has no known drug allergies.

data_table.Vital signs: Temp 36.7°C, HR 80 bpm, BP 115/72 mmHg, RR 16
data_table.Examination: Cervix 4 cm dilated, membranes intact, cephalic presentation, station -1
data_table.Investigations: CTG baseline 140 bpm, normal variability, accelerations present; GBS swab at 36 weeks: NOT DONE
data_table.Medications: Nil regular, no known drug allergies

lead_in: What is the most appropriate intrapartum antibiotic prophylaxis plan?

options.text: No intrapartum antibiotic prophylaxis is required; treat only if the infant develops clinical signs of sepsis
options.rationale: No IAP, treat infant if symptomatic is wrong because the purpose of IAP is preventing early-onset GBS disease before clinical signs develop; waiting for infant signs misses the prevention window. Would be correct only with very low risk and confirmed negative GBS (RANZCOG: Group B Streptococcus in Pregnancy).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Group B Streptococcus in Pregnancy

options.text: Benzylpenicillin 1.2 g IV loading dose, then 600 mg IV 4-hourly until delivery, commencing immediately as she is in labour with unknown GBS status
options.rationale: IAP for all unknown-status labour is wrong because IAP for unknown status is reserved for those with risk factors (preterm labour <37, ROM >=18h, temp >=38, prior GBS infant, GBS bacteriuria); this woman currently has none. Would be correct only if risk factors emerged (RANZCOG: Group B Streptococcus in Pregnancy).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Group B Streptococcus in Pregnancy

options.text: Take a low vaginal swab for GBS culture and PCR now, and commence benzylpenicillin 1.2 g IV loading dose only if the swab returns positive or membranes rupture
options.rationale: Swab in labour and treat if positive is wrong because culture results will not be available in time to guide intrapartum decision-making; the anti-pattern is using a too-slow test as a decision tool. Would be correct only as preconception or antenatal screening, not as intrapartum decision-making (RANZCOG: Group B Streptococcus in Pregnancy).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Group B Streptococcus in Pregnancy

options.text: Monitor for the development of clinical risk factors - if membranes rupture for ≥18 hours or maternal temperature reaches ≥38.0°C, commence benzylpenicillin 1.2 g IV loading dose then 600 mg IV 4-hourly
options.rationale: For women with unknown GBS status at term, IAP is indicated ONLY if clinical risk factors develop: ROM ≥18 hours, maternal temperature ≥38.0°C, preterm labour <37 weeks, GBS bacteriuria in current pregnancy, or previous infant with GBS disease. This woman currently has none of these, so the plan is expectant management with monitoring for risk factors. (Source: RANZCOG: Group B Streptococcus in Pregnancy; KEMH: GBS Guideline)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Group B Streptococcus in Pregnancy

options.text: Cefazolin 2 g IV loading dose then 1 g IV 8-hourly as the preferred antibiotic for unknown GBS status with no risk factors
options.rationale: Cefazolin for unknown status with no risk factors is wrong because cefazolin is reserved for non-severe penicillin allergy with confirmed indication for IAP; she has no allergies and no current indication for IAP. Would be correct only with non-severe penicillin allergy and an indication for IAP (RANZCOG: Group B Streptococcus in Pregnancy).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Group B Streptococcus in Pregnancy

explanation.summary: This term woman with unknown GBS status and no clinical risk factors does not require immediate IAP. The standard Australian approach is to monitor for the development of risk factors during labour: ROM ≥18 hours, intrapartum fever ≥38.0°C, or preterm birth. If none develop, IAP is not indicated. This differs from some international approaches that recommend universal IAP for unknown GBS status, but Australian guidance uses a risk-factor-based approach for women whose screening status is unknown.
key_points: IAP is indicated for known GBS-positive women AND for unknown-GBS women with clinical risk factors: ROM ≥18 hrs, temp ≥38.0°C, <37 wks, GBS bacteriuria, previous infant with GBS disease. (Source: RANZCOG: Group B Streptococcus in Pregnancy)
For unknown GBS status at term with NO risk factors, IAP is not required. (Source: KEMH: GBS Guideline)
The standard IAP regimen is benzylpenicillin 1.2 g IV loading dose then 600 mg IV 4-hourly until delivery. (Source: RANZCOG: Group B Streptococcus in Pregnancy)
Clindamycin is used for women with severe penicillin allergy only if GBS sensitivities are known; vancomycin is used if sensitivities are unknown. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)
explanation.pearls: IAP is considered adequate if the loading dose is given at least 4 hours before delivery. Document the time of first dose clearly to guide neonatal management.

sources.label: RANZCOG: Group B Streptococcus in Pregnancy

stem: A 29-year-old primigravida at 29+5 weeks gestation is being managed as an inpatient for early-onset foetal growth restriction. Her pregnancy is otherwise uncomplicated with a normal morphology scan at 20 weeks. Serial growth ultrasounds have shown an estimated foetal weight consistently below the 3rd centile since 24 weeks with a falling growth velocity. Umbilical artery Doppler studies today show absent end-diastolic flow. Previous Dopplers 2 weeks ago showed a raised umbilical artery pulsatility index (>95th centile) but positive end-diastolic flow. Middle cerebral artery Doppler today shows a low pulsatility index consistent with brain-sparing. The ductus venosus Doppler shows a positive a-wave. The amniotic fluid index is 7.5 cm (borderline oligohydramnios). CTG today shows a baseline of 145 bpm with reduced variability (3-5 bpm) and occasional spontaneous accelerations, but no decelerations. The woman has received a full course of betamethasone (two doses 24 hours apart) completed yesterday. Her blood pressure is 120/78 mmHg, and urinalysis shows no protein.

data_table.Vital signs: Maternal BP 120/78 mmHg, HR 76 bpm, RR 16, Temp 36.6°C
data_table.Examination: No protein on urinalysis, fundal height 25 cm (consistent with FGR)
data_table.Investigations: UA Doppler: absent end-diastolic flow (AEDF); MCA PI low (brain-sparing); DV a-wave positive; AFI 7.5 cm; CTG: baseline 145 bpm, reduced variability (3-5 bpm), no decelerations; EFW <3rd centile

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Commence IV magnesium sulphate 4 g loading dose for foetal neuroprotection and plan for delivery within 24 hours given the AEDF and reduced CTG variability
options.rationale: Delivery within 24 hours is wrong because while delivery is indicated, the 24-48 hour timeframe is appropriate when DV a-wave is positive and there are no CTG decelerations; insisting on within 24 hours overstates urgency. Would be correct only if DV a-wave were abnormal or recurrent decelerations developed (RANZCOG: Foetal Growth Restriction Guideline; KEMH).

options.text: Commence IV magnesium sulphate 4 g loading dose over 20 minutes followed by 1 g/hour maintenance, and plan delivery within 24-48 hours with continuous intrapartum CTG monitoring
options.rationale: In FGR at 29+5 weeks with AEDF, brain-sparing, borderline AFI, and reduced CTG variability (but no decelerations and DV a-wave positive), delivery is indicated within 24-48 hours. Magnesium sulphate for foetal neuroprotection (4 g IV loading dose over 20 minutes, then 1 g/hour maintenance) should be commenced, given the gestation is <30 weeks and delivery is imminent. The positive DV a-wave and absence of CTG decelerations allow for this timeframe without requiring immediate emergency delivery. (Source: RANZCOG: Foetal Growth Restriction Guideline; KEMH: FGR Guideline)

options.text: Perform immediate caesarean section as the AEDF and reduced CTG variability indicate acute-on-chronic foetal compromise requiring delivery within 2-4 hours
options.rationale: Immediate caesarean within 2-4 hours is wrong because AEDF with reduced variability but positive DV a-wave does not meet criteria for emergency category-1 delivery; immediate delivery is for recurrent decelerations, reversed end-diastolic flow or abnormal DV a-wave. Would be correct only with abnormal DV a-wave or recurrent decelerations (RANZCOG: FGR Guideline).

options.text: Perform a repeat CTG in 12 hours and only plan delivery if the CTG deteriorates to show recurrent decelerations or the ductus venosus a-wave becomes absent or reversed
options.rationale: Repeat CTG in 12 hours is wrong because AEDF plus reduced variability is itself an indication for delivery; expectant management beyond 48 hours after these features develop is unsafe. Would be correct only at earlier surveillance phases before AEDF develops (RANZCOG: FGR Guideline).

options.text: Administer a rescue course of betamethasone (two further doses 24 hours apart) and plan for delivery at 32+0 weeks, repeating umbilical artery Dopplers twice weekly
options.rationale: Rescue steroids and delay to 32 weeks is wrong because the prior course was only the day before, so a rescue course is not indicated; furthermore, AEDF plus reduced variability mandates delivery within 24-48 hours, not delay to 32 weeks. Would be correct only if >7 days had elapsed since last course AND foetal surveillance were reassuring (RANZCOG: FGR Guideline).

explanation.summary: This woman at 29+5 weeks has FGR with AEDF, brain-sparing, borderline AFI, and reduced CTG variability. The combination warrants delivery within 24-48 hours. The positive DV a-wave and absence of CTG decelerations allow this window rather than mandating immediate delivery. Magnesium sulphate for foetal neuroprotection (<30 weeks, delivery imminent) should be commenced. Corticosteroids have been given and do not need repeating. The key decision is timing: AEDF + reduced variability = deliver within 24-48 hours; AEDF + abnormal DV or decelerations = deliver within hours.
key_points: AEDF on UA Doppler in FGR indicates delivery within 24-48 hours if CTG is non-reassuring but without decelerations and DV a-wave remains positive. (Source: RANZCOG: Foetal Growth Restriction Guideline)

Magnesium sulphate 4 g IV loading dose over 20 minutes for foetal neuroprotection is indicated when delivery is planned <30 weeks gestation. (Source: KEMH: Magnesium Sulphate for Neuroprotection Guideline)

Immediate delivery (Category 1 CS) is indicated for AEDF with recurrent CTG decelerations, reversed EDF, or abnormal DV Doppler (absent/reversed a-wave). (Source: RANZCOG: Foetal Growth Restriction Guideline)
A rescue course of corticosteroids is indicated if >7 days since the last course AND delivery is anticipated within the next 7 days. (Source: RANZCOG: Antenatal Corticosteroids Guideline)
explanation.pearls: The ductus venosus a-wave is the last Doppler parameter to become abnormal in FGR deterioration - a positive a-wave is reassuring but does not exclude significant compromise. It is the 'canary in the coal mine' for foetal acid-base status.

sources.label: KEMH: Magnesium Sulphate for Neuroprotection Guideline

stem: A 30-year-old G3P1 at 34+2 weeks gestation presents with a sudden small amount of dark red vaginal bleeding that began immediately after she felt a 'popping' sensation and a gush of fluid 30 minutes ago. She reports no pain. Her pregnancy was complicated by a low-lying placenta noted at the 20-week morphology scan, which was reported to have cleared the os by the 32-week follow-up scan. A velamentous cord insertion was noted on the 32-week scan but vasa praevia was not specifically reported. On arrival, she is haemodynamically stable with HR 88 bpm and BP 120/75 mmHg. Speculum examination shows dark blood in the vagina with ongoing oozing. A bedside cardiotocograph is performed immediately and shows a foetal heart rate baseline of 120 bpm, reduced variability, and a sinusoidal-like pattern with regular shallow oscillations. There are no accelerations and no decelerations. The estimated blood loss is approximately 100 mL.

data_table.Vital signs: Maternal HR 88 bpm, BP 120/75 mmHg, RR 18, Temp 36.5°C, SpO2 98%
data_table.Examination: Speculum: dark blood in vagina, ongoing oozing; digital examination deferred; abdomen soft non-tender
data_table.Investigations: CTG: baseline 120 bpm, reduced variability, sinusoidal-like pattern; 32-week scan: low-lying placenta cleared, velamentous cord insertion noted; EBL ~100 mL

lead_in: What is the most likely diagnosis underlying the CTG pattern?

options.text: Placental abruption causing foetal hypoxia and compensatory foetal heart rate pattern
options.rationale: Placental abruption is wrong because abruption presents with painful bleeding, uterine tenderness and a hypertonic uterus; this woman has painless bleeding after ROM in the context of velamentous cord insertion with a sinusoidal CTG, fitting vasa praevia. Would be correct only with the painful-bleeding-and-woody-uterus triad (RANZCOG: Antepartum Haemorrhage Guideline; KEMH).

options.text: Placenta praevia with a recurrent low-lying placenta causing marginal sinus bleeding and foetal bradycardia
options.rationale: Placenta praevia is wrong because the 32-week scan confirmed placental clearance of the os; recurrent praevia is unlikely, and praevia bleeding is maternal not foetal (would not cause a sinusoidal CTG unless massive maternal haemorrhage caused hypotension, which is absent here). Would be correct only with confirmed low-lying placenta and maternal hypotension (RANZCOG: Antepartum Haemorrhage Guideline).

options.text: Vasa praevia with rupture of a foetal vessel traversing the membranes overlying the internal os
options.rationale: The triad of painless vaginal bleeding following membrane rupture, a known velamentous cord insertion, and a sinusoidal CTG pattern is pathognomonic for vasa praevia with foetal vessel rupture. The blood loss is small (100 mL) because it represents foetal haemorrhage - a foetus has a total blood volume of only approximately 80 mL/kg, so even 100 mL is catastrophic. The sinusoidal CTG pattern reflects severe foetal anaemia. (Source: RANZCOG: Vasa Praevia Guideline; KEMH: Vasa Praevia Guideline)

options.text: Uterine rupture with extrusion of the foetus into the peritoneal cavity and severe foetal distress
options.rationale: Uterine rupture is wrong because rupture causes acute severe abdominal pain, loss of presenting part, maternal haemodynamic instability and often catastrophic CTG change; this woman is pain-free, stable, with intact uterus. Would be correct only with the rupture triad and prior uterine surgery (RANZCOG: Uterine Rupture Guideline).

options.text: Foetal-maternal haemorrhage due to a placental lake rupture during membrane rupture
options.rationale: Feto-maternal haemorrhage from placental lake rupture is wrong because FMH typically presents with persistent foetal bradycardia or sinusoidal pattern WITHOUT visible vaginal bleeding (foetal blood enters maternal circulation); visible bleeding after ROM with velamentous cord insertion points strongly to vasa praevia. Would be correct only if there were no visible vaginal bleeding with foetal anaemia (RANZCOG: Antepartum Haemorrhage Guideline).

explanation.summary: This woman has the classic presentation of vasa praevia: painless vaginal bleeding immediately following membrane rupture, a known velamentous cord insertion (a major risk factor), and a sinusoidal CTG pattern indicating severe foetal anaemia. The blood loss is small because it represents foetal, not maternal, haemorrhage - a foetus at 34 weeks has a total blood volume of only approximately 250 mL, so even 100 mL is life-threatening. Immediate caesarean delivery is indicated.
key_points: Vasa praevia occurs when foetal vessels run across the internal os within the membranes, at risk of rupture at ROM. (Source: RANZCOG: Vasa Praevia Guideline)
A sinusoidal CTG pattern is a classic sign of severe foetal anaemia and requires immediate delivery. (Source: RANZCOG: Intrapartum Foetal Surveillance)

The key risk factor is velamentous cord insertion, especially with a low-lying placenta in the second trimester; these pregnancies should have a targeted scan for vasa praevia. (Source: KEMH: Vasa Praevia Guideline)
Management is immediate caesarean section - foetal survival depends on prompt recognition and delivery. (Source: RANZCOG: Vasa Praevia Guideline)
explanation.pearls: A sinusoidal pattern on CTG is an obstetric 'code blue' - call for immediate help, prepare for emergency CS, and alert neonatal team for foetal resuscitation.

stem: A 28-year-old G3P1 at approximately 6+2 weeks gestation by LMP presents to the early pregnancy assessment service with 4 days of mild left iliac fossa pain and spotting. She is haemodynamically stable with HR 78 bpm and BP 110/68 mmHg. Transvaginal ultrasound shows an empty uterus with an endometrial thickness of 8 mm. The left adnexa demonstrates a 2.4 cm mass with a hyperechoic ring but no visible yolk sac or foetal pole. There is no free fluid in the pouch of Douglas. Her serum β-hCG is 2100 IU/L, and a repeat 48 hours later is 2850 IU/L (a 36% rise). Her full blood count, renal function, and liver function tests are normal.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 78 bpm, BP 110/68 mmHg, RR 16, Temp 36.4°C
data_table.Examination: Mild left adnexal tenderness, no cervical motion tenderness, no peritoneal signs
data_table.Investigations: TVUS: empty uterus, left adnexal mass 2.4 cm with hyperechoic ring, no yolk sac/foetal pole, no free fluid; β-hCG 2100 → 2850 (48 hr, 36% rise); FBC, UEC, LFTs normal

lead_in: Which of the following findings would make this woman UNSUITABLE for medical management with single-dose methotrexate?

options.text: An adnexal mass measuring 2.4 cm on transvaginal ultrasound
options.rationale: Adnexal mass 2.4 cm is wrong because mass size <3.5-4 cm is a criterion supporting methotrexate (not excluding it); 2.4 cm is well within the acceptable size range. Would be correct only if mass were >=4 cm (RANZCOG: Diagnosis and Management of Ectopic Pregnancy; KEMH).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Diagnosis and Management of Ectopic Pregnancy

options.text: A serum β-hCG of 2850 IU/L with a suboptimal rise of 36% over 48 hours
options.rationale: Beta-hCG 2850 with suboptimal rise is wrong because beta-hCG <5000 is a criterion for methotrexate; suboptimal rise confirms non-viable pregnancy and supports rather than excludes medical management. Would be correct only with beta-hCG >5000 or rapidly rising (RANZCOG).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Diagnosis and Management of Ectopic Pregnancy

options.text: The presence of a hyperechoic ring around the adnexal mass without a visible yolk sac
options.rationale: Tubal ring without yolk sac is wrong because that ultrasound appearance is classic tubal ectopic ('bagel sign'); absence of yolk sac/foetal pole is common and absence of cardiac activity is in fact a positive predictor for methotrexate success. Would be correct only if a viable foetal pole with cardiac activity were present (KEMH).

options.text: A normal platelet count, normal serum creatinine, and normal liver transaminases
options.rationale: Normal renal/hepatic/platelets is wrong because normal blood count, creatinine and transaminases are prerequisites for methotrexate (it is renally excreted and hepatically metabolised); they support, not exclude, medical management. Would be correct only if abnormal (Therapeutic Guidelines: Ectopic Pregnancy; AMH: Methotrexate).

options.text: The presence of embryonic cardiac activity within the ectopic gestation on ultrasound
options.rationale: The presence of foetal cardiac activity is a contraindication to medical management with methotrexate due to a significantly higher failure rate. Cardiac activity indicates a more established ectopic gestation that is less likely to respond to methotrexate, and surgical management (salpingectomy) is generally recommended. (Source: RANZCOG: Diagnosis and Management of Ectopic Pregnancy; KEMH: Ectopic Pregnancy Guideline)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Diagnosis and Management of Ectopic Pregnancy

explanation.summary: This woman has a probable tubal ectopic pregnancy based on empty uterus, adnexal mass with tubal ring sign, and suboptimal β-hCG rise. She meets most criteria for single-dose methotrexate (haemodynamic stability, adnexal mass <3.5 cm, β-hCG <5000 IU/L, no free fluid, normal organ function, no contraindications). However, foetal cardiac activity is a known contraindication due to high failure rate with medical management. The question tests recognition of exclusion criteria.
key_points: Criteria for methotrexate therapy: haemodynamic stability, unruptured ectopic, adnexal mass <3.5-4 cm, β-hCG <5000 IU/L, no foetal cardiac activity, no free fluid, and normal renal/hepatic/haematological function. (Source: RANZCOG: Diagnosis and Management of Ectopic Pregnancy)
Presence of foetal cardiac activity is an absolute contraindication to methotrexate due to high failure rates; surgical management is indicated. (Source: KEMH: Ectopic Pregnancy Guideline)

Suboptimal β-hCG rise (less than 66% in 48 hours) supports a non-viable pregnancy and is often seen in ectopic gestation; it does not preclude medical therapy. (Source: RANZCOG: Diagnosis and Management of Ectopic Pregnancy)
Single-dose methotrexate 50 mg/m² IM is the standard regimen; follow-up β-hCG at days 4 and 7 to confirm ≥15% decline between days 4 and 7. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Ectopic Pregnancy)
explanation.pearls: The five contraindications to methotrexate for ectopic pregnancy can be remembered as 'HEART': Haemodynamic instability, Embryonic cardiac activity, Adnexal mass >4 cm, Renal/hepatic dysfunction, Too-high β-hCG (>5000).

sources.label: RANZCOG: Diagnosis and Management of Ectopic Pregnancy

stem: A 32-year-old nulligravid woman presents to the emergency department with progressive abdominal distension, bloating, and reduced urine output over the past 3 days. She underwent oocyte retrieval for IVF 8 days ago after ovarian stimulation with a GnRH antagonist protocol and a trigger injection of hCG. On examination, her abdomen is distended, tense, and diffusely tender without peritonism. A fluid thrill is present. Her weight today is 68 kg, up from a pre-cycle weight of 62 kg. Her HR is 102 bpm, BP 95/60 mmHg, RR 22, SpO2 96% on room air. Urine output over the past 6 hours has been 120 mL (20 mL/hr). A transvaginal ultrasound shows bilateral enlarged ovaries measuring 10 cm and 11 cm with multiple cysts, and a moderate amount of ascites. Her haemoglobin is 165 g/L, haematocrit 0.49, WCC 16.5 x 10^9/L, serum creatinine 105 micromol/L (pre-cycle 62 micromol/L), ALT 55 U/L.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 102 bpm, BP 95/60 mmHg, RR 22, SpO2 96% RA, Temp 36.8°C
data_table.Examination: Abdomen distended, tense, diffuse tenderness, fluid thrill present; weight gain 6 kg over 8 days
data_table.Investigations: TVUS: ovaries 10 cm and 11 cm, multiple cysts, moderate ascites; Hb 165 g/L, Hct 0.49, WCC 16.5, Creatinine 105 (from 62), ALT 55, Coags normal; urine output 20 mL/hr
data_table.Medications: IVF stimulation cycle with GnRH antagonist protocol; hCG trigger 8 days ago

lead_in: What is the most appropriate NEXT step in management?

options.text: Commence IV fluid resuscitation with a balanced crystalloid at 1-2 mL/kg/hr, insert an indwelling catheter for strict fluid balance, commence prophylactic dose low molecular weight heparin, and admit under the gynaecology team for daily assessment
options.rationale: IV fluid resuscitation plus prophylactic LMWH plus daily ward review is wrong because oliguria despite 6 kg weight gain, haemoconcentration and tense abdomen indicate moderate-to-severe OHSS requiring paracentesis for tense ascites and improved renal perfusion; supportive measures alone miss the volume-shift problem. Would be correct only in mild-moderate OHSS without tense ascites or renal impairment (RANZCOG: OHSS; KEMH: OHSS Guideline).

options.text: Perform an ultrasound-guided paracentesis to drain ascites under strict aseptic technique, commence IV fluid resuscitation cautiously, insert an indwelling catheter, and administer prophylactic dose low molecular weight heparin
options.rationale: This woman has moderate-to-severe OHSS with significant ascites causing abdominal distension, dyspnoea, and likely contributing to renal impairment (oliguria, elevated creatinine) through abdominal compartment syndrome. Paracentesis is indicated for symptomatic relief and to improve renal perfusion. IV fluids should be given cautiously to avoid worsening third-space fluid accumulation, and thromboprophylaxis with LMWH is mandatory given the hypercoagulable state of OHSS. (Source: RANZCOG: Ovarian Hyperstimulation Syndrome; KEMH: OHSS Guideline)

options.text: Administer therapeutic dose low molecular weight heparin, as the haemoconcentration and oliguria indicate severe OHSS with high thrombotic risk requiring full anticoagulation
options.rationale: Therapeutic LMWH is wrong because while OHSS is hypercoagulable, therapeutic anticoagulation is reserved for clinical thromboembolism; prophylactic dosing is standard for moderate-severe OHSS and therapeutic dose adds bleeding risk after oocyte retrieval. Would be correct only with confirmed VTE (RANZCOG: OHSS).

options.text: Discharge home on oral cabergoline 0.5 mg daily for 8 days, with strict instructions for daily weight monitoring, fluid intake of 3 L/day, and return if symptoms worsen
options.rationale: Discharge on cabergoline is wrong because cabergoline is a PREVENTIVE agent for OHSS in high-risk women, not a treatment for established moderate-severe OHSS with oliguria and ascites; the anti-pattern is treating established disease with prevention measures. Would be correct only as antenatal OHSS prophylaxis in high-risk IVF stimulation (RANZCOG: OHSS).

options.text: Perform a diagnostic laparoscopy to exclude ovarian torsion, given the bilateral adnexal enlargement and sudden onset of abdominal distension and pain
options.rationale: Diagnostic laparoscopy for torsion is wrong because bilateral enlargement with ascites and systemic signs is classic OHSS, not torsion; surgical intervention in friable hyperstimulated ovaries with coagulopathy risk would be hazardous. Would be correct only with acute severe unilateral pain plus Doppler evidence of torsion (KEMH: OHSS Guideline).

explanation.summary: This woman has moderate-to-severe early-onset OHSS post-IVF, characterised by haemoconcentration (Hb 165, Hct 0.49), renal impairment (creatinine rise, oliguria), ascites, and enlarged ovaries. The key interventions are paracentesis to relieve abdominal compartment syndrome and improve renal perfusion, cautious IV fluid resuscitation (avoiding aggressive hydration which can worsen third-spacing), strict fluid balance monitoring, and prophylactic LMWH for thromboembolism prevention. Cabergoline is preventative, not therapeutic, and laparoscopy is not indicated without signs of torsion.
key_points: OHSS is classified by severity: mild (abdominal bloating, ovarian size 5-12 cm), moderate (nausea, vomiting, ascites on ultrasound), severe (clinical ascites, oliguria, haemoconcentration Hct >0.45, creatinine >150, liver dysfunction). (Source: RANZCOG: Ovarian Hyperstimulation Syndrome)

Paracentesis is indicated for tense ascites causing respiratory compromise, oliguria, or significant pain. It provides rapid symptomatic relief and can improve renal function. (Source: KEMH: OHSS Guideline)
Thromboprophylaxis with prophylactic dose LMWH is mandatory for all inpatients with moderate-severe OHSS due to the hypercoagulable state. (Source: RANZCOG: Ovarian Hyperstimulation Syndrome)
IV fluids should be administered cautiously (strict input-output) as aggressive hydration can worsen third-space fluid accumulation. (Source: KEMH: OHSS Guideline)
explanation.pearls: In OHSS, the haematocrit is your most important guide: a rising Hct indicates worsening haemoconcentration and is a warning sign of severe disease, while a falling Hct with clinical improvement signals resolution. Oliguria despite adequate hydration = time for paracentesis.

stem: A 36-year-old G2P1 at 29+0 weeks gestation was diagnosed with gestational diabetes at 26 weeks on a 75 g OGTT (fasting 5.4 mmol/L, 1-hour 10.5 mmol/L, 2-hour 8.9 mmol/L). Her BMI is 32 kg/m². She has been managed with lifestyle modification (diet and exercise) for 3 weeks. Her fasting BGLs have consistently been 5.0-5.3 mmol/L (target <5.1 mmol/L) and her 2-hour postprandial BGLs are 6.2-6.6 mmol/L (target <6.7 mmol/L). An ultrasound at 28 weeks showed an estimated foetal weight on the 78th centile with a normal amniotic fluid index. She asks about medication options to improve her fasting glucose. She has no contraindications to metformin or insulin. She is concerned about the risk of needing insulin and the need for injections.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 124/76 mmHg, HR 82 bpm, fundal height 30 cm
data_table.Examination: Normal examination; no signs of macrosomia clinically
data_table.Investigations: OGTT: fasting 5.4, 1h 10.5, 2h 8.9 (all diagnostic); fasting BGLs 5.0-5.3 (target <5.1), 2h postprandial 6.2-6.6 (target <6.7); EFW 78th centile, AFI normal
data_table.Medications: Nil pharmacological; lifestyle measures for 3 weeks

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in managing her fasting hyperglycaemia?

options.text: Commence metformin 500 mg orally at night, titrate up to 1500 mg daily as tolerated, and continue diet and exercise measures
options.rationale: This woman has fasting BGLs consistently above the ADIPS target of <5.1 mmol/L despite 3 weeks of lifestyle modification. Metformin is an appropriate first-line pharmacological agent in Australian practice for GDM when lifestyle measures are insufficient. It is particularly effective for fasting hyperglycaemia by reducing hepatic gluconeogenesis. It is initiated at 500 mg nocte and titrated up. Insulin remains an option if metformin is not tolerated or fails to achieve targets. (Source: ADIPS: Gestational Diabetes Mellitus Guideline; RANZCOG: Gestational Diabetes)

options.text: Commence basal insulin (isophane or insulin detemir) at 0.1 units/kg at night, titrated according to fasting BGL, and cease metformin
options.rationale: Basal insulin first-line is wrong because ADIPS supports metformin as first-line oral pharmacotherapy when lifestyle fails, before moving to insulin; insulin is second-line or used alongside metformin if targets are not met. Would be correct only if metformin were contraindicated or refused (ADIPS: GDM Guideline).

options.text: Continue lifestyle measures alone for a further 2 weeks, as the fasting BGLs are only marginally above target and postprandial targets are met
options.rationale: Continue lifestyle 2 more weeks is wrong because ADIPS recommends pharmacotherapy if targets are not achieved within 1-2 weeks of lifestyle; she has had elevated fasting BGLs for 3 weeks. Would be correct only at the 1-2 week mark with marginal elevation (ADIPS: GDM Guideline).

options.text: Commence glibenclamide (glyburide) 2.5 mg orally at night, titrating to a maximum of 20 mg daily
options.rationale: Glibenclamide is wrong because it is not recommended first-line in Australia: it crosses the placenta and is associated with higher rates of macrosomia and neonatal hypoglycaemia compared to metformin or insulin. Would be correct only in very limited resource-poor settings, not Australian practice (ADIPS: GDM Guideline; RANZCOG).

options.text: Commence both metformin 500 mg BD and basal insulin 0.2 units/kg at night simultaneously to rapidly achieve glycaemic control
options.rationale: Metformin plus insulin simultaneously is wrong because dual therapy as initial pharmacological step is not indicated; metformin alone should be trialled first with insulin added only if targets remain unmet after dose escalation. Would be correct only with severe baseline derangement requiring rapid control (ADIPS: GDM Guideline).

explanation.summary: This woman has GDM diagnosed on all three OGTT time points and has fasting hyperglycaemia (target <5.1 mmol/L, her values 5.0-5.3) persisting after 3 weeks of lifestyle modification. Postprandial targets are achieved. The next step is to add metformin, which is the recommended first-line pharmacological agent in Australian guidelines for GDM. Metformin is effective for fasting hyperglycaemia, is well-tolerated, and has a good safety profile. Insulin remains the second-line option if metformin fails.
key_points: ADIPS GDM glycaemic targets: fasting <5.1 mmol/L, 1-hour postprandial <7.8 mmol/L, 2-hour postprandial <6.7 mmol/L. (Source: ADIPS: Gestational Diabetes Mellitus Guideline)
Pharmacological therapy is indicated if targets are not met within 1-2 weeks of lifestyle modification. (Source: ADIPS: Gestational Diabetes Mellitus Guideline)

Metformin is the first-line pharmacological agent for GDM in Australia, particularly effective for fasting hyperglycaemia. Start 500 mg nocte, titrate to maximum 2 g daily. (Source: RANZCOG: Gestational Diabetes)
Glibenclamide is not recommended as first-line due to higher rates of adverse neonatal outcomes; insulin is second-line after metformin. (Source: ADIPS: Gestational Diabetes Mellitus Guideline)
explanation.pearls: Metformin crosses the placenta but extensive data show no increase in congenital anomalies or short-term adverse neonatal outcomes. It is category C in Australia but widely used and recommended by ADIPS for GDM.

stem: A 35-year-old G4P3 at 34+5 weeks gestation presents to the birth suite with sudden onset of dark red vaginal bleeding associated with severe continuous lower abdominal pain that began 45 minutes ago. She reports that the pain started abruptly while she was resting and has not subsided. On examination, she appears distressed and in significant pain. Her abdomen is tense and tender with a woody-hard feel on palpation. The uterus is contracting every 2 minutes with no palpable relaxation between contractions. The foetal heart rate is auscultated at 110 bpm. Vaginal examination is deferred. Speculum examination shows dark blood pooling in the posterior fornix with an estimated blood loss of 250 mL. Her HR is 108 bpm and BP is 150/95 mmHg. An urgent bedside ultrasound shows a posterior placenta clear of the internal os, no retroplacental clot visualised, and a foetus in cephalic presentation with a normal amniotic fluid index. The CTG shows a baseline of 110 bpm with reduced variability, no accelerations, and late decelerations following each contraction.

data_table.Vital signs: Maternal HR 108 bpm, BP 150/95 mmHg, RR 22, SpO2 97%, Temp 36.5°C
data_table.Examination: Abdomen tense, woody-hard, tender, contracting q2min no relaxation; foetal HR 110 bpm; speculum: dark blood pooling, EBL 250 mL
data_table.Investigations: Bedside USS: posterior placenta clear of os, no retroplacental clot seen, AFI normal; CTG baseline 110 bpm, reduced variability, late decelerations

lead_in: Which finding in this presentation most strongly favours a diagnosis of placental abruption over placenta praevia?

options.text: Dark red vaginal bleeding rather than bright red bleeding, as dark blood indicates concealed haemorrhage characteristic of abruption
options.rationale: Dark vs bright red blood is wrong because blood colour can occur in both abruption and praevia (older blood appears darker in either condition); colour alone is not a reliable discriminator. Would never be a primary discriminator (RANZCOG: Antepartum Haemorrhage Guideline).

options.text: A tense, woody-hard uterus with continuous pain and hypertonic contractions that do not relax between tightenings
options.rationale: The combination of a tense, woody-hard (Couvelaire) uterus, continuous severe pain, and hypertonic uterine contractions is classic for significant placental abruption. In placenta praevia, the uterus is typically soft and non-tender between contractions, and pain is usually absent unless labour is established. The hypertonic pattern reflects irritability from blood extravasation into the myometrium. (Source: RANZCOG: Antepartum Haemorrhage Guideline; KEMH: Antepartum Haemorrhage Guideline)

options.text: A history of chronic hypertension and smoking, which are established risk factors for abnormal placentation and low-lying placenta
options.rationale: Hypertension and smoking risk factors is wrong because both are risk factors for abruption AND praevia (smoking is also a praevia risk factor); risk factors do not differentiate the acute diagnosis as strongly as clinical signs do. Would be correct only as supporting evidence, not the strongest differentiator (RANZCOG: Antepartum Haemorrhage Guideline).

options.text: The CTG showing foetal heart rate baseline of 110 bpm with reduced variability and late decelerations following each contraction
options.rationale: Pathological CTG is wrong because pathological tracings occur in both conditions (abruption from placental separation, praevia from maternal hypotension or cord compression); CTG abnormality indicates compromise but not cause. Would never be a primary differentiator (RANZCOG: Intrapartum Foetal Surveillance).

options.text: Ultrasound demonstrating a posterior placenta that is clear of the internal os with no retroplacental clot visualised
options.rationale: Ultrasound showing placenta clear of os is wrong because while it excludes praevia, it does not confirm abruption (ultrasound is negative in up to 50% of abruptions); the question asks for a finding that favours abruption, not one that excludes praevia. Would be correct only as a praevia rule-out (RANZCOG: Antepartum Haemorrhage Guideline).

explanation.summary: This woman presents with classic features of a significant placental abruption: sudden-onset severe continuous pain, a tense woody-hard (Couvelaire) uterus, hypertonic contractions without relaxation, and dark vaginal bleeding. Risk factors include chronic hypertension and smoking. Although ultrasound shows the placenta clear of the os (excluding praevia), up to 50% of retroplacental clots are not visualised on ultrasound. The key discriminator between abruption and praevia is the quality of the pain and uterine tone - abruption causes continuous pain with a tense, tender uterus, while praevia is typically painless with a soft, non-tender uterus.
key_points: Placental abruption presents with PAINFUL vaginal bleeding, a tense/tender uterus, and hypertonic contractions; placenta praevia presents with PAINLESS bleeding and a soft non-tender uterus. (Source: RANZCOG: Antepartum Haemorrhage Guideline)
A normal ultrasound does NOT exclude abruption - retroplacental clots may be isodense with placental tissue and missed in up to 50% of cases. (Source: KEMH: Antepartum Haemorrhage Guideline)

The Couvelaire uterus (woody-hard, discoloured) results from blood extravasation into the myometrium and is a sign of significant concealed abruption. (Source: RANZCOG: Antepartum Haemorrhage Guideline)

Late decelerations with reduced variability in the setting of abruption indicate uteroplacental insufficiency and may warrant expedited delivery. (Source: RANZCOG: Intrapartum Foetal Surveillance Guideline)
explanation.pearls: Never perform a digital vaginal examination in undiagnosed APH until placenta praevia is excluded by ultrasound - a digit through a praevia can provoke catastrophic haemorrhage. Speculum examination is safe.

stem: A 31-year-old primigravida at 36+6 weeks gestation is admitted to the antenatal ward with new-onset hypertension. She has been well throughout her pregnancy until 3 days ago when she noted mild ankle swelling and a frontal headache. Her blood pressure on arrival is 155/105 mmHg, confirmed on repeat measurement 30 minutes later. Urinalysis shows 2+ protein on dipstick with a spot urine protein-to-creatinine ratio of 45 mg/mmol (normal <30). She reports no visual disturbances, no epigastric pain, and no nausea. On examination, she has mild pitting ankle oedema, normal reflexes with no clonus, and a soft non-tender abdomen. The foetal CTG is reactive with a baseline of 145 bpm, normal variability, and accelerations. Ultrasound shows an appropriately grown foetus on the 55th centile with a normal amniotic fluid index and normal umbilical artery Dopplers. Her blood results show haemoglobin 118 g/L, platelets 195 x 10^9/L, creatinine 62 micromol/L, ALT 28 U/L, and urate 0.36 mmol/L. She has been commenced on oral labetalol 200 mg BD, and her blood pressure has improved to 140/90 mmHg after 24 hours.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 155/105 (confirmed, now 140/90 on labetalol), HR 82 bpm, RR 16, SpO2 98%, Temp 36.6°C
data_table.Examination: Mild pitting ankle oedema, normal reflexes, no clonus, soft non-tender abdomen, fundal height = dates
data_table.Investigations: Hb 118, Plt 195, Cr 62, ALT 28, Urate 0.36; PCR 45 mg/mmol; CTG reactive, baseline 145; USS EFW 55th centile, AFI normal, UA Doppler normal
data_table.Medications: Labetalol 200 mg PO BD (commenced 24 hrs ago); Betamethasone course completed 12 hrs ago

lead_in: What is the most appropriate timing for delivery?

options.text: Induction of labour within the next 24-48 hours, as delivery is recommended for this hypertensive disorder of pregnancy at or beyond 37+0 weeks
options.rationale: RANZCOG and international guidelines consistently recommend that women with pre-eclampsia at ≥37 weeks gestation should be delivered, as the risks of continuing the pregnancy (progression to severe disease, eclampsia, placental abruption) outweigh the benefits of further foetal maturation. For this woman at 36+6 (effectively term within hours), delivery should be planned within 24-48 hours. The PHOENIX trial demonstrated reduced maternal morbidity with planned early delivery at 37 weeks compared to expectant management. (Source: SOMANZ: Hypertensive Disorders of Pregnancy; RANZCOG: Pre-eclampsia Guideline)

options.text: Continue oral labetalol and expectant management with twice-weekly review until 39+0 weeks, as her blood pressure is now controlled and she has no severe features
options.rationale: Continue oral labetalol to 39 weeks is wrong because pre-eclampsia at term (>=37 weeks) is itself an indication for delivery; the PHOENIX trial showed reduced maternal morbidity with planned 37-week delivery vs expectant management. Would be correct only for gestational hypertension without pre-eclampsia (SOMANZ: Hypertensive Disorders of Pregnancy; PHOENIX Trial).

options.text: Perform immediate caesarean section, as the proteinuria with a PCR of 45 mg/mmol and hypertension at 36+6 weeks meets severe-features criteria requiring urgent delivery
options.rationale: Immediate caesarean for severe pre-eclampsia is wrong because she does not meet severe pre-eclampsia criteria (BP <170/110, platelets normal, creatinine normal, transaminases normal, no symptoms, PCR 45 mg/mmol is significant proteinuria but does not by itself denote severe disease); vaginal delivery via induction is appropriate. Would be correct only with severe features (SOMANZ).

options.text: Discharge home on labetalol 200 mg BD with instructions for daily BP monitoring and twice-weekly CTG and ultrasound, with delivery planned at 40+0 weeks
options.rationale: Discharge with delivery at 40+0 is wrong because pre-eclampsia at term mandates delivery, not outpatient observation; discharging risks deterioration outside monitoring. Would never be correct in pre-eclampsia at term (SOMANZ; Safer Care Victoria: Timing of Birth).
source_refs: Safer Care Victoria: Timing of Birth

options.text: Wait until 38+0 weeks to allow full corticosteroid effect, then induce labour if the blood pressure remains controlled on oral labetalol without development of severe features
options.rationale: Wait until 38+0 for full steroid effect is wrong because steroids are not indicated at >=37 weeks (lungs are mature) and waiting prolongs pre-eclampsia unnecessarily. Would be correct only at preterm gestations (SOMANZ; RANZCOG: Antenatal Corticosteroids Guideline).

explanation.summary: This woman at 36+6 weeks has pre-eclampsia without severe features (BP controlled on oral labetalol, normal bloods, normal foetal assessment). Guidelines consistently recommend delivery at ≥37 weeks for pre-eclampsia, regardless of severity. The PHOENIX trial demonstrated that planned delivery at 37 weeks in women with pre-eclampsia reduces maternal morbidity compared to expectant management, without increasing neonatal morbidity. Her BP is controlled, she has no severe features, and vaginal delivery with induction is appropriate. Corticosteroids are not indicated at term.
key_points: Pre-eclampsia at ≥37 weeks gestation: delivery is recommended within 24-48 hours, regardless of severity. (Source: SOMANZ: Hypertensive Disorders of Pregnancy; RANZCOG: Pre-eclampsia Guideline)
The PHOENIX trial showed planned early delivery reduces maternal morbidity in late preterm pre-eclampsia without increasing neonatal morbidity. (Source: PHOENIX Trial)

Severe pre-eclampsia features include: BP ≥170/110, platelets <100, creatinine >90, transaminases >70, epigastric pain, visual disturbance, clonus. (Source: SOMANZ: Hypertensive Disorders of Pregnancy)
Antenatal corticosteroids are not indicated at ≥37+0 weeks as foetal lung maturity is established. (Source: RANZCOG: Antenatal Corticosteroids Guideline)
explanation.pearls: The cutoff of 37 weeks is critical in pre-eclampsia management: before 37 weeks, expectant management may be appropriate if no severe features; at or after 37 weeks, delivery is recommended irrespective of severity.

sources.label: Safer Care Victoria: Timing of Birth

stem: A 28-year-old primigravida at 40+2 weeks gestation is in active labour with an epidural in situ. Her pregnancy has been uncomplicated. The membranes ruptured spontaneously 6 hours ago with clear liquor. Oxytocin augmentation was commenced 2 hours ago for slow progress and is currently running at 12 mU/min. She is contracting 4 in 10 minutes. On vaginal examination, the cervix is 7 cm dilated, the presenting part is at station 0, and there is a caput. A foetal scalp electrode has been placed due to difficulty with external monitoring. The CTG over the past 60 minutes shows a baseline of 165 bpm, variability of 2-3 bpm, and shallow late decelerations of 15 bpm lasting 45 seconds following each contraction. There are no accelerations. The maternal temperature is 37.8°C, HR 98 bpm, BP 115/70 mmHg. The midwife has performed foetal scalp stimulation with no acceleratory response.

data_table.Vital signs: Maternal Temp 37.8°C, HR 98 bpm, BP 115/70 mmHg, RR 20
data_table.Examination: Cervix 7 cm, station 0, caput present, clear liquor, FSE in situ
data_table.Investigations: CTG: baseline 165 bpm, variability 2-3 bpm, shallow late decelerations of 15 bpm lasting 45 sec with each contraction, no accelerations, no response to foetal scalp stimulation
data_table.Medications: Epidural in situ; Oxytocin infusion at 12 mU/min

lead_in: How should this CTG be classified, and what is the most appropriate immediate action?

options.text: Classify as suspicious CTG; continue oxytocin, reposition the mother into left lateral, and reassess the CTG in 30 minutes after a 500 mL IV fluid bolus
options.rationale: Suspicious classification and wait 30 min is wrong because tachycardia >160 plus reduced variability <5 plus late decelerations meets pathological criteria, not suspicious; continuing oxytocin in pathological CTG worsens hypoxia. Would be correct only for a suspicious CTG (one major feature without ongoing decelerations) (RANZCOG: Intrapartum Foetal Surveillance Guideline).

options.text: Classify as pathological CTG; cease the oxytocin infusion immediately, commence maternal oxygen via face mask at 8 L/min, and prepare for expedited delivery if the CTG does not improve within 30 minutes
options.rationale: Pathological with maternal O2 and wait 30 min is wrong because maternal oxygen has not been shown to improve foetal outcomes and is not recommended by RANZCOG; furthermore waiting 30 min after a pathological tracing with no response to scalp stimulation is too long. Would be correct only if maternal hypoxia were itself the cause (RANZCOG: Intrapartum Foetal Surveillance Guideline).

options.text: Classify as pathological CTG; cease the oxytocin infusion, reposition to left lateral, consider terbutaline 250 mcg SC for intrauterine resuscitation, and prepare for immediate delivery if the CTG does not normalise promptly
options.rationale: The CTG meets criteria for pathological: baseline tachycardia (>160 bpm), reduced variability (2-3 bpm), and late decelerations, with no response to scalp stimulation. Immediate actions include ceasing oxytocin, repositioning, and considering acute tocolysis (terbutaline 250 mcg SC) to reduce uterine activity and improve placental perfusion. If the CTG does not normalise promptly with intrauterine resuscitation, expedited delivery is indicated. The cervix is 7 cm, so caesarean delivery may be required if vaginal delivery is not imminent. (Source: RANZCOG: Intrapartum Foetal Surveillance Guideline; KEMH: Foetal Surveillance in Labour)

options.text: Classify as suspicious CTG due to maternal pyrexia and epidural; administer paracetamol 1 g IV, continue oxytocin, and reassess CTG in 15 minutes after the temperature normalises
options.rationale: Suspicious due to pyrexia/epidural and continue oxytocin is wrong because while maternal pyrexia/epidural can contribute to foetal tachycardia, the reduced variability, late decelerations and lack of scalp-stimulation acceleration go beyond simple maternal effects; continuing oxytocin in pathological tracing is unsafe. Would be correct only with isolated mild tachycardia attributable to pyrexia (RANZCOG: Intrapartum Foetal Surveillance Guideline).

options.text: Classify as pathological CTG; perform an immediate category 1 caesarean section without further intrauterine resuscitation measures as the foetus is already compromised
options.rationale: Immediate caesarean without intrauterine resuscitation is wrong because intrauterine resuscitation (cease oxytocin, reposition, tocolysis) should be initiated while preparing for theatre; many tracings recover and avoid unnecessary surgery. Would be correct only with acute prolonged bradycardia or terminal bradycardia (RANZCOG: Intrapartum Foetal Surveillance Guideline).

explanation.summary: This CTG demonstrates a pathological pattern: baseline foetal tachycardia (165 bpm), reduced variability (2-3 bpm), and late decelerations following every contraction, with no accelerations and no response to foetal scalp stimulation. The correct management involves immediate intrauterine resuscitation (ceasing oxytocin, left lateral positioning, and considering acute tocolysis with terbutaline 250 mcg SC) while preparing for expedited delivery if there is no prompt resolution. The cervix is 7 cm, so vaginal delivery is not imminent, and caesarean section may be needed. Maternal pyrexia is a contributing factor but does not explain the pathological features.
key_points: RANZCOG CTG classification: Normal (all 4 features reassuring), Suspicious (1 non-reassuring), Pathological (≥2 non-reassuring or ≥1 abnormal feature). (Source: RANZCOG: Intrapartum Foetal Surveillance Guideline)
Pathological features: baseline <100 or >160, variability <5 bpm for >50 min, late or prolonged decelerations >50% of contractions. (Source: RANZCOG: Intrapartum Foetal Surveillance Guideline)

Foetal scalp stimulation: a reassuring acceleratory response (rise of ≥15 bpm for ≥15 sec) indicates a non-acidotic foetus; absence of response warrants further assessment. (Source: KEMH: Foetal Surveillance in Labour)

Intrauterine resuscitation: cease oxytocin, reposition, consider acute tocolysis (terbutaline 250 mcg SC). Do NOT use maternal oxygen routinely. (Source: RANZCOG: Intrapartum Foetal Surveillance Guideline)
explanation.pearls: A CTG with baseline tachycardia, reduced variability, and late decelerations is an obstetric 'code yellow' - act immediately with intrauterine resuscitation, escalate to senior staff, and prepare the theatre team. The combination is highly predictive of foetal acidosis.

stem: A 24-year-old primigravida delivered vaginally 10 days ago following induction of labour at 38 weeks for gestational hypertension. The third stage was managed actively with intramuscular oxytocin 10 IU, and the placenta was delivered complete by controlled cord traction. She was discharged home on day 2 post-delivery with no concerns. She now presents to the emergency department with new-onset heavy vaginal bleeding that began 4 hours ago, soaking through 3 maternity pads. She also reports a 2-day history of dull lower abdominal pain, malaise, and a temperature of 38.2°C measured at home. The lochia has been malodorous for 3 days. On examination, she looks pale and unwell. Her HR is 106 bpm, BP 100/60 mmHg, and temperature is 38.4°C. The uterus is tender, boggy, and palpable 3 cm below the umbilicus. On speculum, there is heavy dark red blood with clots in the vagina, and the os is open with blood and tissue visible. Her haemoglobin is 88 g/L (was 108 g/L antenatally), WCC is 16.2 x 10^9/L, and CRP is 125 mg/L.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 106 bpm, BP 100/60 mmHg, Temp 38.4°C, RR 18, SpO2 97%
data_table.Examination: Pale, unwell, uterus tender boggy 3 cm below umbilicus, speculum: heavy dark blood with clots, os open, tissue visible
data_table.Investigations: Hb 88 g/L (from 108 antenatal), WCC 16.2 x 10^9/L, CRP 125 mg/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Commence IV clindamycin 900 mg 8-hourly and IV gentamicin 5 mg/kg daily for endometritis, and administer IV oxytocin 40 units infusion for uterine atony
options.rationale: Antibiotics plus oxytocin alone is wrong because the open os with visible tissue strongly indicates retained products of conception; antibiotics and uterotonics without addressing retained tissue do not solve the bleeding source. Would be correct only for endometritis without retained products (KEMH: Secondary PPH Guideline).

options.text: Perform a bimanual examination and attempt digital removal of retained tissue from the uterine cavity under sedation, followed by IV oxytocin and antibiotics
options.rationale: Digital exploration without ultrasound is wrong because blind digital exploration risks uterine perforation, especially in a tender, infected postpartum uterus; confirmation with ultrasound and evacuation under anaesthesia in theatre is safer. Would be correct only in an emergency without ultrasound access (KEMH: Secondary PPH Guideline).

options.text: Perform an urgent pelvic ultrasound to assess for retained products of conception, commence IV antibiotics (clindamycin and gentamicin), administer an IV oxytocin infusion, and arrange for suction curettage in theatre if retained products are confirmed
options.rationale: The clinical picture of secondary PPH at day 10 with fever, malodorous lochia, tender boggy uterus, open os with visible tissue, and a falling haemoglobin is classic for retained products of conception with superimposed endometritis. Management includes: urgent pelvic ultrasound to confirm retained products, commence IV broad-spectrum antibiotics (clindamycin + gentamicin for endometritis), administer uterotonics (oxytocin), and arrange suction curettage in theatre. Antibiotics should be started before the procedure. (Source: KEMH: Secondary Postpartum Haemorrhage Guideline; Therapeutic Guidelines: Obstetric Emergencies)

options.text: Administer IV tranexamic acid 1 g, IV ergometrine 250 mcg, and oral misoprostol 800 mcg, and observe for 4 hours; if bleeding persists, arrange a pelvic ultrasound
options.rationale: TXA, ergometrine, misoprostol then wait 4h is wrong because while these may temporarily reduce bleeding, they do not address retained products; delay risks ongoing haemorrhage and worsening sepsis. Would be correct only as bridging while awaiting theatre (KEMH: Secondary PPH Guideline).

options.text: Commence oral doxycycline 100 mg BD and oral metronidazole 400 mg TDS for endometritis, and arrange a pelvic ultrasound as an outpatient within 48 hours if the bleeding settles
options.rationale: Oral antibiotics and outpatient ultrasound is wrong because she is systemically unwell (tachycardic, hypotensive, febrile, anaemic) with ongoing haemorrhage; outpatient management is unsafe. Would be correct only in mild secondary endometritis without sepsis or significant bleeding (Therapeutic Guidelines: Obstetric Emergencies).

explanation.summary: This woman has secondary postpartum haemorrhage occurring 10 days post-delivery, most likely due to retained products of conception with superimposed endometritis. Key features: heavy vaginal bleeding, tender boggy uterus, open os with visible tissue, fever, malodorous lochia, and a significant drop in haemoglobin. The appropriate management is urgent pelvic ultrasound to confirm retained products, IV broad-spectrum antibiotics (clindamycin and gentamicin), uterotonics, and suction curettage in theatre. Retained products are the most common cause of secondary PPH, occurring in approximately 1% of deliveries.
key_points: Secondary PPH is defined as excessive vaginal bleeding occurring between 24 hours and 6 weeks postpartum. (Source: KEMH: Secondary Postpartum Haemorrhage Guideline)
The most common causes are retained products of conception, endometritis, and subinvolution of the placental site. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Obstetric Emergencies)

Management includes: pelvic ultrasound, IV antibiotics (clindamycin + gentamicin for endometritis), uterotonics, and suction curettage if retained products are confirmed. (Source: KEMH: Secondary Postpartum Haemorrhage Guideline)
Always send evacuated tissue for histopathology to confirm retained products and exclude gestational trophoblastic disease. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Obstetric Emergencies)
explanation.pearls: Secondary PPH with fever and malodorous lochia = retained products with endometritis until proven otherwise. Ultrasound is the key investigation, but surgical evacuation should not be delayed if the clinical picture is convincing and the patient is unstable.

stem: A 38-year-old G2P1 at 30+1 weeks gestation was diagnosed with GDM at 27 weeks on a 75 g OGTT (fasting 5.6 mmol/L, 1-hour 10.8 mmol/L, 2-hour 9.2 mmol/L). She commenced metformin 500 mg BD at diagnosis, titrated to 1 g BD over 2 weeks, and has been on this dose for the past 3 weeks with ongoing dietary modification. Her fasting BGLs have been 5.3-5.8 mmol/L consistently. Her 2-hour postprandial BGLs have been 6.5-7.2 mmol/L. An ultrasound at 29 weeks showed an estimated foetal weight on the 82nd centile with a normal amniotic fluid index. She has no contraindications to insulin and has been counselled about the need to escalate therapy. She weighs 78 kg. Her HbA1c is 5.8% (40 mmol/mol).

data_table.Vital signs: BP 118/74 mmHg, fundal height 32 cm
data_table.Examination: Normal examination
data_table.Investigations: OGTT: fasting 5.6, 1h 10.8, 2h 9.2 (all diagnostic); fasting BGLs 5.3-5.8 (target <5.1); 2h postprandial 6.5-7.2 (target <6.7); EFW 82nd centile; HbA1c 5.8%
data_table.Medications: Metformin 1 g PO BD (max tolerated dose)

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in her glycaemic management?

options.text: Continue metformin 1 g BD and add basal insulin (isophane or insulin detemir) at night, starting at 0.1-0.2 units/kg, titrated to achieve fasting BGL below 5.1 mmol/L
options.rationale: ADIPS guidelines recommend that if glycaemic targets are not met within 1-2 weeks of lifestyle modification and metformin therapy, insulin should be commenced. This woman has persistently elevated fasting BGLs (5.3-5.8 mmol/L) despite maximum metformin for 3 weeks, and elevated postprandial values. Basal insulin at night is the appropriate escalation, targeting fasting hyperglycaemia first. Metformin should be continued alongside insulin. (Source: ADIPS: Gestational Diabetes Mellitus Guideline; RANZCOG: Gestational Diabetes)

options.text: Cease metformin and commence basal-bolus insulin regimen with isophane at night and rapid-acting insulin analogue before each meal to target both fasting and postprandial hyperglycaemia simultaneously
options.rationale: Cease metformin and start basal-bolus is wrong because basal insulin alone is appropriate when fasting hyperglycaemia is the primary problem; basal-bolus is for failed postprandial control on optimised basal. Metformin should be continued alongside insulin to reduce insulin requirements. Would be correct only with severe postprandial spikes despite optimised basal (ADIPS: GDM Guideline).

options.text: Increase metformin to 1.5 g BD (total 3 g daily) and add glibenclamide 2.5 mg at night for an additional 2 weeks before considering insulin
options.rationale: Increase metformin and add glibenclamide is wrong because she is at maximum metformin (2 g/day) and glibenclamide is not recommended in Australia (higher neonatal hypoglycaemia, macrosomia); escalation should be to insulin. Would be correct only outside Australian guideline practice (ADIPS).

options.text: Continue current metformin dose and dietary measures for a further 2 weeks as the fasting BGLs are only marginally above target, then reassess with repeat HbA1c
options.rationale: Continue current regimen and reassess in 2 weeks is wrong because ADIPS recommends escalation if targets are not met within 1-2 weeks of maximal oral therapy; fasting BGLs have been above target for 3 weeks with EFW already on the 82nd centile, raising macrosomia risk. Would be correct only at the 1-2 week mark with marginal elevation (ADIPS).

options.text: Commence rapid-acting insulin analogue before breakfast only, as her 2-hour postprandial BGLs are above target while fasting values are approaching acceptable levels
options.rationale: Prebreakfast bolus only is wrong because her fasting BGLs (5.3-5.8) are consistently above the 5.1 target, driving macrosomia; fasting hyperglycaemia should be tackled with basal insulin first. Would be correct only if fasting were at target and postprandial were the primary issue (ADIPS).

explanation.summary: This woman has GDM with persistent fasting hyperglycaemia (target <5.1, hers 5.3-5.8) and elevated postprandial BGLs (target <6.7, hers 6.5-7.2) despite 3 weeks of maximum metformin therapy. The foetal growth is already on the 82nd centile. ADIPS guidelines recommend escalating to insulin when targets are not achieved on oral therapy. Basal insulin (isophane or detemir) at night is the appropriate first step, targeting fasting hyperglycaemia. Metformin should be continued alongside insulin to maintain its insulin-sensitising benefits.
key_points: ADIPS GDM glycaemic targets: fasting <5.1 mmol/L, 1-hour postprandial <7.8 mmol/L, 2-hour postprandial <6.7 mmol/L. (Source: ADIPS: Gestational Diabetes Mellitus Guideline)
If targets are not met within 1-2 weeks of metformin therapy, insulin should be commenced. Metformin should be continued. (Source: ADIPS: Gestational Diabetes Mellitus Guideline)
Basal insulin (isophane or detemir) starting at 0.1-0.2 units/kg at night is the appropriate first-line insulin regimen for predominant fasting hyperglycaemia. (Source: RANZCOG: Gestational Diabetes)
Glibenclamide is not recommended in Australian practice due to higher rates of adverse neonatal outcomes compared to insulin. (Source: ADIPS: Gestational Diabetes Mellitus Guideline)
explanation.pearls: Fasting hyperglycaemia in GDM is a stronger predictor of macrosomia than postprandial hyperglycaemia. When fasting BGLs are above target on maximal metformin, do not delay insulin initiation - the foetus is already being exposed to hyperglycaemia.

stem: A 34-year-old primigravida at 37+1 weeks gestation attends her routine antenatal appointment. On abdominal palpation, the foetus is found to be in a breech presentation with the foetal head ballotable at the fundus. A bedside ultrasound confirms a frank breech presentation with the foetal legs extended, a flexed foetal head, normal amniotic fluid index, and a posterior placenta not covering the os. The estimated foetal weight is on the 55th centile. She is otherwise well with no significant past medical history. She has no contraindications to external cephalic version (ECV) and is keen to avoid a caesarean section if possible. She has read about breech vaginal delivery and asks whether this is a safe option for her.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 112/70 mmHg, HR 78 bpm, fundal height 37 cm
data_table.Examination: Foetus in frank breech on palpation; bedside USS confirms frank breech, flexed head, AFI normal, placenta posterior not covering os, EFW 55th centile
data_table.Investigations: USS: frank breech, flexed head, normal AFI, posterior placenta, EFW 55th centile

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management recommendation?

options.text: Offer external cephalic version, and if unsuccessful or declined, discuss planned caesarean section at 39+0 weeks as the safest mode of delivery for a term breech
options.rationale: RANZCOG recommends that women with a breech presentation at or near term should be informed about external cephalic version (ECV) and offered it if clinically appropriate. If ECV is unsuccessful or declined, planned caesarean section is recommended based on the Term Breech Trial which demonstrated reduced perinatal mortality and morbidity with planned caesarean compared to planned vaginal breech delivery. Vaginal breech delivery may be considered in carefully selected cases with an experienced clinician, but the default recommendation is caesarean. (Source: RANZCOG: Management of Breech Presentation at Term)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Management of Breech Presentation at Term

options.text: Offer external cephalic version, and if unsuccessful, plan a vaginal breech delivery as this primigravida has a frank breech with flexed head and normal foetal weight on the 55th centile, which are favourable factors
options.rationale: Plan vaginal breech after failed ECV is wrong because the Term Breech Trial showed reduced perinatal morbidity and mortality with planned caesarean vs planned vaginal breech delivery; vaginal breech remains a possibility only with rigorous selection and experienced operator, and primigravidity is a relative risk factor. Would be correct only in carefully selected cases with experienced clinician after counselling (RANZCOG: Management of Breech Presentation at Term).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Management of Breech Presentation at Term

options.text: Proceed directly to booking an elective caesarean section at 39+0 weeks without offering ECV, as primigravidae have a lower ECV success rate and ECV carries risks of foetal distress and placental abruption
options.rationale: Skip ECV entirely is wrong because RANZCOG recommends offering ECV to all women with breech at term in the absence of contraindications; ECV is worthwhile (35-40% success in primigravidae) and risks are low (foetal HR changes 5%, abruption <1%, emergency CS <0.5%). Would be correct only with contraindications to ECV (RANZCOG).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Management of Breech Presentation at Term

options.text: Perform external cephalic version immediately at this visit without tocolysis, as she is 37 weeks, and if successful, discharge home to await spontaneous labour
options.rationale: Immediate ECV at clinic without tocolysis is wrong because ECV should be performed in a setting with immediate caesarean access, tocolysis (e.g. terbutaline 250 mcg SC), informed consent and appropriate facilities; clinic ECV without tocolysis lowers success and safety. Would never be correct in this setting (RANZCOG).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Management of Breech Presentation at Term

options.text: Advise the woman that breech presentation at 37 weeks has a high chance of spontaneous version before term, and arrange a repeat ultrasound at 39 weeks to reassess presentation before making a delivery plan
options.rationale: Wait until 39 weeks for reassessment is wrong because spontaneous version after 37 weeks occurs in only ~8% of breech; waiting misses the optimal ECV window and risks labour with undiagnosed plan. Would be correct only when ECV has already failed and a delivery plan is in place (RANZCOG).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Management of Breech Presentation at Term

explanation.summary: This primigravida at 37 weeks with a frank breech presentation should be offered ECV, which has a success rate of approximately 35-40% in nulliparous women. If ECV is unsuccessful or declined, a planned caesarean section is the recommended mode of delivery based on evidence from the Term Breech Trial. Vaginal breech delivery may be an option in carefully selected cases with an experienced clinician, but the default recommendation is caesarean. ECV should be performed with tocolysis in a setting with immediate access to caesarean section.
key_points: RANZCOG recommends that all women with breech presentation at term should be offered ECV in the absence of contraindications. (Source: RANZCOG: Management of Breech Presentation at Term)

ECV success rates: approximately 35-40% in nulliparous women, 60-65% in multiparous women. Tocolysis (terbutaline 250 mcg SC) improves success. (Source: RANZCOG: Management of Breech Presentation at Term)
If ECV is unsuccessful or declined, planned caesarean section is recommended based on the Term Breech Trial (reduced perinatal mortality and morbidity). (Source: Term Breech Trial)

Contraindications to ECV: placenta praevia, abruption, non-reassuring foetal status, ruptured membranes, multiple pregnancy, uterine anomaly, hyperextended foetal head. (Source: RANZCOG: Management of Breech Presentation at Term)
explanation.pearls: The window for ECV is 37-40 weeks: too early and the foetus may spontaneously revert, too late and the foetus may be too large to turn, and spontaneous labour may intervene.

sources.label: RANZCOG: Management of Breech Presentation at Term

stem: A 32-year-old G2P1 at 33+5 weeks presents with a single episode of painless bright red vaginal bleeding. Ultrasound at 32 weeks showed an anterior placenta completely covering the internal os (major praevia). She is haemodynamically stable, bleeding has settled, CTG reassuring, cervical exam deferred. She has had no previous corticosteroid courses this pregnancy. The plan is admission for monitoring and elective caesarean at 36+0 to 37+0 weeks. She is keen on whatever course will best protect her baby's lung function.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 80 bpm, BP 110/70 mmHg, Temp 36.7°C
data_table.Examination: No active bleeding, soft non-tender uterus, CTG reactive
data_table.Investigations: Known major placenta praevia on previous USS

lead_in: What is the most appropriate corticosteroid plan?

options.text: Administer betamethasone 11.4 mg IM, two doses 24 hours apart, now
options.rationale: Antenatal corticosteroids are recommended for women at risk of preterm birth before 34+6 weeks. At 33+5 with active bleeding from major praevia and likely early delivery, a single course is indicated (RANZCOG; RCOG). 'Late preterm' (34+0 to 36+6) ACS is selective rather than universal.

options.text: Administer no corticosteroids as she is now beyond 34 weeks
options.rationale: Withhold steroids because beyond 34 weeks is wrong because she is 33+5 (below 34+0), inside the standard window; the misclassification skips clear benefit. Would be correct only at >=34+6 with no late-preterm criteria (RANZCOG).

options.text: Administer a single dose of betamethasone 11.4 mg IM
options.rationale: Single dose is wrong because the complete course is two doses 24 hours apart; single doses have reduced benefit. Would be correct only when delivery is so imminent that the second dose cannot be given (RANZCOG).

options.text: Administer weekly betamethasone 11.4 mg IM until delivery
options.rationale: Weekly repeats until delivery is wrong because routine weekly repeats are not recommended due to reduced birth weight and head circumference; only a single rescue dose is considered when delivery is anticipated within 7 days and the first course was >=7 days ago. Would be correct only as a single rescue dose, not weekly (RANZCOG).

options.text: Administer dexamethasone 6 mg IM, four doses 12-hourly, in place of betamethasone, on the basis of superior neurodevelopmental outcomes
options.rationale: Dexamethasone 4-dose regimen is wrong because Australian practice predominantly uses betamethasone; while dexamethasone is an equivalent alternative, the question is whether to give ACS not which agent, and the option implies a regimen change without grounds. Would be correct only when betamethasone is unavailable (RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: Corticosteroids for foetal lung maturation are only recommended for women at risk of preterm birth between 23+0 and 34+6 weeks. This woman is 36+0 weeks, and the bleeding is settled; delivery may be planned electively at 37-38 weeks, but steroids have no role at this gestation.
key_points: Antenatal corticosteroids should be given between 23+0 and 34+6 weeks when preterm delivery is anticipated. (Source: RANZCOG: Antenatal Corticosteroid Guideline)
After 34+6 weeks, the foetal lungs are sufficiently mature, and steroids are not beneficial. (Source: RANZCOG: Antenatal Corticosteroid Guideline)
In placenta praevia with settled bleeding, delivery is typically planned for 37-38 weeks without need for steroids. (Source: RANZCOG: Antepartum Haemorrhage Guideline)
explanation.pearls: The magic number for steroids is 34+6 – after that, no corticosteroids, no matter how imminent delivery may seem.

stem: A 30-year-old nullipara at 29+3 weeks presents with new hypertension 170/110 mmHg, headache, and epigastric pain. Urine protein:creatinine ratio 98 mg/mmol. Platelets 130 ×10⁹/L, ALT 110 U/L, creatinine 92 µmol/L. She has received the first dose of betamethasone. CTG is reactive with normal variability. She is commenced on labetalol and magnesium sulphate. After stabilisation, the team discusses timing of delivery. What is the most appropriate plan?

data_table.Examination: Epigastric tenderness, hyperreflexia, 3 beats clonus
data_table.Investigations: PCR 98, Platelets 130, ALT 110, Cr 92, CTG reactive
