data_table.Medications: Labetalol commenced, MgSO₄ infusion, betamethasone first dose given

lead_in: What is the most appropriate timing of delivery?

options.text: Complete the 48-hour steroid course and deliver electively at 48-72 hours, provided maternal and foetal status remain stable
options.rationale: Elective 48-72 hour delay is wrong because severe features (hepatic involvement, symptoms) mandate delivery within 24-48 hours of completing steroids, not deliberate elective delay; the anti-pattern is treating severe pre-eclampsia like late-preterm mild disease. Would be correct only with mild features not severe (SOMANZ Guideline).

options.text: Proceed to delivery within 24 hours, as severe-features hypertensive disease with hepatic involvement mandates delivery regardless of gestational age
options.rationale: Deliver within 24 hours regardless is wrong because at 29 weeks, the 24-48 hour window for steroid completion is justified unless there is severe refractory hypertension, eclampsia, pulmonary oedema or abruption; the foetal benefit of steroid completion is substantial. Would be correct only with severe refractory features (SOMANZ Guideline).

options.text: Continue expectant management with close monitoring until 34 weeks, as the CTG is reassuring and maternal status is stable on treatment
options.rationale: Expectancy to 34 weeks is wrong because expectant management at <34 weeks is reserved for the absence of severe features; she has hepatic involvement and symptoms, which mandate delivery after steroid completion. Would be correct only in mild pre-eclampsia without severe features (SOMANZ Guideline).

options.text: Administer the second dose of betamethasone at 24 hours and plan delivery for 48 hours after the first dose, with continuous monitoring
options.rationale: In severe pre-eclampsia between 24 and 34 weeks, delivery is recommended after the completion of a corticosteroid course (48 hours from first dose) if maternal and foetal conditions permit. This balances the benefits of foetal maturation against maternal risks. Delivery is expedited if deterioration occurs. (Source: SOMANZ Guideline; RANZCOG Pre-eclampsia Guideline)

options.text: Deliver immediately by caesarean, as severe-features hypertension with abnormal LFTs and platelets <150 ×10⁹/L meets haemolysis-elevated-liver-enzymes-low-platelets criteria
options.rationale: Immediate caesarean for HELLP is wrong because HELLP requires haemolysis (LDH >600, low haptoglobin), ALT >70 and platelets <100; her platelets are 130, so HELLP is not fully met. Furthermore, severe pre-eclampsia after steroid course allows vaginal delivery route if appropriate. Would be correct only with full HELLP and rapidly deteriorating maternal status (SOMANZ Guideline).

explanation.summary: Severe pre-eclampsia at 29 weeks without immediate life-threatening complications (no eclamptic seizure, no pulmonary oedema, no abruption) should be managed by completing the corticosteroid course over 48 hours, then proceeding with delivery. This optimises neonatal outcomes while minimising maternal risk. Delivery can be expedited if deterioration occurs.
key_points: For severe pre-eclampsia <34 weeks, delivery is recommended after completion of the antenatal corticosteroid course (48 hours). (Source: SOMANZ Guideline)

Indications for immediate delivery (within hours) include: uncontrolled hypertension despite multiple agents, eclampsia, pulmonary oedema, placental abruption, persistent severe headache/visual disturbance, deteriorating renal or liver function. (Source: RANZCOG Pre-eclampsia Guideline)
HELLP syndrome diagnosis requires haemolysis, elevated liver enzymes, and thrombocytopenia <100 ×10⁹/L. (Source: SOMANZ Guideline)
Magnesium sulphate is indicated for seizure prophylaxis in severe pre-eclampsia, irrespective of delivery timing. (Source: SOMANZ Guideline)
explanation.pearls: In severe pre-eclampsia, the clock starts with the first steroid dose; aim for delivery at 48 hours but be ready to deliver earlier if the mother or fetus deteriorates.

stem: A 33-year-old G2P1 at 34+1 weeks gestation presents with a 2-week history of intense generalised pruritus, worse on the palms and soles and especially at night, disrupting sleep. She has no rash other than excoriations. Her previous pregnancy 3 years ago ended in an uncomplicated term vaginal delivery. Liver function tests show: ALT 120 U/L, AST 100 U/L, GGT 45 U/L, total bile acids 42 micromol/L. Viral hepatitis serology, ANA, and anti-mitochondrial antibodies are negative. An ultrasound of the liver and gallbladder is normal. The foetal ultrasound shows normal growth, amniotic fluid index, and umbilical artery Dopplers. The CTG is reactive. She has been commenced on ursodeoxycholic acid 500 mg BD, which has partially improved her pruritus after 5 days, but bile acids have risen to 58 micromol/L.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 110/70 mmHg, HR 80 bpm, Temp 36.5°C
data_table.Examination: Excoriations over abdomen and limbs, no jaundice, fundal height 34 cm, non-tender uterus, reactive CTG
data_table.Investigations: ALT 120, AST 100, GGT 45, total bile acids 58 micromol/L (increased from 42); USS: normal foetus, AFI, Dopplers; negative viral/autoimmune screen
data_table.Medications: Ursodeoxycholic acid 500 mg BD for 5 days

lead_in: What is the most appropriate plan regarding timing of delivery?

options.text: Continue ursodeoxycholic acid, monitor bile acids weekly, and plan delivery at 37+0 weeks if bile acids remain above 40 micromol/L
options.rationale: Monitor and deliver only if bile acids >40 is wrong because her bile acids are already 58 (>=40) and rising; delaying to threshold rather than at threshold misses the stillbirth-prevention window. Would never be correct given current levels (RANZCOG: Cholestasis in Pregnancy; RCOG Green-top 43).

options.text: Plan delivery at 39+0 weeks after a further ultrasound for growth and amniotic fluid, as the foetus is currently well and bile acids are only moderately elevated
options.rationale: Plan delivery at 39+0 is wrong because bile acids >=40 carry increased stillbirth risk, and delivery at 39 weeks exposes the foetus to that risk; RANZCOG/RCOG recommend 37 weeks for moderate ICP. Would be correct only for mild ICP (bile acids <40) (RANZCOG; RCOG).

options.text: Recommend induction of labour at 37+0 weeks gestation, with continuous intrapartum CTG monitoring, and administer intramuscular vitamin K 10 mg to the mother prior to delivery
options.rationale: For bile acids between 40 and 99 µmol/L (moderate-severe cholestasis), delivery is recommended at 37 weeks gestation, with continuous CTG monitoring in labour. The risk of stillbirth rises after 37 weeks. Maternal vitamin K is given because of the risk of vitamin K deficiency due to fat malabsorption, which can lead to postpartum haemorrhage and foetal intracranial haemorrhage. Ursodeoxycholic acid is continued until delivery. (Source: RANZCOG: Cholestasis in Pregnancy; RCOG Green-top Guideline No. 43)

options.text: Deliver by elective caesarean section at 37+0 weeks, as vaginal delivery with raised bile acids carries an increased risk of foetal distress
options.rationale: Elective caesarean at 37+0 is wrong because there is no evidence supporting routine caesarean for cholestasis; vaginal delivery via induction is standard. Would be correct only with obstetric indications for caesarean (RANZCOG).

options.text: Add oral dexamethasone 4 mg BD to further reduce bile acids and delay delivery until 38+0 weeks if bile acids fall below 40 µmol/L
options.rationale: Dexamethasone to reduce bile acids and delay to 38+0 is wrong because dexamethasone has not been shown to be effective in ICP and UDCA is first-line; delaying to 38+0 exposes the foetus to stillbirth risk. Would never be correct (RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: Intrahepatic cholestasis of pregnancy with bile acids ≥40 µmol/L is associated with an increased risk of stillbirth, especially after 37 weeks. The recommended management is delivery at 37+0 weeks via induction of labour with continuous CTG monitoring. Maternal vitamin K should be given to prevent coagulopathy. Ursodeoxycholic acid is continued until delivery but does not alter the timing. Elective caesarean is not routinely required. Bile acids >100 µmol/L prompt delivery even earlier (36-37 weeks).
key_points: Bile acids ≥40 µmol/L: deliver at 37 weeks; bile acids ≥100 µmol/L: consider delivery at 36 weeks. (Source: RANZCOG: Cholestasis in Pregnancy; RCOG Guideline)
First-line therapy is ursodeoxycholic acid 500-1000 mg BD; it reduces pruritus and may lower bile acids but does not eliminate stillbirth risk. (Source: RANZCOG: Cholestasis in Pregnancy)
Maternal vitamin K 10 mg IM should be given prior to delivery to prevent foetal and maternal haemorrhage. (Source: RCOG Green-top Guideline No. 43)
Continuous CTG monitoring in labour is recommended due to the risk of acute foetal compromise. (Source: RANZCOG: Cholestasis in Pregnancy)
explanation.pearls: For cholestasis, the bile acid level, not the degree of pruritus, dictates delivery timing. A woman may feel fine but be at high risk of stillbirth.

stem: A 28-year-old primigravida delivers a vigorous infant weighing 3.6 kg vaginally after an uncomplicated spontaneous labour. The placenta delivered spontaneously 5 minutes later with gentle cord traction by the attending midwife. Immediately following placental delivery, the woman experiences sudden severe lower abdominal pain, a vasovagal episode with bradycardia (HR 40 bpm), and estimated blood loss of 600 mL. On inspection, a firm, dark red mass is seen protruding from the vagina, approximately 10 cm in diameter, with the placenta still partially attached. The uterine fundus is not palpable abdominally. Her blood pressure drops to 80/50 mmHg, and she appears pale and diaphoretic.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 40 bpm, BP 80/50 mmHg, RR 20, SpO₂ 96%
data_table.Examination: Firm dark red mass protruding from vagina, placenta partially attached, fundus not palpable abdominally, EBL 600 mL
data_table.Investigations: Not applicable in acute emergency

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate step to manage the uterine inversion?

options.text: Attempt immediate manual replacement of the uterus using the Johnson manoeuvre (pushing the fundus upwards through the cervix with the palm, applying pressure at the last-delivered part first)
options.rationale: The immediate management of uterine inversion is manual replacement without removing the placenta if still attached (removal can increase haemorrhage). The Johnson manoeuvre involves placing the hand in the vagina, cupping the inverted fundus, and pushing it upward through the cervix, applying pressure at the part that inverted last (the fundus) and lifting the uterus back into the abdominal cavity. This should be performed urgently under analgesia or anaesthesia. Delaying to remove the placenta or transfer to theatre without attempting replacement increases blood loss and shock. (Source: RANZCOG: Uterine Inversion; KEMH: Uterine Inversion Guideline)

options.text: Administer intramuscular terbutaline 250 mcg to relax the uterus before attempting replacement, then transfer to theatre for manual replacement under general anaesthesia
options.rationale: Tocolysis before initial replacement is wrong because the first attempt should be immediate (Johnson manoeuvre), with tocolytics (terbutaline, GTN) as adjunct if initial reduction fails; the anti-pattern is delaying replacement to fetch drugs and theatre. Would be correct only if initial replacement fails (RANZCOG: Uterine Inversion).

options.text: Remove the placenta from the inverted uterus first to reduce bulk and facilitate replacement, then push the fundus upwards with a fist
options.rationale: Remove placenta first is wrong because removing the placenta from the inverted uterus exposes the placental bed and dramatically worsens haemorrhage; placenta is replaced with the uterus then removed afterwards. Would never be correct (RANZCOG).

options.text: Perform the O'Sullivan hydrostatic reduction by infusing warm saline into the vagina via a silastic vacuum cup to create a seal, pushing the uterus back into position
options.rationale: Hydrostatic reduction (O'Sullivan) first is wrong because manual replacement (Johnson) is the immediate first step; hydrostatic reduction is an alternative if manual replacement fails. Would be correct only as second-line (KEMH: Uterine Inversion Guideline).

options.text: Transfer immediately to operating theatre for a laparotomy and Huntington procedure (using Allis forceps to pull the round ligaments and uterus back through the inversion ring)
options.rationale: Transfer to theatre for laparotomy first is wrong because laparotomy (Huntington/Haultain procedures) is reserved for cases where manual reduction (under anaesthesia) fails; immediate vaginal attempt is the first step. Would be correct only after failed manual reduction under anaesthesia (RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: Uterine inversion is a rare obstetric emergency presenting with a protruding mass, severe pain, vasovagal shock (bradycardia and hypotension disproportionate to blood loss), and a non-palpable fundus. The immediate management is the Johnson manoeuvre: manual replacement of the uterus through the cervix without removing the placenta. Tocolytics can be used if reduction is difficult, and hydrostatic reduction (O'Sullivan) is an alternative. Laparotomy (Huntington procedure) is a last resort. Removing the placenta prematurely risks catastrophic haemorrhage.
key_points: Uterine inversion presents with a vaginal mass, severe pain, and vasovagal shock (bradycardia, hypotension) disproportionate to blood loss. (Source: RANZCOG: Uterine Inversion)
Immediate manual replacement via Johnson manoeuvre is the first step; do not delay by removing the placenta. (Source: KEMH: Uterine Inversion Guideline)
If reduction fails, tocolytics (terbutaline, nitroglycerin) can be administered to relax the uterus. (Source: RANZCOG: Uterine Inversion)
Laparotomy (Huntington procedure) is reserved for refractory cases. Hydrostatic reduction (O'Sullivan) is an alternative vaginal technique. (Source: RANZCOG: Uterine Inversion)
explanation.pearls: In uterine inversion, the degree of shock often appears out of proportion to the visible blood loss because of parasympathetic stimulation from stretching the pelvic peritoneum. Don't be misled – it's a life-threatening emergency.

stem: A 35-year-old G2P1 at 39+4 weeks is in active labour with spontaneous rupture of membranes 2 hours ago, meconium-stained liquor, and a prolonged second stage. She suddenly becomes agitated, complains of difficulty breathing, and then collapses. The maternal heart rate is 140 bpm with a faint pulse, blood pressure 60/30 mmHg, SpO₂ 78% on room air. She becomes cyanotic and unresponsive. Cardiopulmonary resuscitation is started immediately. The foetal heart rate drops to 60 bpm. An emergency caesarean is performed in the labour ward. Intraoperatively, she develops severe coagulopathy with diffuse oozing from all surgical sites, and laboratory tests show: haemoglobin 70 g/L, platelets 20 ×10⁹/L, fibrinogen <0.5 g/L, APTT >100 sec, PT >60 sec, and elevated D-dimers.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 140 bpm, BP 60/30 mmHg, SpO₂ 78% RA, collapse during labour
data_table.Examination: Cyanotic, unresponsive, severe coagulopathy intraoperatively, diffuse oozing, foetal bradycardia 60 bpm
data_table.Investigations: Hb 70, Plt 20, fibrinogen <0.5, APTT >100, PT >60, elevated D-dimers

lead_in: What is the most likely diagnosis?

options.text: Pulmonary thromboembolism secondary to deep vein thrombosis
options.rationale: Pulmonary embolism is wrong because PE causes hypoxia and collapse but not the profound consumptive coagulopathy (fibrinogen <0.5, low platelets, raised D-dimers) seen here; AFE's hallmark is the DIC component. Would be correct only in pure PE without DIC (RANZCOG: Amniotic Fluid Embolism; UKOSS Study).

options.text: Amniotic fluid embolism causing cardiovascular collapse, respiratory failure, and disseminated intravascular coagulation
options.rationale: Amniotic fluid embolism (AFE) typically presents during labour or immediately postpartum with a triad of acute hypoxia, cardiovascular collapse (often pulseless electrical activity), and consumptive coagulopathy (DIC). Risk factors include advanced maternal age, meconium-stained liquor, and prolonged labour. The rapid development of DIC with extremely low fibrinogen is characteristic. Management is supportive: CPR, high-flow oxygen, massive transfusion, and correction of coagulopathy. (Source: RANZCOG: Amniotic Fluid Embolism; UK Obstetric Surveillance System)

options.text: Septic shock due to chorioamnionitis from meconium-stained liquor, causing hypotension and DIC
options.rationale: Septic shock from chorioamnionitis is wrong because chorioamnionitis typically presents with fever, uterine tenderness and purulent liquor before collapse, and the DIC develops more gradually; the catastrophic sudden onset with rapid DIC fits AFE. Would be correct only with prodromal chorioamnionitis (RANZCOG).

options.text: Anaphylactic reaction to an administered medication causing hypotension and distributive shock, with secondary coagulopathy
options.rationale: Anaphylaxis is wrong because anaphylaxis does not typically cause the extreme consumptive coagulopathy with fibrinogen <0.5; bronchospasm, urticaria and angioedema would be expected. Would be correct only with classic anaphylactic features and triggering exposure (RANZCOG).

options.text: Placental abruption with massive concealed haemorrhage leading to hypovolaemic shock and coagulopathy
options.rationale: Placental abruption is wrong because abruption typically presents with abdominal pain, uterine hypertonicity and vaginal bleeding; sudden respiratory distress and severe DIC without uterine signs favours AFE. Would be correct only with the painful-bleeding-and-woody-uterus picture (RANZCOG: Antepartum Haemorrhage Guideline).

explanation.summary: Amniotic fluid embolism is a catastrophic obstetric emergency characterised by the triad of acute respiratory failure, cardiovascular collapse, and consumptive coagulopathy (DIC). Risk factors include meconium-stained liquor, prolonged labour, and advanced maternal age. Diagnosis is clinical and one of exclusion. The management is entirely supportive: immediate CPR, high-flow oxygen/ventilation, massive transfusion protocol to replace blood products (especially fibrinogen), and delivery of the foetus. It carries a high mortality rate.
key_points: AFE triad: acute hypoxia, cardiovascular collapse (often PEA), and DIC with severe hypofibrinogenaemia. (Source: RANZCOG: Amniotic Fluid Embolism)
Management is supportive: CPR, ventilation, massive transfusion (red cells, platelets, FFP, cryoprecipitate/fibrinogen concentrate), and delivery. (Source: UKOSS; RANZCOG Guideline)
Risk factors: advanced maternal age, multiparity, meconium-stained liquor, operative delivery, prolonged labour. (Source: RANZCOG: Amniotic Fluid Embolism)
Survival requires rapid recognition and aggressive management; the maternal mortality rate remains 20-40%. (Source: UKOSS Study)
explanation.pearls: Amniotic fluid embolism is a diagnosis of exclusion. In any pregnant woman with sudden collapse, respiratory distress, and coagulopathy, treat for AFE while excluding other causes. Early and aggressive fibrinogen replacement is crucial.

stem: A 26-year-old G2P1 underwent suction curettage for a suspected molar pregnancy at 10 weeks gestation after an ultrasound showed a 'snowstorm' appearance and no foetal pole. Histopathology confirms a complete hydatidiform mole. She is keen to conceive again as soon as possible. Her hCG level 2 weeks post-evacuation is 2500 IU/L, down from 120,000 IU/L pre-operatively. She has no vaginal bleeding, and pelvic ultrasound shows an empty uterus with normal adnexa. What is the most appropriate advice regarding future pregnancy?

data_table.Vital signs: BP 115/70 mmHg, HR 78 bpm, Temp 36.7°C
data_table.Examination: No vaginal bleeding, uterus well involuted, no adnexal masses
data_table.Investigations: hCG 2500 IU/L (was 120,000); USS: empty uterus, normal adnexa; histology: complete mole

lead_in: What is the most appropriate advice regarding future pregnancy?

options.text: She may attempt pregnancy once hCG becomes undetectable, with no increased risk in subsequent pregnancies and no surveillance needed
options.rationale: No surveillance, conceive once hCG undetectable is wrong because monthly hCG surveillance is needed for at least 6 months after normalisation to detect persistent trophoblastic disease; conception during this window confounds interpretation. Would never be correct after complete mole (RANZCOG: Gestational Trophoblastic Disease).

options.text: Avoid pregnancy for at least 6 months after hCG normalises, use reliable contraception, with monthly hCG surveillance throughout that period
options.rationale: Following evacuation of a complete mole, hCG is monitored fortnightly until negative, then monthly for 6-12 months (Australian protocol: 6 months after first normal level). Pregnancy should be avoided during this surveillance period to prevent confusion with rising hCG from a new pregnancy vs. persistent gestational trophoblastic disease. Effective contraception (COCP, IUD, implant) is recommended. (Source: RANZCOG: Gestational Trophoblastic Disease; KEMH: Molar Pregnancy Guideline)

options.text: She should undergo a hysterectomy because complete moles have a 15-20% risk of progression to choriocarcinoma and future pregnancy is contraindicated
options.rationale: Hysterectomy is wrong because routine hysterectomy is not required for complete mole in a young woman who desires future fertility; surveillance with hCG manages persistent disease risk, and hysterectomy is reserved for older women or invasive mole. Would be correct only in older women with completed family or invasive disease (RANZCOG).

options.text: She requires a single dose of intramuscular methotrexate 50 mg/m² immediately to prevent progression to persistent trophoblastic disease at any stage
options.rationale: Single-dose methotrexate prophylactically is wrong because prophylactic chemotherapy is not routine for complete mole; most patients resolve with evacuation alone. Would be correct only for selected high-risk profiles (very high hCG, age >40, very large uterus) (RANZCOG).

options.text: She should undergo CT chest and abdomen to stage the disease before pregnancy, as complete moles frequently metastasise to the lungs
options.rationale: CT staging routinely is wrong because routine CT is not performed for uncomplicated complete mole with falling hCG; staging is reserved for persistent/rising hCG or clinical suspicion of metastases. Would be correct only with persistent disease or metastatic symptoms (RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: Following evacuation of a complete hydatidiform mole, hCG must be monitored until normalised, then monthly for at least 6 months to detect persistent trophoblastic disease (which occurs in 15-20% of complete moles). Pregnancy should be avoided during the surveillance period because a rising hCG from a new pregnancy would be indistinguishable from malignant transformation. Reliable contraception is advised. Routine chemotherapy, hysterectomy, and staging CT are not required unless hCG plateaus, rises, or metastases are suspected.
key_points: Complete moles require post-evacuation hCG surveillance: fortnightly until negative, then monthly for 6 months. (Source: RANZCOG: Gestational Trophoblastic Disease)
Pregnancy should be avoided for at least 6 months after the first normal hCG; effective contraception is recommended. (Source: KEMH: Molar Pregnancy Guideline)
Persistent trophoblastic disease (rising or plateaued hCG) requires chemotherapy (methotrexate or actinomycin D). (Source: RANZCOG: Gestational Trophoblastic Disease)
Prophylactic chemotherapy is not routinely indicated for low-risk moles. (Source: RANZCOG: Gestational Trophoblastic Disease)
explanation.pearls: hCG monitoring after a molar pregnancy is the single most important element of follow-up. Any missed test may delay detection of choriocarcinoma, which is highly curable if caught early.

stem: A 30-year-old primigravida at 12+3 weeks gestation presents to the emergency department with a 6-hour history of acute onset severe right iliac fossa pain, associated with nausea and vomiting. The pain began suddenly while she was walking and has been constant since. She has no vaginal bleeding. On examination, she is in significant pain, HR 100 bpm, BP 110/70 mmHg. The abdomen is tender in the right lower quadrant with voluntary guarding but no peritonism. A bedside transvaginal ultrasound shows a viable intrauterine pregnancy consistent with dates, a normal left ovary, and an enlarged right ovary measuring 7 cm with multiple peripheral follicles and absent internal blood flow on colour Doppler. The right ovarian vein is not well visualised. The appendix is not visualised. Her inflammatory markers are normal, and urine dipstick is negative.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 100 bpm, BP 110/70 mmHg, RR 18, Temp 36.5°C
data_table.Examination: Tender RIF, voluntary guarding, no peritonism, no cervical motion tenderness, uterus appropriate size
data_table.Investigations: TVUS: viable IUP, normal left ovary, right ovary 7 cm with peripheral follicles, absent internal Doppler flow; WCC 10.2, CRP 4

lead_in: What is the most appropriate definitive management?

options.text: Diagnostic laparoscopy with ovarian detorsion and ovarian cystectomy if a benign cyst is identified, with ovarian conservation
options.rationale: Ovarian torsion in pregnancy is a surgical emergency requiring urgent laparoscopy for detorsion to preserve ovarian function. Even if the ovary appears ischaemic, detorsion and conservation are recommended because ovarian recovery is common. Cystectomy can be performed for a benign-appearing cyst; oophorectomy is only considered if the ovary is frankly necrotic and non-viable. Laparoscopy is safe in the second trimester with appropriate port placement. (Source: RANZCOG: Adnexal Masses in Pregnancy; KEMH: Ovarian Torsion Guideline)

options.text: Oophorectomy via laparotomy, as the absent Doppler flow indicates irreversible ischaemia and the ovary cannot be salvaged
options.rationale: Oophorectomy via laparotomy is wrong because absent Doppler flow does not reliably predict non-viability; many ovaries recover after detorsion even when discoloured. Ovarian conservation is standard of care. Would be correct only with frankly necrotic non-viable ovary (RANZCOG: Adnexal Masses in Pregnancy).

options.text: Conservative management with analgesia, antiemetics, and observation for 24 hours, as the pain may resolve spontaneously
options.rationale: Observation and analgesia is wrong because torsion does not resolve spontaneously and delay results in ovarian necrosis; urgent surgical detorsion is required. Would never be correct (KEMH: Ovarian Torsion Guideline).

options.text: CT abdomen and pelvis with intravenous contrast to confirm the diagnosis before proceeding to surgery
options.rationale: CT with contrast is wrong because CT is relatively contraindicated in pregnancy due to radiation exposure, the diagnosis is clinical/ultrasound-based, and imaging delays surgery. Would be correct only if there were a non-pregnancy diagnostic dilemma and ultrasound failed (RANZCOG).

options.text: Aspiration of the ovarian cyst under ultrasound guidance to reduce size and relieve the torsion without surgery
options.rationale: Cyst aspiration is wrong because aspiration does not detorse the pedicle and may cause rupture, bleeding or infection; surgical detorsion is the only effective treatment. Would never be correct in torsion (KEMH: Ovarian Torsion Guideline).

explanation.summary: Ovarian torsion in pregnancy is a gynaecological emergency requiring urgent laparoscopy with detorsion and ovarian conservation. The presence of a viable pregnancy does not contraindicate laparoscopic surgery in the first or second trimester. The ovary should be conserved regardless of its colour at surgery, as recovery is common. Doppler ultrasound showing absent flow is supportive but does not mandate oophorectomy. Delaying surgery risks ovarian necrosis and potential miscarriage.
key_points: Ovarian torsion in pregnancy: urgent laparoscopy with detorsion and ovarian conservation. (Source: RANZCOG: Adnexal Masses in Pregnancy)
Ovarian salvage is possible even with a dark, ischaemic-appearing ovary; oophorectomy is a last resort. (Source: KEMH: Ovarian Torsion Guideline)
Laparoscopy is safe in pregnancy up to 28 weeks with appropriate port placement (open entry, left upper quadrant if needed). (Source: RANZCOG: Adnexal Masses in Pregnancy)
Ultrasound features: enlarged ovary >5 cm, peripheral follicles, absent Doppler flow, and the 'whirlpool sign' of the twisted pedicle. (Source: KEMH: Ovarian Torsion Guideline)
explanation.pearls: In a pregnant patient with acute pelvic pain, always consider ovarian torsion, especially with an ovarian mass. The ovary is never too black to save – detort and observe.

stem: A 28-year-old primigravida at 22+5 weeks presents with painful regular contractions and cervical dilation 3 cm with bulging membranes. Foetal ultrasound: singleton, cephalic, AFI normal, estimated foetal weight 540 g (50th centile). She has been counselled with the neonatology team. After detailed discussion of likely outcomes at periviable gestation, the family has indicated they wish for active resuscitation if the infant is liveborn.

data_table.Examination: Cervix 3 cm, bulging membranes
data_table.Investigations: USS: singleton, cephalic, AFI normal

lead_in: What is the most appropriate plan regarding antenatal corticosteroids?

options.text: Administer betamethasone 11.4 mg IM, two doses 24 hours apart, since active resuscitation is planned
options.rationale: RANZCOG and Australian/New Zealand neonatal consensus now support antenatal corticosteroids from 22+0 weeks when active neonatal resuscitation is planned, as ACS reduces neonatal mortality and major morbidity. Below 22 weeks ACS is not recommended (RANZCOG ACS; ANZNN periviable consensus).

options.text: Withhold ACS as gestation is below 23 weeks where benefit is uncertain
options.rationale: Withhold ACS at <23 weeks is wrong because updated RANZCOG/ANZNN guidance supports ACS from 22+0 weeks when active resuscitation is planned, given evidence of reduced neonatal mortality; the anti-pattern is using outdated 23+0 cutoff. Would be correct only at <22+0 weeks or when palliative neonatal care is chosen (RANZCOG; ANZNN).

options.text: Administer a single dose of betamethasone 11.4 mg IM only
options.rationale: Single dose is wrong because the complete course is two doses 24 hours apart; partial courses have reduced effect though some benefit if delivery is imminent. Would be correct only if delivery occurred before the second dose could be given (RANZCOG).

options.text: Administer dexamethasone 6 mg IM every 6 hours for four doses
options.rationale: Dexamethasone 6 mg q6h is wrong because the standard regimen is 6 mg IM 12-hourly for four doses; q6h is too frequent and not evidence-based. Would never be correct (RANZCOG).

options.text: Defer the decision until membranes rupture and only give if delivery is within 24 hours
options.rationale: Defer until membranes rupture is wrong because ACS is given when preterm birth is anticipated within 7 days; waiting for ROM loses the lead time required for steroid effect on lung maturation. Would never be correct (RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: Corticosteroids are recommended from 23 weeks gestation for women at risk of preterm delivery within 7 days, using betamethasone 11.4 mg IM, two doses 24 hours apart.
key_points: Antenatal corticosteroids indicated at 23+0 to 34+6 weeks. (Source: RANZCOG: Antenatal Corticosteroid Guideline)
Betamethasone 11.4 mg IM, 2 doses 24 hours apart. (Source: RANZCOG: Antenatal Corticosteroid Guideline)
explanation.pearls: At 23 weeks, every hour gained in utero and every dose of steroids matters.

stem: A 35-year-old woman presents after her third consecutive miscarriage at 9 weeks. She had one previous live birth at term 4 years ago. She has no significant medical history. Which set of investigations is most appropriate?

data_table.Examination: Normal general and pelvic exam
data_table.Investigations: Products of conception: normal female karyotype

lead_in: What is the most appropriate set of investigations?

options.text: Anticardiolipin antibodies, lupus anticoagulant, beta-2 glycoprotein I antibodies, plus pelvic ultrasound for anomalies
options.rationale: RANZCOG guidelines recommend testing for antiphospholipid syndrome (lupus anticoagulant, anticardiolipin, beta-2 glycoprotein I) and a pelvic ultrasound (3D if possible) to evaluate the uterine cavity. Parental karyotyping is not routinely recommended unless there is a family history or specific indication. (Source: RANZCOG: Recurrent Miscarriage)

options.text: Antiphospholipid antibodies, parental karyotypes, inherited thrombophilia screen, and outpatient diagnostic hysteroscopy
options.rationale: Karyotypes plus thrombophilia screen is wrong because parental karyotyping is not routine in Australia (offered only with family history of chromosomal disease) and inherited thrombophilia screening (other than APS) is not recommended because it does not alter management. Would be correct only with relevant family history (RANZCOG: Recurrent Miscarriage).

options.text: Full blood count, thyroid function tests, fasting glucose, and 75-gram oral glucose tolerance test
options.rationale: FBC, TFTs, glucose, OGTT is wrong because these are general health screens, not specific causes of recurrent miscarriage; while TFTs are often included, fasting glucose and OGTT are not standard recurrent-miscarriage workup. Would be correct only as general health check, not specific workup (RANZCOG: Recurrent Miscarriage).

options.text: Serum anti-Müllerian hormone, early follicular FSH, oestradiol level, and ovarian antral follicle count
options.rationale: Ovarian reserve markers is wrong because AMH, FSH, oestradiol and AFC assess reserve and IVF prognosis, not causes of recurrent loss; ovarian reserve does not explain her three losses. Would be correct only for fertility planning, not recurrent miscarriage workup (RANZCOG: Recurrent Miscarriage).

options.text: MTHFR mutation testing, factor V Leiden, prothrombin gene mutation, and fasting homocysteine level
options.rationale: MTHFR and inherited thrombophilias is wrong because MTHFR testing is not recommended (no clinical management implications) and factor V Leiden/PT mutation testing is not routine in Australian recurrent-miscarriage workup. Would be correct only in selected research/case-specific contexts (RANZCOG: Recurrent Miscarriage).

explanation.summary: The recommended investigation for recurrent miscarriage (≥3 consecutive losses) includes antiphospholipid antibody testing (to diagnose APS) and a pelvic ultrasound (to detect uterine anomalies). Parental karyotyping and inherited thrombophilia testing are not routinely recommended.
key_points: Routine investigations for recurrent miscarriage: antiphospholipid antibodies (lupus anticoagulant, anticardiolipin, beta-2 GPI) and pelvic ultrasound. (Source: RANZCOG: Recurrent Miscarriage)
Parental karyotyping is only indicated if there is a family history of chromosomal rearrangements. (Source: RANZCOG: Recurrent Miscarriage)
Inherited thrombophilias are not associated with recurrent miscarriage and should not be tested. (Source: RANZCOG: Recurrent Miscarriage)
explanation.pearls: Antiphospholipid syndrome is the only treatable cause of recurrent miscarriage; low-dose aspirin and heparin can significantly improve live birth rates.

stem: A 16-year-old girl reports 8 months of severe crampy lower abdominal pain with each period, missing school for 1-2 days each cycle. Her cycles are regular at 28 days with menarche at 13. She has tried regular paracetamol 1 g qid with minimal benefit. There is no pain outside of menses, no dyspareunia (she is not sexually active), no abnormal bleeding, no urinary or bowel symptoms and no fever. Examination is normal. Pregnancy test negative. She is otherwise well, has no contraindication to NSAIDs and no medication allergies. Her mother is concerned and asks whether laparoscopy is required to exclude endometriosis before further treatment.

data_table.Examination: Normal abdominal and external genital exam
data_table.Investigations: None

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Commence regular naproxen 500 mg twice daily for 3-5 days starting 1-2 days before menses, with review at 3 months
options.rationale: Primary dysmenorrhoea is diagnosed clinically; first-line is regular NSAIDs taken pre-emptively, not laparoscopy. NSAID failure after a 3-month trial, plus consideration of COCP, would precede invasive workup (RANZCOG; eTG).

options.text: Refer for diagnostic laparoscopy now to exclude endometriosis
options.rationale: Diagnostic laparoscopy now is wrong because primary dysmenorrhoea is a clinical diagnosis and laparoscopy is reserved for refractory pain after NSAID and COCP trials, or features suggestive of secondary dysmenorrhoea. Would be correct only after failed medical management or red flags for endometriosis (RANZCOG; ESHRE).

options.text: Continue paracetamol 1 g four times daily and add codeine 30 mg qid as needed
options.rationale: Paracetamol plus codeine is wrong because opioids are not first-line in adolescent dysmenorrhoea (dependence risk, no disease-modifying effect) and NSAIDs have stronger evidence. Would never be correct as first-line (eTG).

options.text: Commence GnRH agonist (goserelin) to induce amenorrhoea
options.rationale: GnRH agonist is wrong because GnRH agonists in adolescents risk bone density loss and are not used without confirmed endometriosis with add-back HRT planning; NSAIDs are first-line. Would be correct only in confirmed refractory endometriosis (RANZCOG).

options.text: Reassure and recommend transcutaneous electrical nerve stimulation as first-line
options.rationale: TENS as first-line is wrong because TENS is a useful adjunct with modest evidence but not first-line pharmacotherapy; NSAIDs remain first-line. Would be correct only as adjunct (RANZCOG; Cochrane).

explanation.summary: Primary dysmenorrhoea is caused by prostaglandin release from the endometrium. NSAIDs like ibuprofen are first-line because they inhibit prostaglandin synthesis. Starting before menses improves efficacy. COCP is used if NSAIDs fail.
key_points: First-line: NSAIDs (ibuprofen 400-600 mg TDS) started 1-2 days before menses. (Source: RANZCOG: Dysmenorrhoea)
COCP is an effective second-line therapy. (Source: RANZCOG: Dysmenorrhoea)
explanation.pearls: Timing matters with NSAIDs for dysmenorrhoea: start before the pain begins.

stem: A 30-year-old primigravida at 32 weeks gestation reports severe pain over the pubic symphysis that radiates to the inner thighs, worse with walking, climbing stairs, and turning in bed. Examination reveals tenderness over the symphysis pubis and positive FABER test. What is the most appropriate first-line management?

data_table.Examination: Tender symphysis pubis, positive FABER, no neurological deficits
data_table.Investigations: USS: foetus well, cephalic

lead_in: What is the most appropriate first-line management?

options.text: Refer to a women's health physiotherapist for pelvic girdle exercises, support belt fitting, and analgesia
options.rationale: Symphysis pubis dysfunction (pelvic girdle pain) is managed conservatively with physiotherapy focusing on pelvic stabilisation exercises, a pelvic support belt, and simple analgesia. Avoiding aggravating movements (abduction, asymmetric loading) is key. (Source: KEMH: Pelvic Girdle Pain in Pregnancy)

options.text: Induction of labour at 37 weeks gestation to minimise further musculoskeletal damage and ongoing maternal discomfort
options.rationale: Induction at 37 weeks for SPD is wrong because delivery does not improve outcomes and induces iatrogenic prematurity morbidity; SPD typically resolves postnatally without delivery escalation. Would be correct only for standard obstetric indications, not SPD itself (KEMH: Pelvic Girdle Pain).

options.text: Strict bed rest for the remainder of the pregnancy with bedside toileting and meals provided
options.rationale: Strict bedrest is wrong because bedrest leads to deconditioning, VTE risk and does not improve pain; the anti-pattern is treating SPD as a structural injury requiring immobilisation. Would never be correct (KEMH: Pelvic Girdle Pain).

options.text: Intra-articular corticosteroid injection into the symphysis pubis joint under image guidance in interventional radiology
options.rationale: Intra-articular corticosteroid injection is wrong because injections are reserved for severe refractory cases after conservative measures have failed; not first-line. Would be correct only after physiotherapy and analgesia fail (KEMH: Pelvic Girdle Pain).

options.text: Prescribe regular oral opioid analgesia such as immediate-release oxycodone for severe ongoing pelvic pain
options.rationale: Regular oxycodone is wrong because opioids are avoided in pregnancy (foetal CNS depression, neonatal abstinence) and not first-line for SPD; paracetamol plus non-pharmacological measures are preferred. Would be correct only as short-course rescue for severe pain after conservative measures (KEMH: Pelvic Girdle Pain).

explanation.summary: Pelvic girdle pain in pregnancy is managed with physiotherapy, a pelvic support belt, and simple analgesia. Avoidance of aggravating activities (stair climbing, asymmetric standing) is important. Bed rest and early delivery are not recommended.
key_points: First-line: physiotherapy, pelvic belt, paracetamol. (Source: KEMH: Pelvic Girdle Pain)
Avoid bed rest and opioids. (Source: KEMH: Pelvic Girdle Pain)
explanation.pearls: Symphysis pubis dysfunction does not typically affect mode of delivery; vaginal birth is usually possible with careful positioning.

stem: A 34-year-old G1P0 at 28 weeks with known fundal fibroid (8 cm at 20-week scan) presents with 36 hours of severe localised pain over the uterine fundus, mild nausea, and a low-grade temperature of 37.6. There is no vaginal bleeding, no leaking of fluid, no contractions and no urinary symptoms. Examination: tender over the fundal fibroid, no peritonism, normal bowel sounds, uterus otherwise soft. CTG reactive, foetus active. Ultrasound: 9 cm fibroid with heterogeneous echotexture and central cystic change, no free fluid, normal placental position, normal Dopplers. White cell count mildly elevated 14, CRP 35, urinalysis nil. Beta-hCG appropriate for gestation.

data_table.Examination: Severe tenderness over the uterine fundus, no peritonism, bowel sounds present
data_table.Investigations: USS: 9 cm fibroid with heterogeneous echotexture, no free fluid, foetus well

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Admit for analgesia (paracetamol +/- short-course oral or IV opioids), hydration, monitoring of foetal wellbeing and serial CTG, with avoidance of NSAIDs at this gestation
options.rationale: Red degeneration of a fibroid in pregnancy is treated conservatively: analgesia, rest, hydration and foetal monitoring. NSAIDs (e.g. ibuprofen, indomethacin) are avoided after 30 weeks due to ductal closure risk but more importantly are best avoided across the third trimester; opioids are acceptable short-term. Surgery and tocolysis are not indicated unless complications develop (RANZCOG fibroids; eTG).

options.text: Commence indomethacin 50 mg orally tds for 7 days
options.rationale: Indomethacin 7 days is wrong because indomethacin risks foetal ductus arteriosus constriction and oligohydramnios in the third trimester; while it has anti-inflammatory effect on degeneration, safer analgesia is preferred. Would be correct only before 30 weeks when third-trimester risks do not yet apply (eTG; RANZCOG).

options.text: Proceed to elective myomectomy at this gestation
options.rationale: Antenatal myomectomy is wrong because it carries high risk of haemorrhage and foetal loss; reserved for refractory torted pedunculated fibroids. Would be correct only with torsion of a pedunculated fibroid or refractory necrotic lesion (RANZCOG).

options.text: Diagnose placental abruption and proceed to immediate caesarean section
options.rationale: Caesarean for abruption is wrong because abruption presents with bleeding, woody hypertonic uterus and foetal distress; her CTG is reassuring and pain is localised, not the abruption picture. Would be correct only with confirmed abruption (RANZCOG).

options.text: Commence broad-spectrum IV antibiotics for presumed septic fibroid
options.rationale: Broad-spectrum antibiotics for septic fibroid is wrong because mild inflammatory marker rise is expected in red degeneration; without sepsis or chorioamnionitis features, antibiotics are not indicated. Would be correct only with sepsis or infection (RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: Red degeneration of a fibroid presents with acute abdominal pain, vomiting, and tenderness over the fibroid. Conservative management with analgesia and monitoring is the standard, as it resolves spontaneously.
key_points: Red degeneration: acute pain, vomiting, tenderness over fibroid in second/third trimester. (Source: RANZCOG: Fibroids in Pregnancy)
Management is conservative: analgesia, rest, foetal monitoring. (Source: RANZCOG: Fibroids in Pregnancy)
Surgery is only indicated for complications like torsion of a pedunculated fibroid or infarction. (Source: RANZCOG: Fibroids in Pregnancy)
explanation.pearls: Red degeneration is common and benign – provide strong analgesia and reassure. Avoid unnecessary surgery.

stem: A 30-year-old G3P0 at 14+0 weeks attends a recurrent-pregnancy-loss clinic. She has had two previous second-trimester losses at 20 and 22 weeks, both with painless cervical dilation and rapid delivery of a liveborn infant who died shortly after delivery. No infection, no bleeding, no abruption identified at either loss. There are no other risk factors for preterm birth (no smoking, no LLETZ, no Mullerian anomaly, no DES exposure). Current TVUS cervical length 38 mm with closed internal os. She is otherwise well and asks specifically about the option of a prophylactic suture.

data_table.Examination: Normal pelvic exam, closed cervix
data_table.Investigations: TVS: cervical length 38 mm, closed os

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Offer a history-indicated cervical cerclage at 12-14 weeks now
options.rationale: Two or more previous second-trimester losses consistent with cervical insufficiency are the classical indication for history-indicated (prophylactic) cerclage placed at 12-14 weeks. Serial surveillance alone is less effective for this pattern (RANZCOG cerclage; RCOG Green-top 60).

options.text: Serial TVUS cervical length every 2 weeks, with ultrasound-indicated cerclage only if length falls below 25 mm before 24 weeks
options.rationale: Serial TVUS with ultrasound-indicated cerclage is wrong because that strategy is preferred when there is ONE prior preterm birth/loss; with two or more typical mid-trimester insufficiency losses, history-indicated cerclage at 12-14 weeks is recommended. Would be correct only with one prior preterm birth (RANZCOG cerclage).

options.text: Commence vaginal progesterone 200 mg daily from 16 weeks without cerclage
options.rationale: Vaginal progesterone alone is wrong because progesterone is indicated for asymptomatic short cervix on TVS or one prior preterm birth, but does not replace history-indicated cerclage in classical insufficiency. Would be correct only as adjunct or alternative when cerclage is declined/contraindicated (RANZCOG).

options.text: Bed rest at home from 16 weeks with avoidance of intercourse and exercise
options.rationale: Bed rest is wrong because bedrest has no evidence of benefit and increases VTE risk; it cannot substitute for cerclage. Would never be correct (RANZCOG).

options.text: Await spontaneous cervical change and place emergency rescue cerclage only if dilation occurs
options.rationale: Rescue cerclage only when dilation occurs is wrong because rescue cerclage carries higher complication risk and lower success; planned history-indicated cerclage at 12-14 weeks is superior in classical insufficiency. Would be correct only as salvage when history-indicated cerclage was missed or declined (RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: A history-indicated cerclage at 12-14 weeks is recommended for women with ≥2 second-trimester losses consistent with cervical insufficiency. This prevents recurrence and improves live birth rates.
key_points: History-indicated cerclage: ≥2 prior mid-trimester losses without another cause. (Source: RANZCOG: Cervical Insufficiency)
Prophylactic cerclage is placed at 12-14 weeks. (Source: RANZCOG: Cervical Insufficiency)
explanation.pearls: The best predictor of cervical insufficiency is a history of painless, progressive second-trimester cervical dilation – the current cervical length doesn't matter for the decision to place a history-indicated cerclage.

stem: A 28-year-old G2P1 at 36+2 weeks presents with reduced foetal movements for 12 hours. Bedside ultrasound confirms intrauterine foetal demise. She is haemodynamically stable, no bleeding, no contractions. The senior registrar has confirmed and broken the news, with bereavement midwifery support called. She is keen to take the baby home and is asking whether she could await spontaneous labour rather than be induced now.

data_table.Examination: No foetal heart auscultated, uterus size consistent with dates
data_table.Investigations: USS: no cardiac activity, foetal demise confirmed

lead_in: What is the most appropriate counselling and management plan?

options.text: Counsel that delaying delivery beyond approximately 48 hours after foetal death increases the risk of disseminated intravascular coagulation, and offer induction of labour as the preferred option, while supporting her preferences
options.rationale: Prolonged retention of a dead foetus risks DIC (developing significantly after 4 weeks but rising from 2 days onward). Induction of labour is generally recommended; expectant management beyond a short window requires close haematological monitoring (RANZCOG stillbirth).

options.text: Proceed to category 1 caesarean section as soon as theatre is available
options.rationale: Category 1 caesarean is wrong because caesarean is reserved for maternal indications (e.g. praevia with bleeding, abruption with maternal compromise); after IUFD, vaginal birth is preferred and avoids surgical risk. Would be correct only with maternal indications for caesarean (RANZCOG stillbirth).

options.text: Discharge home and await spontaneous labour at term
options.rationale: Discharge and await spontaneous labour at term is wrong because awaiting term risks DIC from prolonged retention; induction is recommended. Would never be correct as default (RANZCOG).

options.text: Administer betamethasone now and induce labour in 48 hours
options.rationale: Betamethasone and delay 48h is wrong because corticosteroids have no foetal benefit after demise; the delay raises DIC risk without benefit. Would never be correct after IUFD (RANZCOG).

options.text: Repeat ultrasound and CTG in 24 hours to reconfirm the diagnosis before discussing options
options.rationale: Repeat scan in 24h is wrong because diagnosis is already confirmed by ultrasound (typically by two clinicians per protocol); delay prolongs distress and DIC risk. Would never be correct once diagnosis is confirmed (RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: When a stillbirth is diagnosed, the immediate priorities are sensitive communication, offering bereavement care, discussing delivery options (typically induction of labour), and offering investigations to determine cause.
key_points: After stillbirth diagnosis: empathetic communication, offer induction of labour within 24-48 hours, support. (Source: PSANZ/Safer Care Victoria)
Investigations: maternal bloods, placental pathology, foetal autopsy if consent. (Source: PSANZ/Safer Care Victoria)
explanation.pearls: The words used when telling a mother her baby has died will stay with her forever. Use clear language, not euphemisms, and allow silence.

stem: A 30-year-old G3P2 at 38+0 weeks presents with sudden severe abdominal pain and minimal vaginal bleeding. Examination: tense, tender, woody-hard uterus, fundal height larger than expected. HR 128, BP 88/54, RR 24, SpO2 97%, peripherally cool. CTG: foetal bradycardia 70 bpm with absent variability. She has chronic hypertension on labetalol and was a smoker until early pregnancy. Bedside ultrasound: posterior placenta, no praevia, retroplacental haematoma visible. Bloods sent: Hb 78 g/L, platelets 92, INR 1.6, fibrinogen 1.4 g/L.

data_table.Examination: Tense, tender, woody-hard uterus, minimal PV loss
data_table.Investigations: CTG: bradycardia 90 bpm, reduced variability

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Activate the massive transfusion protocol and proceed immediately to category 1 caesarean section
options.rationale: This is placental abruption with maternal shock, foetal bradycardia and early DIC. Immediate category 1 caesarean section with MTP activation is required; both mother and foetus are in extremis (RANZCOG abruption; ANZCOR).

options.text: Commence tocolysis with nifedipine and observe with serial CTG
options.rationale: Tocolysis is wrong because tocolysis is contraindicated in abruption with maternal compromise and foetal distress; immediate delivery is required. Would never be correct in this scenario (RANZCOG).

options.text: Insert two large-bore IV cannulae, give 2 L crystalloid bolus and reassess in 30 minutes
options.rationale: Cannulae and wait 30 min is wrong because resuscitation runs concurrently with delivery preparation; waiting 30 minutes worsens outcomes for mother and foetus. Would never be correct as isolated step (RANZCOG).

options.text: Attempt artificial rupture of membranes to expedite vaginal delivery
options.rationale: ARM to expedite vaginal birth is wrong because vaginal birth in abruption requires stable mother and foetus and advanced labour; with shock and bradycardia, caesarean is mandatory. Would be correct only with stable mother, stable foetus and advanced labour, none of which apply (RANZCOG).

options.text: Transfer to tertiary centre for higher-level obstetric input
options.rationale: Transfer for tertiary input is wrong because she is haemodynamically unstable and the foetus is in extremis; transfer is unsafe and immediate on-site delivery is required. Would be correct only in a stable patient at non-tertiary site (RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: Concealed placental abruption presents with severe constant pain, a tense rigid uterus, and foetal distress with minimal external bleeding. Immediate delivery is indicated.
key_points: Concealed abruption: pain, rigid uterus, minimal vaginal bleeding, foetal distress. (Source: RANZCOG: Antepartum Haemorrhage Guideline)
It is an obstetric emergency requiring immediate caesarean delivery. (Source: RANZCOG: Antepartum Haemorrhage Guideline)
explanation.pearls: In abruption, the amount of vaginal bleeding does not reflect the severity – the worst haemorrhage is concealed.

stem: A 28-year-old nullipara at 41 weeks is in labour with oxytocin augmentation. The CTG shows a baseline of 150 bpm with normal variability but repetitive late decelerations after each contraction. Contractions are 5 in 10 minutes. What is the most appropriate next step?

data_table.Vital signs: Maternal HR 100, BP 110/70
data_table.Examination: Cervix 6 cm, contractions 5:10
data_table.Investigations: CTG: baseline 150, normal variability, late decelerations

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Cease the oxytocin infusion, reposition the mother to left lateral, and consider acute tocolysis with terbutaline 250 mcg SC
options.rationale: Late decelerations indicate uteroplacental insufficiency. The first step is intrauterine resuscitation: cease oxytocin to reduce uterine activity, reposition, and consider tocolysis if contractions are hyperstimulated. (Source: RANZCOG: Intrapartum Foetal Surveillance Guideline)

options.text: Continue oxytocin at the same rate and prepare for immediate vaginal delivery
options.rationale: Continue oxytocin is wrong because continuing oxytocin in late decelerations worsens uteroplacental insufficiency by increasing uterine activity and reducing perfusion; the anti-pattern is maintaining augmentation in the face of foetal distress. Would never be correct in late decelerations (RANZCOG: Intrapartum Foetal Surveillance Guideline).

options.text: Perform an immediate caesarean section without further assessment
options.rationale: Immediate caesarean without resuscitation is wrong because normal variability and baseline mean there is time for intrauterine resuscitation; many tracings recover and avoid unnecessary surgery. Would be correct only if intrauterine resuscitation fails or with prolonged bradycardia (RANZCOG: Intrapartum Foetal Surveillance Guideline).

options.text: Apply a foetal scalp electrode and take a foetal scalp blood sample
options.rationale: Scalp electrode and FBS is wrong because scalp blood sampling is used if CTG remains pathological after resuscitation, not as first step; the immediate response is intrauterine resuscitation. Would be correct only as second step after failed resuscitation (RANZCOG).

options.text: Administer a maternal oxygen mask at 8 L/min and increase the oxytocin to achieve better contractions
options.rationale: Maternal oxygen and increased oxytocin is wrong because maternal oxygen has not been shown to improve foetal outcomes and is not recommended; increasing oxytocin would worsen the situation. Would never be correct (RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: Late decelerations reflect transient foetal hypoxia due to uteroplacental insufficiency. Immediate steps are to reduce uterine activity (cease oxytocin), improve placental perfusion (left lateral), and consider tocolysis. Escalation to delivery depends on response.
key_points: Late decelerations: cease oxytocin, reposition, consider tocolysis. (Source: RANZCOG: Intrapartum Foetal Surveillance Guideline)
If CTG normalises, oxytocin may be cautiously restarted at lower dose. (Source: RANZCOG: Intrapartum Foetal Surveillance Guideline)
explanation.pearls: Late decelerations with normal variability = time for conservative measures; late decelerations with reduced variability = immediate delivery.

stem: A 28-year-old primipara, 6 weeks postpartum, reports persistent low mood, anhedonia, poor sleep when the baby sleeps, and feelings of guilt. Her Edinburgh Postnatal Depression Scale (EPDS) score is 14. She is breastfeeding. She has no suicidal ideation. What is the most appropriate initial management?

data_table.Examination: Normal physical examination
data_table.Investigations: EPDS score 14, TSH normal

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial management?

options.text: Refer for psychological therapy (CBT or interpersonal therapy) and consider sertraline 50 mg daily, with risk-benefit discussion in breastfeeding
options.rationale: Moderate postpartum depression (EPDS 13-19) should be managed with psychological therapy and considered for antidepressant medication. Sertraline is the first-line SSRI in breastfeeding due to minimal excretion into breast milk. (Source: RANZCOG: Perinatal Mental Health; Beyond Blue Guideline)

options.text: Reassurance that the 'baby blues' are normal and will resolve without any intervention or follow-up within a few weeks
options.rationale: Reassurance of baby blues is wrong because baby blues resolve within 2 weeks; symptoms beyond 2 weeks with functional impairment define postpartum depression. Would be correct only within the first 2 weeks postpartum without functional impact (RANZCOG: Perinatal Mental Health).

options.text: Immediate admission to a mother-baby psychiatric unit due to perceived risk of harm to the baby in the home setting
options.rationale: Immediate inpatient mother-baby admission is wrong because inpatient admission is reserved for severe depression, suicidal ideation or postpartum psychosis with safety concerns; this is moderate depression without active risk. Would be correct only with severe risk features (RANZCOG: Perinatal Mental Health).

options.text: Commence fluoxetine 20 mg daily and advise her to stop breastfeeding due to neonatal serotonergic risk concerns
options.rationale: Fluoxetine plus stop breastfeeding is wrong because fluoxetine has a long half-life and higher milk:plasma ratio, making sertraline preferred in breastfeeding; stopping breastfeeding is unnecessary on appropriate SSRI. Would be correct only when sertraline fails or is contraindicated (RANZCOG: Perinatal Mental Health).

options.text: Advise increased exercise and structured social engagement only, with reassessment in routine clinic in four weeks time
options.rationale: Lifestyle measures only is wrong because exercise and social engagement are adjuncts but insufficient for moderate depression (EPDS 13-19); active treatment with therapy and medication is recommended. Would be correct only for mild symptoms or as adjunct to active treatment (RANZCOG: Perinatal Mental Health).

explanation.summary: Postpartum depression with an EPDS score of 14 is moderate. First-line treatment includes psychological therapy and consideration of an antidepressant. Sertraline is the preferred SSRI during breastfeeding. Untreated depression can affect maternal-infant bonding.
key_points: Postpartum depression: treat with CBT/interpersonal therapy ± SSRI (sertraline first-line in breastfeeding). (Source: RANZCOG: Perinatal Mental Health)
Baby blues resolve by day 10; symptoms persisting beyond 2 weeks are depression. (Source: RANZCOG: Perinatal Mental Health)
explanation.pearls: Sertraline is the SSRI of choice in breastfeeding due to minimal infant exposure. The mother's mental health is paramount – untreated depression also harms the infant.

stem: A 24-year-old G1P0 at 8+2 weeks gestation presents with vaginal bleeding and lower abdominal cramps. Speculum: cervical os is open with a small amount of tissue at the os. TVUS: empty uterus, endometrial thickness 8 mm, no retained products clearly visible, no adnexal mass, no free fluid. Beta-hCG 1200 IU/L today (was 8500 IU/L two days ago). She is haemodynamically stable, Hb 122, blood group A Rh-positive, no allergies. She has had no prior surgery and is keen to be home with her partner. The team is choosing between expectant, medical and surgical management.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 85 bpm, BP 110/70 mmHg, Temp 36.8°C
data_table.Examination: Open os, tissue in canal; minimal bleeding now
data_table.Investigations: TVUS: empty uterus, endometrial thickness 5 mm

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Offer expectant, medical (misoprostol) or surgical (suction curettage) management options, and support her choice
options.rationale: This is an incomplete or completing miscarriage in a haemodynamically stable woman. Australian guidance (RANZCOG, eTG) supports shared decision-making between expectant, medical and surgical management with comparable safety; choice depends on patient preference, bleeding and availability.

options.text: Proceed directly to suction curettage in theatre today
options.rationale: Direct surgical curettage is wrong because surgical management is one option among three (expectant, medical with misoprostol, surgical) with comparable safety in stable women; mandating surgery ignores patient preference. Would be correct only when the patient prefers surgical management or with active heavy bleeding (RANZCOG).

options.text: Administer single-dose methotrexate as the pregnancy may be ectopic
options.rationale: Methotrexate for possible ectopic is wrong because TVUS confirms intrauterine pregnancy products and beta-hCG is falling appropriately, making ectopic unlikely. Would never be correct here (RANZCOG).

options.text: Admit for IV oxytocin infusion to expedite expulsion of retained products
options.rationale: IV oxytocin to expedite expulsion is wrong because oxytocin is not standard for first-trimester miscarriage; misoprostol is the medical option of choice for first-trimester pregnancy loss. Would be correct only for second/third-trimester losses with viable uterine response (eTG).

options.text: Discharge home with no follow-up; the falling beta-hCG indicates resolution
options.rationale: Discharge with no follow-up is wrong because follow-up beta-hCG to negative confirms resolution and excludes retained products or molar pregnancy; the anti-pattern is treating falling hCG as complete resolution without confirmation. Would never be correct (RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: Incomplete miscarriage is diagnosed when there is an open cervical os with retained products of conception. Complete miscarriage shows a closed os and an empty uterus. Threatened miscarriage has a closed os and a viable pregnancy.
key_points: Incomplete miscarriage: open os, products of conception visible or retained. (Source: RANZCOG: Early Pregnancy Loss)
Management options include expectant, medical, or surgical evacuation depending on clinical stability. (Source: RANZCOG: Early Pregnancy Loss)
explanation.pearls: The appearance of the cervical os on speculum examination is the key to differentiating complete from incomplete miscarriage.

stem: A 34-year-old G2P1 at 30+2 weeks gestation with known chronic hypertension presents with worsening epigastric pain, nausea, and headache. Her blood pressure is 160/105 mmHg despite oral labetalol 200 mg three times daily. Urinalysis shows 3+ protein. Laboratory results reveal: haemoglobin 105 g/L, platelets 55 ×10⁹/L, aspartate aminotransferase (AST) 280 U/L, alanine aminotransferase (ALT) 240 U/L, lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) 650 U/L, haptoglobin <0.3 g/L, serum creatinine 115 µmol/L, urate 0.52 mmol/L. Peripheral blood film shows schistocytes. CTG shows a baseline of 150 bpm with reduced variability and occasional late decelerations. She has received the first dose of betamethasone 11.4 mg IM 6 hours ago.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 160/105, HR 98 bpm, RR 20, SpO₂ 96%, Temp 36.8°C
data_table.Examination: Epigastric tenderness, hyperreflexia, 3 beats clonus, uterine fundus soft
data_table.Investigations: Plt 55, AST 280, ALT 240, LDH 650, haptoglobin <0.3, Cr 115; CTG: reduced variability, late decelerations; Betamethasone dose 1 given 6h ago
data_table.Medications: Labetalol 200 mg TDS PO, IV hydralazine

lead_in: What is the most appropriate plan regarding delivery?

options.text: Administer the second dose of betamethasone at 24 hours and aim for delivery at 48 hours from the first dose, provided maternal and foetal conditions remain stable
options.rationale: Wait 48 hours for full steroid course is wrong because HELLP with severe features (low platelets, raised AST, raised LDH, haemolysis) is rapidly progressive and delivery should not be delayed beyond 24 hours; the anti-pattern is treating HELLP like mild pre-eclampsia where steroid completion is feasible. Would be correct only in mild pre-eclampsia without HELLP (SOMANZ).

options.text: Proceed to delivery within 24 hours, preferably after administering the second dose of betamethasone at 12 hours from the first if maternal and foetal status permit, but do not delay beyond 24 hours
options.rationale: HELLP syndrome is a progressive condition that can deteriorate rapidly. Delivery is the definitive treatment. While completing the steroid course is ideal, in the presence of worsening features (platelets dropping, rising transaminases, renal impairment, foetal compromise on CTG), delivery should not be postponed beyond 24 hours. A second dose of betamethasone can be given at 12–24 hours if feasible, but not at the expense of maternal safety. (Source: SOMANZ Guidelines; RANZCOG Pre-eclampsia Guideline)

options.text: Give the second dose of betamethasone now (6 hours after first) and deliver immediately after the infusion, as expedited delivery is required regardless of steroid status
options.rationale: Second dose at 6 hours is wrong because steroid doses are 24 hours apart (or minimum 12 hours when needed); giving at 6 hours does not optimise neonatal benefit and the rush is not necessary. Would be correct only with truly imminent delivery within hours (SOMANZ).

options.text: Commence magnesium sulphate infusion for foetal neuroprotection and expectantly manage for 48 hours to complete the steroid course, as long as platelets remain above 50 ×10⁹/L
options.rationale: Expectant 48 hours with MgSO4 is wrong because HELLP with foetal compromise carries high risk of eclampsia, hepatic rupture and abruption; expectancy beyond 24 hours is not recommended even with platelets >50. Would never be correct in HELLP with foetal compromise (SOMANZ).

options.text: Perform immediate caesarean delivery under general anaesthesia without waiting for the second betamethasone dose
options.rationale: Immediate caesarean under GA is wrong because immediate delivery is reserved for acute deterioration, eclampsia or non-reassuring CTG unresponsive to resuscitation; close monitoring with expedited delivery within 12-24 hours is reasonable. Would be correct only with acute decompensation, not now (SOMANZ).

explanation.summary: HELLP syndrome is a life-threatening variant of pre-eclampsia requiring delivery. While completion of the corticosteroid course is desirable for neonatal benefit, maternal safety is paramount. In the presence of laboratory deterioration and foetal CTG abnormalities, delivery should be accomplished within 24 hours, ideally after the second steroid dose if feasible at 12 hours. Magnesium sulphate is indicated for seizure prophylaxis, not solely for neuroprotection.
key_points: HELLP syndrome: deliver within 24 hours; delaying for steroids should not compromise maternal wellbeing. (Source: SOMANZ Guidelines)
Severe features: platelets <100, AST ≥70, LDH >600, haemolysis, renal dysfunction. (Source: RANZCOG Pre-eclampsia Guideline)
Foetal CTG abnormalities necessitate close monitoring and potential urgent delivery. (Source: RANZCOG Intrapartum Foetal Surveillance Guideline)
explanation.pearls: HELLP can deteriorate in hours; repeated laboratory surveillance is critical to timing delivery.

stem: A 26-year-old primigravida at 38 weeks gestation with severe pre-eclampsia is being managed with intravenous magnesium sulphate (4 g loading dose, then 1 g/hour) and oral labetalol 200 mg BD. Four hours after commencing the magnesium infusion, she has a generalised tonic-clonic seizure lasting 3 minutes. The seizure self-terminates. On examination, she is postictal with a GCS of 10. Blood pressure is 150/100 mmHg, respiratory rate 16, patellar reflexes present but reduced. The magnesium level 1 hour ago was 1.8 mmol/L (therapeutic range 1.7–3.5 mmol/L). Urine output has been 30 mL/hr.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 150/100, HR 90 bpm, RR 16, SpO₂ 95%, GCS 10, patellar reflexes reduced
data_table.Examination: Postictal, no focal neurological signs, fundus soft
data_table.Investigations: Mg²⁺ 1.8 mmol/L, urine output 30 mL/hr, CTG reactive

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate next step?

options.text: Administer an additional 2 g intravenous bolus of magnesium sulphate over 5-10 minutes and continue the maintenance infusion at 1 g/hour
options.rationale: A recurrent eclamptic seizure while on magnesium therapy (therapeutic level) indicates a need for an additional bolus of 2 g over 5–10 minutes, with close monitoring for magnesium toxicity. A further bolus of 2 g may be repeated if seizures persist. Intubation and phenytoin are reserved for refractory cases. (Source: SOMANZ Guidelines: Eclampsia; KEMH Eclampsia Guideline)

options.text: Stop the magnesium sulphate infusion immediately, administer intravenous calcium gluconate 1 g for magnesium toxicity, and commence intravenous phenytoin loading dose
options.rationale: Stop MgSO4 and give calcium gluconate plus phenytoin is wrong because the magnesium level is therapeutic not toxic, so calcium gluconate (the antidote) is not indicated; phenytoin is less effective than magnesium in eclampsia. Would be correct only with documented magnesium toxicity (absent reflexes, respiratory depression) (SOMANZ).

options.text: Give intravenous midazolam 5 mg to terminate the seizure and arrange an urgent non-contrast CT head to exclude intracranial haemorrhage
options.rationale: Midazolam plus CT head is wrong because the immediate management of recurrent eclamptic seizure is an additional magnesium bolus, not benzodiazepine; CT head is reserved for focal neurology or atypical features. Would be correct only with focal signs raising suspicion of intracranial haemorrhage (SOMANZ).

options.text: Prepare for immediate caesarean delivery, as recurrent seizures despite magnesium indicate impending foetal demise
options.rationale: Immediate caesarean is wrong because the immediate priority is maternal seizure control with magnesium; delivery (the definitive treatment) is expedited once stabilised. Would be correct only after stabilisation (SOMANZ).

options.text: Administer intravenous labetalol 20 mg to urgently lower blood pressure to below 140/90 mmHg, as hypertension is the trigger for seizure recurrence
options.rationale: Labetalol to lower BP is wrong because while BP control matters, the immediate priority is stopping the seizure with magnesium; the BP target in pre-eclampsia is not <140/90 but rather <160/110 to maintain placental perfusion. Would be correct only after seizure control as adjunct (SOMANZ).

explanation.summary: Recurrent eclamptic seizure on therapeutic magnesium warrants an additional 2 g IV bolus, repeated once if needed. Calcium gluconate is for magnesium toxicity, not for therapeutic levels. Delivery is planned after stabilisation. Blood pressure control is secondary.
key_points: For recurrent eclampsia: additional MgSO₄ 2 g IV bolus, then reassess. (Source: SOMANZ Guideline)
Magnesium toxicity: absent patellar reflexes, respiratory depression, hypotension. (Source: KEMH Eclampsia Guideline)
explanation.pearls: Magnesium therapy for eclampsia is titrated to clinical response, not serum levels. Recurrent seizures = need for more magnesium, not less.

stem: A 31-year-old primigravida at 30+6 weeks with early-onset foetal growth restriction (estimated foetal weight below the 3rd centile) has been managed as an inpatient for the past week. Today's umbilical artery Doppler shows absent end-diastolic flow (AEDF), middle cerebral artery Doppler shows a low pulsatility index (brain-sparing), and ductus venosus Doppler shows a positive a-wave. Amniotic fluid index is 6.5 cm. CTG demonstrates a baseline of 145 bpm, reduced variability (4 bpm), and occasional spontaneous accelerations, but no decelerations. She received a full course of betamethasone (two doses 24 hours apart) 8 days ago.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 118/76 mmHg, HR 78 bpm
data_table.Examination: No proteinuria, fundal height 26 cm
data_table.Investigations: UA Doppler: AEDF; MCA: low PI; DV: positive a-wave; AFI 6.5 cm; CTG: reduced variability, no decelerations; Betamethasone completed 8 days ago

lead_in: What is the most appropriate timing for delivery?

options.text: Administer a rescue course of betamethasone and deliver within 24 hours, given AEDF and reduced CTG variability
options.rationale: Deliver within 24 hours is wrong because with positive DV a-wave and no CTG decelerations, delivery can be planned within 24-48 hours (not strictly within 24); the option is overly aggressive on timing. Would be correct only with abnormal DV a-wave or recurrent decelerations (RANZCOG: FGR Guideline).

options.text: Deliver within 24–48 hours, giving a rescue course of betamethasone as the prior course was 8 days ago, and commence magnesium sulphate for foetal neuroprotection
options.rationale: AEDF with reduced CTG variability but positive DV a-wave and no decelerations indicates delivery within 24–48 hours. A rescue steroid course is indicated because >7 days have passed and delivery is anticipated. Magnesium sulphate is given for neuroprotection (<32 weeks). (Source: RANZCOG: Foetal Growth Restriction Guideline; KEMH FGR Guideline)

options.text: Immediate caesarean delivery within 2 hours, as AEDF with reduced variability indicates acute foetal decompensation
options.rationale: Immediate caesarean within 2 hours is wrong because immediate delivery is reserved for reversed EDF, abnormal DV (absent/reversed a-wave) or recurrent CTG decelerations; positive DV a-wave allows the 24-48h window. Would be correct only with abnormal DV a-wave or recurrent decelerations (RANZCOG: FGR Guideline).

options.text: Continue expectant management with daily CTG and repeat Dopplers in 72 hours, as a positive DV a-wave is reassuring
options.rationale: Continue expectancy with repeat Dopplers in 72h is wrong because AEDF plus reduced variability is itself an indication for delivery; positive DV a-wave allows time but does not justify continued expectancy beyond 48 hours. Would be correct only at earlier surveillance phases before AEDF develops (RANZCOG: FGR Guideline).

options.text: Deliver at 34 weeks once the rescue steroid course is effective, as AEDF alone is not an indication for preterm delivery
options.rationale: Deliver at 34 weeks is wrong because AEDF at 30 weeks mandates delivery within 24-48 hours; waiting to 34 weeks risks foetal death from progressive decompensation. Would be correct only with isolated mild umbilical artery PI elevation, not AEDF (RANZCOG: FGR Guideline).

explanation.summary: In FGR with AEDF, brain-sparing, reduced CTG variability but positive DV a-wave and no decelerations, delivery is indicated within 24–48 hours. A rescue course of betamethasone is given if >7 days since the last course. Magnesium sulphate is indicated for neuroprotection at <32 weeks.
key_points: AEDF + reduced CTG variability + positive DV a-wave = deliver in 24–48 h. (Source: RANZCOG: FGR Guideline)
Rescue steroids if >7 days since last course and delivery expected within 7 days. (Source: RANZCOG: Antenatal Corticosteroid Guideline)
Magnesium sulphate for neuroprotection if <32 weeks and delivery imminent. (Source: KEMH Guideline)
explanation.pearls: The ductus venosus a-wave is the last Doppler to become abnormal; a positive a-wave allows a brief window to give steroids, but close monitoring is mandatory.

stem: A 34-year-old G2P1 at 39+2 weeks gestation is in spontaneous labour with a prolonged second stage and meconium-stained liquor. The membranes ruptured 5 hours ago. She suddenly becomes agitated, complains of difficulty breathing, and then collapses with loss of consciousness. The cardiac monitor shows pulseless electrical activity. Cardiopulmonary resuscitation is commenced immediately, and an emergency caesarean section is performed in the labour ward 6 minutes after the arrest. A live infant is delivered with Apgars of 3, 5, and 7.
